Types of environment

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #eng-prod-sig.

Environments are discrete, logical locations where we run software. There are three broad classes of environment in use at Ocado:

Development (DEV)

Systems Integration Testing (SIT)

Production (PRD)

Production (PRD)

Within these broad classes there are several special cases, such as the 'RTE' type of production environment.

The way we use environments, and the commitments we make about them, vary based on their classification.

DTAP classification

DTAP stands for Development, Test, Acceptance and Production. The term is taken from a standard approach to software deployment. Though our environments do not map precisely onto the DTAP model, we use the term DTAP to disambiguate the kind of environment we're referring to. The OTP DTAP types are
OTP DTAP types are defined below.
Development environments (DEV)

Used to test functionality against mocks, either manually or automatically. Development environments are typically used to test individual components, or small groups of components in isolation. These environments are shared by teams who are working on the same overall product family - for example
- for example OSP, Ocean or OSP Now!

Standard DEV environments - typically, standard DEV environments exist at the scope of an entire product family and are shared very widely across teams. Access to DEV environments is the most permissive of the environment types.

** GitLab CI **

Used to run unit tests

Used to run contract tests

Used to run component tests

Used to run system tests

Used to run system load tests

** Platform DEV **

Platform / tooling / infrastructure teams should not test their changes on the DEV environments that development teams are using, because development teams need these environments to be stable so they can develop features. Instead, such platform teams should create their own independent ‘Platform
‘Platform DEV’ environments for developing and testing platform changes.

Systems Integration Test (SIT) environments

SIT environments are used to bring together an entire product and test the behaviour of the components when they are fully integrated and running together. These environments will map closely to the type of production environments.

Used to run (cross-stream) integration tests

Used to run end-to-end tests

Used to run end-to-end load tests

Principles:

Streams are required to offer at least one logical SIT environment for cross stream testing.

Changes must be tested before deployment to SIT.
Avoid SIT tests not originating at the boundaries of the SIT environment, prefer end-to-end tests from external boundaries verified using monitoring and alerting.

Other teams rely on SIT being as similar to PRD as possible, so keep new code in SIT for the shortest time possible. If delays ensue, back out changes to the PRD version in the meantime, and update mocks in DEV to more accurately represent the actual instances in SIT, so that the issue is now
the issue is now replicated in DEV.
Other teams rely on SIT being available and serving realistic data, so if you know SIT is broken, restore it to a state that mirrors PRD as soon as possible.
Changes should be backwards compatible, so it should not be necessary to wait for other teams to upload their new versions of code to SIT before you deploy your changes to PRD.

Autoscaling rules should be same in SIT environments as in production during office hours. Our services are elastic, so this does not mean we run the same number of instances or provisioned throughput.

Monitoring and alerting rules should be same in SIT environments as in production during office hours. Verifying behaviour using monitoring and alerting allows us to work towards unattended deployments via automatic backout.

?> NOTE: Platform/tooling/infrastructure teams should not test their changes on the SIT environments that development teams are using, because development teams need these environments to be stable so they can develop features. Instead, such platform teams should create their own independent
own independent Platform SIT environments for developing and testing platform changes.

** Stream-specific SIT **

For very large products, there can be benefits in integration testing large subcomponents owned by a stream - in these cases stream specific integration environments can be used for testing that subsystem.

Used to run subsystem (stream) end-to-end integration tests instead of OSP SIT.

Used to run subsystem (stream) end-to-end load tests

?> NOTE: This is an optional environment - streams can elect to have one if they see value in it. Example: Managed Fulfilment SIT (LSC)

Production (PRD) environments

These environments are used directly by retailers and customers, and in general are crucial to how the Technology Business generates revenue. Of the different environment types, they are subject to the highest level of change control.

Production environments are usually aligned with a specific client and scope of usage. For example, a retailer client might have three production environments - one for running software that is 'per retailer', and two environments that are specifically intended for running their two CFCs.

Principles:

Synthetic load can be run against PRD to facilitate monitoring and alerting

PRD services should not talk to any mocks

Any environment that controls real-world CFC hive hardware should be PRD by default because we require higher standards of software version control, monitoring and alerting, access control, deployment policies, and safety protocols when controlling this type of hardware. If you have doubts, contact
doubts, contact the stream management.

A PRD environment should be considered ‘live’ when it is handed over to Solutions, or when commissioning is complete (CFC-scoped systems only).

Standard development environments

** Abundo **

Abundo should be considered a real retailer, Abundo environments are considered Production environments used for operational trialling. It is not a Pre-Prod environment. Changes should be fully tested in SIT before deploying to Abundo. Abundo has real customers across Ocado, and the Abundo team run
the Abundo team run the operation as part of their daily roles - they rely on Abundo being stable and fully functional.

If it’s not possible to fully test something in SIT please address the deficiencies of SIT before deploying to Abundo. Changes should be deployed to Abundo before they are deployed to other retailers. In this respect you could call Abundo a canary environment. See Deployment Order for more details.

Verifying changes are implemented as intended in Abundo is not mandated, as they are already tested in SIT. In particular where services are not usually exercised frequently, a deployment does not need to be held up unless trialling is required. Aim to minimise the time a change spends in Abundo to

spends in Abundo to keep environments consistent. Production Incidents in Abundo will invoke the full Service Desk support procedure. ECom Abundo incidents only need to be handled during office hours. They will be subject to full post-mortem procedures.
** Retailer Test Environment (RTE) **

RTE is a special kind of production environment. RTEs are provided to the clients to support their integration testing, trialing, and learning needs. From the perspective of Ocado Technology, they are considered low-volume production environments. The environments are paid for by the clients,

for by the clients, either explicitly or as part of a bundle deal with other software and hardware services. Retailers use RTEs extensively to prepare for OSP launch. The support arrangements reflect this.

Management principles

Retailers use RTEs for their integration testing and for any other use cases they want to test in a non-production environment, such as testing how to set up a voucher.

It is important to treat data access as seriously as with other types of PRD. An RTE can contain real retailer data (even PII). It’s up to the retailers to decide what data they put in their RTEs.

RTEs must not be used by Ocado Technology for testing.

RTEs should be kept in line with the retailer’s PRD environment, configured in the same way (except for the differences mentioned elsewhere in this list).

RTE and PRD environments should be deployed in quick succession.

If requested by retailers, enable new features in their RTEs before enabling them in their PRD environments.

Resilience, alerting, and scaling
Run applications on a single instance only (thus, a single application zone) in order to minimize running costs. If this starts to cause problems, discuss it with your Head of Engineering.

Configure alerting to tolerate the single app instance being temporarily unavailable and a new instance being brought up to replace it. Alerting after 5 minutes of service unavailability normally suffices for this. Set the alert severity according to the 'Support Principles' below.

RTEs should only be very lightly loaded by the retailer: scale down Dynamo, Kinesis, RDS, and so on accordingly.

Support principles

Retailers escalate issues with the environment to the Service Desk.

Usually, issues and alerts should be categorized as P3 or P4 incidents (support during Ocado office hours only, typically European time zones). However, Solutions can request the Service Desk to upgrade the severity of an incident in an RTE to P1/P2. At this point, the resolver teams will be
teams will be escalated to 24/7 for incident investigation and resolution.

For a period of 2 months prior to the planned retailer launch, the Service Desk classifies all the RTE incidents as they would for production environments. This means RTEs will have 24/7 support, identical to the production environment.

An RTE environment is considered ‘live’ from the moment it is handed over to Solutions.

** Sandbox **

** Sandbox **

Sandbox is an environment used by OSP clients to test their External API and Data Harbor integration points, both during initial OSP set-up, and when new APIs or feeds (or new versions of these) are made available.

The Sandbox environment is shared by all retailers.

It does not persist data, so there is no danger of one retailer seeing another retailer’s data.

It runs 24/7.

** Pre-Production (PRE_PROD) **
The PRE-PROD environment type is currently not supported at Ocado. Considering the balance of the arguments below, we are not considering introducing a pre-prod environment at this time.

Reasons for introducing a pre-prod include: - We could allow changes to sit in SIT for longer, and move the emphasis to keeping them in Pre-prod for the shortest time possible. - Pre-prod should be more stable than SIT because it would change less often.

Reasons against introducing a pre-prod include: - The deployment flow will get more complex. Teams may be tempted to skip a step as a result. - There will be more environments to maintain code and data in - We are not using the environments we have (DEV/SIT) consistently at the moment - we should
moment - we should fix this first - Pre-prod will still not resemble PRD (like SIT doesn’t today) if teams keep changes pending in Pre-prod for any length of time - AWS running costs will increase

If you have any questions related to this section, ask in the #incident-response-sig channel, or speak with one of your line managers.

Raising an incident

If you become aware of a problem that is actively impacting (or is inevitably going to impact) client partners, or other system users, then this is an incident and you MUST immediately raise an incident with Service Desk. You MUST NOT postpone raising the incident, and you MUST NOT fix the problem
NOT fix the problem immediately in the hope of avoiding raising an incident.

Why is it important to raise an incident?

There are many reasons why it is important to proactively raise an incident:

The Service Assurance team can proactively inform partners of an issue, if appropriate.

If the problem is reported through other channels then Service Desk will raise an incident, and will then spend time trying to identify which team to contact, only to find that the team were already aware of the issue.
Partners lose confidence in us every time they report an issue to Service Desk, and are told that we hadn't detected the problem ourselves.

Security principles

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #eng-prod-sig.

As a provider of technology services, it is important that Ocado ensures that all such services are sufficiently secure before making them available to clients. Our policy is that security is built into our approach to software development at all stages, starting early in the development lifecycle,
lifecycle, and that secure development practices are well supported by our tools. We apply the following principles to all production environments:

no backdoors.

access to production must meet the following requirements:

disabled by default

provided on a temporary basis

controlled based on the principle of least privilege & limited to the required systems

include a second factor of authentication

generate audit events

all changes must go through the relevant process and tooling, and be recorded.

Security incidents

In any situation where you find evidence of a security related incident of any kind - unauthorised access, a potential breach, DDOS, intrusion, it is critically important that you contact the Service Desk immediately so they can triage and manage the situation through the incident management
incident management process. The Service Desk are able to call on specialists to assist with incidents where needed, including the SecOps team.
Service Organization Control (SOC)

In order to provide independent assurance to clients that the OSP product family is developed and managed in a secure way, Ocado has committed to producing annual SOC2 reports which are verified by an independent auditor. Our goal is to produce a SOC2 type 1 report covering only security -

only security - effectively the 'MVP' of SOC2. Once we have completed this aim we will review the next steps and whether we will extend the report either to type 2, or with a wider scope. This report will be made available to our clients.

Ocado have also commited to producing a SOC1 report in the future.

SOC 1

SOC 1

SOC1 is an American auditing standard and the description of a specific report, the report looks at the controls which are relevant to a clients financial reporting. It is restricted to controls relevant to an audit of a clients financial statements.

SOC 2

SOC2 is an American auditing standard and the description of a specific report, the report looks at the controls we have in place around security, and optionally around processing integrity, availability, confidentiality and privacy. The requirements of SOC2 controls are defined by a of Trust
by a of Trust Service Criteria. In summary SOC2 is a report, the Trust Service Criteria are the requirements.

The Trust Service Criteria cover a broad range of activities at Ocado, including not just the core software development process, but also how we recruit people and ensure they have the right skills, our corporate governance and ethics, how we set and measure goals, and how we manage risk. There are
risk. There are two types of SOC2 report:

type 1: reports look at a point in time and assess whether the controls we define meet the requirements of the Trust Service Criteria, and whether the controls are actually in place; they are essentially "point in time"
type 2: reports look at everything covered in the type 1 report but also consider the effectiveness off the controls over a period of time

Deployment

The following section covers deployment order and rollout strategy.

Deployment order

Deployment order

Deployment order refers to the sequence in which changes should be applied to different environments. In general, changes progress from development and test environments, through a trial environment (if applicable), and finally roll out across production environments.

Deployment order

Production rollout - Wave and Bake

In a system with multiple production environments or a large number of devices to deploy to, changes should be rolled out gradually across the production estate, allowing time between each deployment. To describe best practice in this space, we define two concepts:

Deployment waves - deployments are rolled out to the production environments in stages called waves. Early waves would typically deploy to one production environment (or a small proprotion of devices). Later waves might deploy to several production environments (or increasing numbers of devices) in
of devices) in parallel, as confidence in the deployment grows.

Bake time - a pause between each wave called the bake time gives a chance to gain confidence that the change hasn't broken anything before initiating the next deployment wave. Bake time can be reduced as a change is rolled out more widely and confidence in it grows.

!> Ocado Smart Platform has many production environments. Any deployment carries risk and can cause an incident for a client. Deploying a change to multiple production environments too quickly can cause a multi-retailer or multi-CFC incident. Multi-retailer and multi-CFC incidents are bad for our
are bad for our clients, bad for our service levels, bad for our reputation, and bad for our stress levels - we really want to avoid them! They can be avoided by rolling changes out progressively.

When choosing wave sizes and bake times, teams should consider factors such as the criticality of their system, and the period required to become confident a deployment is working as expected. The diagrams below show how waves and bake times might typically be applied for per-retailer, per-CFC, and

per-CFC, and developer tooling rollouts. If you are unsure of the approach to take with a particular system, look for guidance from within your area - E5s and stream architects are a good starting point for guidance.

It may be possible, or even desirable, to deploy to multiple production environments in early waves, e.g., to ensure we are testing on different versions of hardware early on in the process. This should be to a limited number of devices in each environment. Extra safety can be achieved in the
be achieved in the deployment process by also implementing a similar feature enablement rollout pattern to decouple the rollout of a new version of software from new functionality becoming active.

The exact proportions of devices we deploy to and the lengths of the bake times may be dictated by tooling provided by third parties, e.g., mobile app stores. Where there are no such constraints, we should tune these values to optimise for the right balance of rollout speed and minimising impact on

Platform rollout

Teams are free to choose which retailer(s) to deploy to in each wave, but should consider the following factors:

Current order volumes - prefer to deploy to smaller retailers first.

Number of sites being controlled (in the case of multi-site apps).

Features used and timezones - prefer to deploy sooner to retailers who will use the deployment during Ocado Technology office hours.
Service levels - if a retailer has had their service levels impacted recently, consider moving them to a later wave, at least temporarily.

Ensure changes are backwards compatible wherever possible, since other teams may deploy/backout in a different order. Bake time may be skipped for emergency changes.

Deployment and rollout FAQ

?> Why not deploy to Abundo and another retailer at the same time?

Changes introduced to the Abundo environment need to be verified and authorised by the Abundo team first. Only then the changes can be applied to other retailers.

?> What if Abundo is not going to trial my change, perhaps because it is so small?

For consistency, always deploy to Abundo first.

?> Why do we still need to roll out a change progressively when it has been tested on Abundo?

Abundo alone cannot give us sufficient confidence that a change is good because:

Abundo has very low order volumes

The Abundo team do not routinely use all functions of OSP

Abundo does not pick every day

Abundo sometimes does not operate at all for weeks at a time
?> Why do we deploy to the Retailer Test Environments (RTE) at the same time as their PRD environments?

The RTE environments are for the retailer to do their integration testing. They are not test environments for Ocado Technology. All Ocado Technology OSP feature testing should be performed in SIT, and trialled in Abundo. The Retailer Test Environments are effectively just a second PRD instance of
PRD instance of the environment for the retailer to use.

?> Should I deploy an app to an environment where it won't be used by a retailer or a CFC?

Some OSP partners don't use all the OSP features. This may mean not all applications need to run in all environments. If you don't deploy an app:

it will reduce cloud costs but
it can also increase the risk of a production incident if another app tries to call it.

Deploying different apps to different environments also increases the complexity of deployment pipelines. The approach to this issue differs depending on an OSP stream. To understand the approach in your stream, consult your local OSP architect.

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #maturity-model-and-sdlc-sig.

This page uses RFC 2119 MUST/SHOULD/MAY style terminology.

The SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle) is the process for planning, creating, testing, deploying, and operating a system. The Ocado Technology SDLC includes the following phases:

Plan - prioritise the right work

Discover - clarify the problem and find the simplest solution

Code - write the code and tests

Build - create a deployable artifact

Test - confirm that the changes meet functional and non-functional requirements

Deploy - update code or config in an environment

Trial - get feedback from internal users

Operate - support your software in production environments

Feedback - hear what your users have to say about your product

Each phase contains SDLC activities that teams MUST implement, unless there is a very specific reason a particular activity is not relevant. The Ocado Technology SDLC aligns with our Maturity Model. Follow the implementation recommendations in the SDLC to maximise your maturity model scores. The
model scores. The following section provides descriptions of each SDLC phase, and the activities within it.

Plan

During the Planning phase you prioritise the team backlog, and refined it to remove obsolete entries.

P0 - Maintain a comprehensive, prioritized, and refined team backlog

TL;DR: Every item that your team is working on, or is considering working on in the future, MUST have a Jira or GitLab issue. This includes feature work, bug fixes, tech debt improvements, performance improvements, and security work. You MUST prioritize the team backlog regularly. The product

The product manager MUST let the team know which tasks they should work on. You SHOULD regularly refine the team backlog to remove items that are no longer relevant or that have little prospect of being implemented.
Justification

Maintaining a comprehensive team backlog ensures the team has good visibility and understanding of work requests, allowing them to prioritize work effectively. Each commit needs to reference a ticket ID to record the lineage of the commit. See the SDLC activity C8 - Provide lineage for every
lineage for every Production change for more details.
Regularly prioritizing the team backlog ensures that the team is always working on the most important tasks. Backlog prioritisation is a key component of agile product development.

There is little value in keeping a backlog full of items that are not going to be implemented. A large backlog is difficult to manage and time-consuming to prioritize.

Implementation

The backlog MUST include:

all requests from within the team, and from external stakeholders

all functional requirements (features)

non-functional requirements - for example: performance improvements, security improvements, tech debt tasks, and user experience improvements

Depending on the team's agreed ways of working, smaller support tasks may not be represented on the backlog.

The backlog MUST be stored in one of the approved backlog management tools. Amongst other things, this ensures each backlog item has a unique ID that can be referenced during the rest of the SDLC. Unless your project is confidential or restricted, your backlog items SHOULD be open to other teams in
to other teams in Ocado Technology and Solutions to read, and comment on. See the source control policy for a description of Confidential and Restricted.

The product manager MUST own the prioritization process, unless there is no product manager role. The backlog MUST be prioritized regularly. The product manager MUST involve the team in the prioritization process, particularly for more technical tasks. The product manager MUST identify the tasks

identify the tasks that should be worked on next, for example by moving them to a new column on a GitLab or Jira board. Regular prioritization SHOULD be built into the team's way of working rather than being dependent on an individual. This allows prioritization to continue, even if there is no
even if there is no product manager role for a period of time.

The product manager MUST own any backlog refining processes unless there is no product manager role. As a refiner, you SHOULD be aggressive when refining the backlog. If you remove an item and nobody notices, it probably wasn't that important in the first place. If somebody does ask about a removed

ask about a removed item later, you can always add it to the backlog again. You SHOULD archive backlog items rather than permanently delete them. This ensures the details from the backlog item are available at a later date, if required.

Regular backlog refinement SHOULD be built into the team's way of working rather than being dependent on an individual. This allows backlog refinement to continue even if there is no product manager role for a period of time.

Evidencing

Evidencing

You MUST show the team backlog, including functional and non-functional requirement backlog items.

You MUST describe the prioritization process. You MUST show evidence that prioritization takes place. You MUST describe the process for identifying the task(s) that should be worked on next. You MUST show evidence that you follow this process.

You SHOULD describe the backlog refining process. You SHOULD show evidence that backlog refining takes place.

Discover

Discover

During the discovery phase explore and clarify the problem to be solved, and design a high level technical solution. Work with product managers and engineers across teams, and look for the simplest solution.

Never skip the discovery phase. Reducing the scope of the problem is the quickest way to deliver a new feature. Making changes to the problem, scope, or high level design once coding has begun is always much more expensive and time-consuming than doing the same during Discovery.

DI1 - Privacy By Design

TL;DR: For every new capability, the team MUST determine whether new personal data will be processed, or existing personal data will be used for a new use case. If the capability meets these conditions, follow the Privacy by Design process.

Justification

Justification

GDPR, and similar overseas data protection regulations, require us to employ Privacy by Design. This means our SDLC MUST include processes to ensure our systems respect data privacy.

Implementation

Implementation

Robust data privacy processes are an integral part of the Privacy by design approach. To achieve this, you MUST have a team process that ensures you consider data privacy for every new capability. This COULD be implemented in a number of ways:

Using checklists that are applied for all changes

Required fields as part of a Jira workflow

Recurring meetings, such as backlog refinement, where this need is considered for all changes

A mandatory stage in a higher-level roadmap workflow, where the data handling has been considered for multiple changes

Privacy by Design related documents MUST be retained for the lifetime of the data.

?> There is a potential challenge when it comes to systems that do not process personal data today. Unless the team have a process in place to detect if/when personal data is introduced in the future, how can they be sure the introduction of such data would trigger the Privacy by Design process? If
Design process? If there is any possibility of a system handling personal data in future (no matter how remote) the team MUST incorporate this SDLC activity.

Evidencing

You MUST show:

the team process or mechanism you use to ensure data privacy is considered for every new capability

the criteria you use to determine whether the Privacy by Design process needs to be invoked for each capability
For a capability chosen at random, you MUST show a record of whether any new personal data was introduced, or existing data was used for a new purpose.

If the team's SDLC does not implement Privacy by design, you MUST provide a strong statement justifying why there is no possibility of the team's systems processing personal data today, or in the future.

DI2 - Document high level solution designs and key decisions

TL;DR: The team MUST document the key decisions taken during discovery, and the high level solution design. The documentation SHOULD capture the reasoning behind key decisions.

Justification

Justification

The SDLC Discovery phase covers exploring the problem, and also the high level technical solution. Decisions are made during this process. Documenting the key decisions, and the reasoning behind them, ensures:

there is a common understanding of the decision reached

if the decision is challenged in the future, it can be justified

old discussions are not inadvertently repeated

there is confidence that other options were also considered

anyone new to the domain can bring themselves up to speed with what has already been agreed, and why

Documenting the high level solution design ensures:

there is a common understanding of the high level solution design

anyone new to the domain can use the solution design as an introduction to the solution architecture

these benefits are multiplied when multiple teams are involved in discovery; the documents will often be referred to during later phases of the SDLC

Implementation

Teams MUST have a process to ensure:

High level design and key decisions are documented for every feature and architectural change

The documents are referenced by the associated backlog item

This requirement COULD be met by integrating the SDLC Checklists into your GitLab or Jira projects.

Documents SHOULD be in a consistent format, and written to a level of detail such that they are meaningful to everyone involved in implementing the feature. Teams SHOULD consider using Architectural Decision Records (ADRs), and a standard ADR template.

These documents MUST be retained for the life of the feature, i.e. until the feature is decommissioned.

Evidencing

For a feature sampled at random, you MUST show document(s) containing:

the key decisions made

the high level solution design

Code

Code

The coding phase of the SDLC is the process of moving from requirements to having a software deliverable.

C1 - Perform threat modelling

TL;DR: You MUST create a threat model whenever you create a new system. Every time you add a new feature, you MUST consider whether the threat model needs to be updated. You MUST review any threat model that hasn't been updated for twelve months.

Justification

Justification

Threat modelling is a process by which potential security threats can be identified, enumerated, and mitigations can be prioritised. Without a threat model, a team cannot be confident they are prioritising the most important security controls.

Implementation

Visit the Threat Modelling page for full information on how to create and maintain a threat model. Threat modelling should be timeboxed. Create a threat model whenever you create a new system, or make an architectural change. You should use the threat model to guide the technical design of your

design of your system, ensuring the final design gives an appropriate level of protection against each risk identified in the model. Ask on #application-security if you would like AppSec assistance with threat modelling.

You MUST create a threat model whenever you create a new system, or make an architectural change. Threat modelling SHOULD be timeboxed. You MUST use the threat model to guide the technical design of your system, ensuring the final design gives an appropriate level of protection against each risk
against each risk identified in the model.

All threat models MUST be kept up to date as the system evolves. When designing any new feature, the team MUST agree whether it might affect the system's threat model. If the threat model might be affected, the team MUST arrange a threat modelling session to review the model. The SDLC checklists
The SDLC checklists COULD be used to ensure:

the threat model is considered for every new feature and architectural change.

any updates to the threat model are captured on the associated Jira or GitLab ticket.

The Eng Prod SDLC checklists include a reference implementation for this approach. If a system's threat model hasn't been updated for twelve months the team MUST schedule a quick review to ensure the model takes into account any environmental changes that might lead to new attack vectors.

Threat models MUST be maintained for the lifetime of the system being modelled, i.e. until the system is decommissioned.

Evidencing

For any system sampled at random, you MUST show:

the documented threat model

the last review of the threat model (each model must be reviewed at least once every 12 months)

the SDLC checklist or process used to ensure that threat model updates are considered for every new feature or architectural change

references to relevant Jira or GitLab feature tickets regarding threat model updates

C2 - Privacy by Design (implementation)

TL;DR: Your team MUST team implement Privacy by Design. All personal data MUST be transported over an encrypted channel. Direct Personal Data MUST only ever be persisted in encrypted form, using one of the Ocado Technology approved encryption standards. Access to personal data MUST adhere to the
MUST adhere to the principle of least privilege.

Justification

EU GDPR legislation, and similar overseas data protection regulations, require Ocado Technology to protect personal data. Failure to do so can result in extremely heavy financial penalties.

Implementation

Implementation

During the discovery phase teams MUST identify personal data changes though Privacy By Design. During the coding phase you MUST protect personal data by ensuring:

All personal data is always transported over an encrypted channel.

Direct Personal Data is only ever be persisted in encrypted form, using one of the Ocado Technology approved encryption standards.

Personal data is only used for use cases identified and approved during the Discovery phase - Privacy By Design SDLC activity.

Personal data is only accessible according to the principle of least privilege.

Robust data privacy processes are an integral part of the Privacy by Design approach. Teams MUST have an SDLC process to ensure the above conditions are met for every new capability.

Evidencing

Evidencing

For any feature sampled at random, you MUST demonstrate how the team applied Privacy by Design principles to ensure the above conditions were met.

C3 - Agree and document technical design decisions

TL;DR: Teams MUST have a documented architectural vision for each application. Teams MUST align technical design decisions with this vision. High level technical design decisions MUST be agreed as a team, involving other teams as necessary. These decisions MUST be documented.

Justification

Suboptimal technical design decisions can lead to software that is difficult to support, hard to extend, insecure, and not scalable. When making design decisions it helps to have a common understanding of the architectural principles used to guide those decisions - referred to here as an
to here as an "architectural vision".

Agreeing design decisions as a team helps reduce the risk of a poor design choice. Documenting design decisions, and their justification, allows others (including future team members) to understand the background to major design choices.

Implementation

An architectural vision document MUST be created for each application, or group of applications. If the system is being actively refactored over time to achieve the desired architectural vision, then the vision is describing a desired future state. If the system is already in the desired
in the desired architectural state, then the document is effectively just an architectural overview. The architectural vision document MIGHT lay out:

the high level responsibilities of each major component

principles relating to how and where state should be stored

concepts applied to ensure the application will scale

architectural patterns applied to ensure resilience

the security model for the application
observability goals for the application

?> NOTE: When you create the architectural vision, it may be helpful to consider what information would be useful to a new starter on the team, or what information would be useful if ownership of the system was transferred to another team. What information would help these people make the same
make the same technical design choices that you would make when they're adding features, or making other changes to the code?

All architecturally significant decisions SHOULD be documented in a consistent format, and SHOULD be easy for other team members to find in the future. Teams SHOULD use a lightweight, developer friendly mechanism such as Architecture Decision Records for this purpose.

Evidencing

Evidencing

You MUST be able to explain how technical design decisions are made in the team, and show a record of historical decisions.

C4 - Decide how changes will be tested

TL;DR: The team MUST discuss, and decide at a high level, how a change will be tested before starting work on coding a change.

Terminology

Test process - a set of activities the team follows to test changes. It has to be agreed by the team, and it MAY be documented, but it doesn’t have to be.

Test plan - documents how a change is tested: what is created (e.g. data, tests, a script for manual tests), how it is run and who participates. It MAY be agreed by the team that a Test Management Tool has to be used, documents generated or the successful execution of a pipeline with automated
with automated tests is a valid test plan.

Risk-based approach - Risk (the likelihood of problems and impact of them) is taken into account when deciding how a change will be tested. In a new feature, testing the happy path would be the first thing to test before going to edge cases. In the case of a bug fix, considering regression bugs

regression bugs would be more interesting. Teams would invest more energy testing a change in a part of the system that decides how much to charge a customer than a part that displays a product description. No formal method for risk-based approach is required.
Justification

It's important to decide early how a change will be tested, because testing plans can affect the design, and phasing of development. The discussions will also give the team a feel for how much engineering time will be required to ensure the change is well tested. The team will check with the
will check with the product team that the solution and tests satisfy the needs.

Implementation

In the testing guidelines there is a description of types of tests to consider, the test pyramid, stages and test principles. In all cases teams MUST remember to:

implement tests based on level of risk

where would you most expect issues?

in which areas would a defect cause the biggest impact?

prefer automated tests over manual tests

If manual tests are required, a test management tool MAY help with planning, tracking and logging the execution of these tests. The Ocado Tech Jira instance includes the Xray Test Management plugin as an option - this is not the only test management solution in use at Ocado Technology, but is
Technology, but is available for all users of the instance.

Evidencing

For any change sampled at random, you MUST be able to provide the test plan.

C6 - Write the code and automated tests

TL;DR: Writing the code is the one step of the SDLC nobody will ever forget to do 😉. You MUST write automated tests as you write your code. Follow the software Hallmarks when implementing new applications, or adding new functionality to existing applications.

Justification

Your code and associated automated tests are part of the same deliverable. Do not prioritise them separately, or you risk shipping code without its tests. The Hallmarks help ensure our applications are secure, resilient, reliable, supportable, usable, documented, and consistent. Test-related
Test-related metrics and heuristics are suggested to facilitate the optimization of the quality of tests (defects detection effectiveness) and improving the testability of code.

Implementation

You MUST follow the software Hallmarks when developing new applications. You MUST apply relevant Hallmarks when adding functionality to an existing application that was not originally developed in line with the Hallmarks.

Refer to C4 - Decide how changes will be tested section for guidance on what types of automated tests should be written. The team SHOULD perform monitoring of chosen metrics or heuristics that depict the quality of tests and the testability of code. The following ones could be taken into account:

% code coverage (preferably branch coverage)

requirements coverage

mutation testing

% of time all tests pass

flakiness - % of time an individual test fails as false positive

The team SHOULD establish guidelines based on metrics and heuristics that trigger corrective measures. The appearance of a blinking test SHOULD indicate presence of indeterminacy that must be eliminated. A surviving mutant or reduction in code coverage MAY be an indicator that additional tests

additional tests should be added. The team SHOULD only add tests where they are valuable. NEVER add tests just to satisfy a metric. If you use a merge request workflow (recommended) you SHOULD review the change against the selected metrics and heuristics before determining whether it is ready to
it is ready to merge. A change that negatively affect the quality or reliability of tests SHOULD NOT be merged.

Evidencing

Show that relevant Hallmarks are followed when developing a new application, or adding new functionality to an existing application.

As per C8 - Provide lineage for every Production change, it MUST be possible to find all code changes, including automated tests, relating to a given backlog item. Source code and associated automated tests MUST be retained for the lifetime of the system, i.e. until it is decommissioned.
C7 - Secure your source code repository

TL;DR: You MUST configure your source code repository such that only authorised project contributors can commit directly to production-bound branches. All other personnel will need to create a merge request that needs to be merged into a production-bound branch by an authorised project contributor.

Terminology

In the context of this SDLC activity we define a production-bound branch, as being a branch that could be used to create a Production release artifact.

Justification

Justification

If unauthorised personnel are allowed to commit changes directly to production-bound branches, it very much increases the likelihood that an unauthorised or unintended change will be deployed to Production.

Implementation

Ocado Technology has a source code control policy that promotes sharing of code unless the code contains high value intellectual property. However, commit access to production-bound branches MUST be limited to authorised project contributors, applying a 'least privilege' policy. This will normally
This will normally mean limiting commit access to the members of the team that own the application.

In this model, other contributors can continue to contribute merge requests, but they will need to create them on branches that are not production-bound. Alternatively they can fork the repository, and create a merge request from the fork. Teams MUST review their source code access control
code access control regularly - teams SHOULD review this every 3 months. GitLab groups MAY be used to simplify source code access control.
It's recommended to set GitLab ACLs at the GitLab group level, so they are inherited by all repositories in the group. GitLab ACLs can be managed using one of the following techniques:

Via AD/Yogi security groups - these are synced to GitLab and appear as GitLab groups with the corresponding members

Using gitlabform to programmatically set the permissions on projects and groups

Using the GitLab Terraform provider

Manual quarterly scheduled audits of who has access

Evidencing

Evidencing

You MUST be able to show, for any repository sampled at random, ACLs on the repository showing that only authorised personnel (normally members of the team that own the app) can commit to production-bound branches.

C8 - Provide lineage for every Production change

TL;DR: For any change deployed to Production, there MUST be a written description of the change including why it was made. If there is a Jira or GitLab issue associated with the change then this ticket MUST be linked to the change. If the change is made via git commit(s), this information MAY be
information MAY be provided directly in the commit message, and/or indirectly via a merge request.

Justification

For each production change there must be a simple and systematic way to find the following change lineage information:

A description of the change This allows people to quickly understand the intention of a change, without trying to reverse engineer this intention from the contents of the change. A description also helps the reviewer to verify whether a change has been fully tested, and whether it should be
it should be approved.
Justification for the change This helps the reviewer to decide whether a change should be approved. It also allows retrospective explanation of why a change was made.

A link to the Jira or GitLab issue for the change (backlog item, support issue, product incident issue, etc) - if one exists For any non-trivial change an issue will contain additional information such as the origin of the change, agreed requirements, success criteria, discussion details, and
details, and decisions taken. This information is useful in understanding why a change was implemented in a certain way.

Implementation

?> Important: SDLC activity P0 - Maintain a comprehensive team backlog requires teams to maintain a comprehensive team backlog. However, some backlog task types may not be represented on the backlog - small support tasks, or tasks relating to live production incidents, for example.

For changes made via git commits, lineage information MUST be provided via one of these mechanisms: * a merge request for the commit * a Jira or GitLab issue referenced from the merge request for the commit * a commit message (directly) * a Jira or GitLab ticket referenced from the commit message.
the commit message. GitLab Push Rules can be used to ensure each commit contains a reference to an issue.

Teams MAY use some combination of the above.

It's common for a backlog item to be broken down into tasks, and for multiple commits to be associated with a task:

Feature -> tasks -> commits

In this case the backlog item MUST be represented by a Jira or GitLab issue. The task MAY also be represented by an issue, alternatively the task MAY be managed via a physical board. If a task doesn't have an issue, then commits MUST reference the backlog issue instead. A team MAY choose to squash
choose to squash commits such that each task has a single commit.

Small refactors MAY be made in the course of making a change - they will get picked up during code review. Larger refactors SHOULD be treated as separate backlog items.

Evidencing

For any Production change sampled at random, you MUST show the associated lineage information.
C9 - Ensure all changes are reviewed before deployment to Prod

TL;DR: Every code or configuration change MUST be code reviewed by a second person before it is deployed to Production. This includes changes made by Generative AI (GenAI). Auto-generated dependency version update changes, such as those made by Renovate, MAY be configured to auto-merge without
auto-merge without review.

Justification

An independent code review helps spot problems the author may have missed, resulting in:

fewer bugs

cleaner code

better security

The review is our main protection against undesirable code reaching production, whether accidental or deliberate.

GenAI generated code can also have bugs, security flaws, or be difficult to interpret. Bugs in GenAI code may be caused by flawed GenAI model output, or by the human author instructing the GenAI model to generate code that was semantically or logically incorrect. For this reason GenAI generated
GenAI generated code must also be reviewed by a second person.
Implementation

Every code or configuration change MUST be code reviewed by a second person, before it is deployed to Production. This includes changes made by Generative AI (GenAI). You SHOULD configure your GitLab projects to use merge requests, and configure merge request approval rules. DO NOT allow merge

DO NOT allow merge request authors to approve their own merge requests. You SHOULD configure GitLab to check whether all comments are resolved before allowing a merge. Auto-generated dependency version update changes, such as those generated by Renovate, MAY be configured to auto-merge without
auto-merge without review.

When performing a review, you SHOULD check the change:

Meets the functional and non-functional requirements of the associated backlog item

Has not caused a regression

Meets coding guidelines

Has been correctly flagged as requiring, or not requiring, a security code review

Even trivial changes MUST be reviewed. By their nature a trivial change is quick to review. Even supposedly trivial changes have the potential to cause problems - perhaps due to an invalid assumption, or because of a mistake during the commit process. Teams SHOULD be pushing small changes to

small changes to Production regularly, ideally several times a day. Reviews for these changes should not be time consuming because the changes are small. Teams SHOULD have a target turnaround time of a few hours for a code review.

Changes behind feature flags MUST be reviewed before they are released to Production (not in the period of time between deployment and the flag being enabled). This ensures the change is definitely fully disabled by feature flags. Each deployment SHOULD be providing discrete, fully tested

fully tested functionality, even if that functionality is small and disabled. Checking the code and making changes at this time is cheap. If the review is left until a big batch of functionality is complete then the team is left with a more onerous big review, much later on in the product life
in the product life cycle. By this point changes are more expensive to make, and often the pressure is on to enable the feature ASAP. In these circumstances technical debt is likely to accumulate.

It's recommended that changes that have been pair programmed SHOULD also be reviewed by an independent reviewer when the change is complete. This ensures there is validation of the change that is unencumbered by the discussions (and assumptions!) made during the pair programming process. A pair
process. A pair programmed change should be of a higher quality, so we might expect the independent review to proceed more rapidly.

Evidencing

For any change sampled at random, you MUST be able to demonstrate:

who reviewed the change

when the change was reviewed

any comments left by the reviewer

that all code review comments were resolved before the code was deployed

TL;DR: A security code review is a specialised code review of the application’s source code to identify security-related weaknesses. Each change MUST be assessed against When to perform a security code review criteria, to determine whether it requires a security code review. Reviews MUST be
Reviews MUST be performed for all relevant changes, using the checklists. Teams SHOULD re-evaluate services when there are changes to the criteria or checklists.

Justification

Our systems need to be secure. Some types of change have the potential to affect the security of a system. We need a process to ensure such changes are identified, and security code reviewed, to ensure system security is maintained.

Implementation

For each change you MUST:
Consider whether a security code review is required.

You SHOULD use SDLC checklists or equivalent, to ensure a security code review is considered for each change. The Eng Prod SDLC checklists include a reference implementation for this approach - see the merge request template. You SHOULD use the When to perform a security code review criteria (or
review criteria (or equivalent) to determine whether a particular change requires a review.

Perform the security code review (if required).

Take the list of relevant secure coding requirements from the checklists, and review the code against these criteria. For more detailed guidelines refer to the security code review documentation.

Record review/no-review.

Record if no review is required. As per the SDLC checklists, a checkbox is recommended to make this a one-click operation. If a review has taken place, ensure there is a link to the review output from the change's GitLab MR. For more detailed guidelines refer to the security code review
code review documentation.

The security code review criteria (when to perform a review, what to look for) may change over time. Such updates will be announced in an appropriate Slack channel by the AppSec team. Teams SHOULD then reassess their services against the updated criteria.

Evidencing

You MUST be able to show the criteria used to determining whether a change should have a security code review. For a change sampled at random, you MUST show the record of whether it needed a security code review. Do this with reference to the criteria for whether a change needs such a review. For a
a review. For a randomly sampled change that did require a security code review, you MUST be able to show any comments made during the review, and show that they were actioned.

TL;DR: Support documentation is the documentation the development team members and/or Service Desk refer to when supporting the system. Support documentation MUST be kept up to date as the application changes.

Justification

Well maintained support documentation helps support personnel to quickly diagnose, and remediate Production incidents. This leads to lower MTTR (Mean Time To Resolution), and hence higher service availability. Conversely, outdated or otherwise incorrect support documentation can actually increase
actually increase MTTR. Some of the same documentation may also be useful in day to day work - particularly for newer team members.

Support documentation MUST be kept up to date as the application changes. The recommended way to achieve this is to have an 'Acceptance criteria' checklist that is completed for each Merge Request, and to have an 'Update support docs' entry on the checklist. Such checklists can be implemented using
implemented using GitLab Merge Request templates.

team. Everyone on support MUST know where to find the documentation, and SHOULD be familiar with its content.

The Data Dictionary is a record of what data objects and derived objects are being produced in the data lake. It is used to aid data discovery, and provide context to the data. It's used by people in numerous roles, including analysts, development teams, data engineers, and data governance
and data governance specialists. The value of the data dictionary is significantly reduced if the data within it is incomplete, or out of date.

Justification

Clear ownership leads to resources that are well understood and managed over time. We need to be able to list all the resources we develop and run so we can:

Build

The build phase of the SDLC is the process of creating a deployable artifact from source code. The build stage should be used to ensure that the source code used to create the artifact is maintainable (using static analysis), and that the artifact itself meets functional and non-functional
and non-functional requirements.

B1 - Securely build code on an approved build server

TL;DR: You MUST use GitLab CI on the central GitLab server to build your code. You MUST ensure that only authorised personnel can trigger a deployment to Production.

Justification

Justification

We use a single CI solution across all of Ocado Technology so that:

we can share expertise internally

we can provide or generate pipeline code that will be applicable to all teams

the cost per developer is lower, through economies of scale

a single platform team can support CI for all dev teams

Using GitLab CI on the central GitLab servers ensures:

we can reason about the security of the path our code takes to Production, including the permissions users have, and how users and services authenticate

there is a centralised record of builds and build time tests, providing an audit trail for artifacts deployed to production

our build server is security patched

our build server is highly available

we can restore our build server from backup if necessary

we can easily report on the costs of our build processes

development teams focus on their core mission, not maintaining build servers

platform teams no longer need to provide a mechanism for development teams to create their own build servers

Limiting who can deploy code to production ensures we adhere to the principle of least privilege.

Implementation

You MUST use GitLab CI on the central GitLab server to build your code. You MUST create a GitLab CI build and deployment pipeline that includes a stage to deploy to Production. You MUST configure the GitLab project so that only members of your team can commit to the project directly. Other GitLab
Other GitLab users MAY submit merge requests.

Build job console output must be retained for at least one month.

Evidencing

For any app sampled at random, you MUST:

show the build job history on GitLab CI

demonstrate how only authorised personnel can deploy the app to Production

B2 - Automate your tests

TL;DR

TL;DR

All non-trivial applications MUST have a set of tests that are run at build time to check the build meets functional and non-functional requirements. These tests SHOULD be automated.

Justification

Justification

Automated tests are a cornerstone of Continuous Integration. Automated tests have a number of benefits over manual tests. Automated tests:

can be run on every build to detect regressions

run more quickly than manual tests
can be run on-demand - there is no need to schedule someone to run the tests

There are some scenarios where manual testing is preferable. These include exploratory testing, and testing the user experience. However, the more often a test needs to be executed, the stronger the case for automating it. Have a strong preference for automated tests.

Implementation

Implementation

All non-trivial applications MUST have a set of tests that are run at build time to check the build meets functional and non-functional requirements. These tests SHOULD be automated. Build time tests SHOULD nearly always include:

Static analysis

Unit tests

Security tests

Security tests

Where applicable, build-time tests SHOULD also include:

Component tests

Contract tests

Contract tests

Load tests often take too long, or are too expensive, to run automatically on every build. In this case, you MAY schedule these to run once per day, and/or could run them manually when there is risk of a change impacting performance or scaling characteristics.

Open-source libraries and frameworks can contain security vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities can leave apps using the open-source code vulnerable to attack. Scanning with Snyk detects these vulnerabilities. The SIM process ensures vulnerabilities are triaged, and appropriate action taken.

original artifact. Neither of these is desirable in the emergency situations in which old artifacts are most frequently used.

The Testing SDLC phase is where we ensure system changes meet their function and non-functional requirements, and that there are no regressions.

T1 - Follow the testing guidelines

TL;DR: You MUST follow test strategy and the principles of testing laid out in the testing guidelines.

Justification

The testing guidelines defines a common set of test activities that teams can utilise to ensure our systems are reliable, scalable, and secure.

Implementation

Implementation

You MUST follow testing principles. You MUST follow test strategy. It can be stream/department/cluster/team strategy which is based on test pyramid and test types recommended in testing guidelines. You MUST use a risk-based approach to testing, i.e. add tests where:

there is more likely to be a defect, either now, or in the future

the impact of a potential defect justifies the writing of the test

Test cases should be retained for at least 12 months after the release of a feature.

Evidencing

Evidencing
