T2 - Ensure new public facing apps are penetration tested

TL;DR: All new internet facing apps MUST be penetration tested before they are exposed to the internet. This includes apps that expose APIs, UI, and apps that require a user to authenticate (e.g. via Panda) before the app can be accessed.

Justification

Any app exposing functionality on the internet is a potential target for a malicious actor, e.g. a hacker. To protect Ocado assets, the Ocado business, and our reputation it is Ocado security policy that all such apps MUST be penetration tested (pen tested) before they are exposed to the internet
to the internet for the first time, and then on a regular basis thereafter.

Implementation

The following types of app MUST be pen tested before release:

apps exposing user interfaces

apps exposing APIs

apps that require the users to login first, e.g. apps running behind Panda authentication

Apps that are not intended to be exposed on the internet, but where it is the development team who control whether an app is exposed or not (e.g. GCP projects where the dev team configure the firewall) MUST also be pen tested before release. A pen test will normally take place in the Production
in the Production environment, after the app is deployed, but before the app is exposed to the internet (it will be exposed to the pen tester only).

You SHOULD schedule a new pen test if you heavily re-architect your app, or if you make major changes to the security model. You SHOULD NOT schedule a new penetration test every time you add a new API endpoint, or a new UI screen. Request a penetration test at https://infosec.ocado.com. See the
See the Penetration Testing Guidebook for additional information.

Evidencing

For any public or retailer facing system sampled at random, that was released in the past 12 months, you MUST produce the initial penetration testing report.

Deploy

The deployment phase of the SDLC is the process of updating the code or configuration in an environment.
Use Continuous Delivery and Feature Flags

Teams should use a combination of Continuous Delivery and Feature Flags to deliver features. New feature code should be introduced as a series of small incremental code changes, hidden behind a conditional flag, known as a feature flag (or feature toggle). When the feature is complete, a standalone
a standalone deployment is used to flip the feature flag, and enable the feature. Using Continuous Delivery and Feature Flags brings several benefits;

each deployed software version differs only slightly from the previous version. If problems are seen with the code, it is trivial to identify and fix the root cause

incremental changes can be backed out with minimal risk

the code is always (or almost always) in a state where it can be deployed at any time, meaning fixes and smaller changes are not backed up behind bigger changes, and can be developed and released at short notice. Merge conflicts are also less likely

smaller changes can be code reviewed more quickly and with more confidence

DE1 - Use documented and tested deployment and rollback processes

TL;DR: You MUST have documented deployment and rollback processes for each system. If there are manual steps in the deployment or rollback, these SHOULD be incorporated into a checklist. The rollback process includes all actions required for the environment to run as expected. It can be rollback,
It can be rollback, restoration from a snapshot or other.

When preparing each deployment, you SHOULD check that the standard process is sufficient. If additional steps are required, you SHOULD document and test them before performing the deployment. You SHOULD use checklists when performing any manual steps to ensure nothing is forgotten.

Justification

Deploying a change to a system increases the risk of the system having a problem. Even the best-tested changes can have unintended effects. Deployments can also go wrong for environmental reasons not directly related to the change being deployed. Having a well documented and tested deployment

tested deployment process reduces the chance of problems during deployment. Having a well documented and tested rollback process reduces the MTTR (Mean Time To Resolution) should rollback be required to mitigate a production incident.

We SHOULD define how to rollback or backout changes in the event of a problem with a deployment rather than relying on "fix forward" as a strategy. Some risks associated with fixing forward are:

the required changes are not as quick as initially expected

there is no guarantee that a change is made and will work the first time

the code, build, test and deploy process takes much longer than expected

there is something wrong with the required DTAP environments and that delays the ‘simple’ change

It’s easy to plan and test how you will rollback a failed deployment, but it’s not easy to plan how to fix forward from a failed or faulty deployment because you can’t know exactly how it might be unsuccessful until it happens.

Implementation

Implementation

Each system MUST have a documented process for deploying changes. This document should cover all standard deployment scenarios. If some steps in the process are manual steps, there MUST be a documented checklist that is followed for each deployment.
?> Integrate the SDLC Checklists into your GitLab or Jira projects to ensure you always consider whether the standard deployment plan is sufficient.
Similarly, each system MUST have a documented process for rollback. Again, if steps in the process are manual steps, there should be a documented checklist that is followed for each rollback.

For each deployment, the team MUST consider whether there is anything special about the change that means the default deployment or rollback process might not work in this case. Some examples of where this might be necessary for a given application are:
The app deployment relies on some corresponding config. The config needs to be deployed first. If a backout is required then the app should be backed out first, followed by the config.
Version 2.0 of app B is only compatible with version 2.x of app A. Before deploying app B, ensure app A has been deployed. If app A needs to be backed out, then app B must be backed out first.

The new version of the app persists data in a different format in DynamoDB. The old version of the app will not be able to read this new data format. If the app needs to be backed out, a script must be run to revert the DynamoDB data format changes.

The new version of the app requires a DB schema change to be deployed first. The old version of the app is not compatible with the new schema. If the app needs to be backed out, a script must be run to downgrade the DB schema as part of the backout.

If a deviation from the standard processes is required, the teams SHOULD capture the deviation in written form and attach it to the associated change ticket. The modified process SHOULD be verified in a test environment before deploying to Production.

There may be some platforms that do not allow replacing a deployed system with a previous version. For example, it is not possible to rollback versions of apps deployed to iOS or Android devices; in this case a standard "rollback" could be to take a known good version of the application and release
and release it again as the latest version. This would be a more predictable approach than trying to "fix forward"; worth considering.

If you use feature flags to enable changes, remember that traceability of change is important. Your implementation of the feature flag may be defective, and you may have to roll back a deployment, so you MUST provide a documented rollback process.

?> Make application and database changes backwards compatible wherever possible. You can then deploy and backout the change without needing any deviation from the standard process.

Evidencing

You MUST show the standard deployment and rollback process documentation for each system your team owns.

You MUST show that you consider if the standard deployment process is sufficient for each deployment, and make deviations as necessary.

You MUST show that you have a systematic approach that ensures all manual steps in the deployment process (if any) are always completed, e.g. using checklists.

You MUST version control and retain your standard deployment and rollback process. All deviations SHOULD be attached to the associated change ticket and retained for 12 months.

DE2 - Deployment rollout

TL;DR: You MUST deploy to each DTAP environment in the order defined in deployment order. Where there are multiple production environments or a large number of devices to deploy to, you MUST use Wave and Bake to roll out changes across production environments.

Justification

Justification

Deploying to each DTAP environment in the correct order ensures:

All DTAP environments have the latest version of code/config. Testing against an out of date DTAP environment risks false-positive test results.

A change passes the test suites in all environments before it is deployed to Production.

Using Wave and Bake reduces the chances of a broken deployment affecting multiple retailers, sites, or users.

Implementation

The DTAP deployment order is documented in DTAP classification. Wave and Bake is documented in Deployment rollout - Wave and Bake. You MUST configure your build pipeline to deploy changes to the environments in the correct order, implementing Wave and Bake for rollout across production
across production environments.

?> You MAY make an exception for emergency deployments to fix an urgent production issue. In these situations the risk of shortcutting the normal deployment rollout process (the biggest risk being that the change is not fully tested) SHOULD be weighted against the time saved getting the fix into
the fix into Production. If a deployment does skip environment(s), you SHOULD come back and deploy to those environments as soon as possible afterwards.

If you do have a legitimate reason to not deploy a particular change to the DTAP environments in the normal order, or to diverge from your normal Wave and Bake process, you MUST ensure this is documented somewhere (e.g. in the GitLab/Jira issue for the change, or in the record of a production
of a production incident).

Evidencing

For any change, show the build pipeline or audit log for the change. You MUST show either:

The change is deployed to the DTAP environments in the correct order, and then rolled out across production environments following the principles of Wave and Bake.

A written record of why the change necessitated an exception to the normal process. Such exception records should be kept for a minimum of 12 months.

DE3 - Use an approved deployment tool

TL;DR: You MUST use one of the approved deployment tools from the Deployment tooling section of the Tech Stacks site to deploy your changes.

Justification

Only authorised personnel MUST be able to trigger a deployment to Production. There MUST also be audit trail of all deployments and backouts. The approved deployment tools enforce authentication, authorisation, access control lists for deployment actions, and capturing of audit records.

Implementation

You MUST use one of the approved deployment tools from the Deployment tooling section of the Tech Stacks site to deploy changes.

Evidencing

For any change, you MUST show the deployment audit log in an approved deployment tool.

DE4 - Automate your deployments

TL;DR: You SHOULD extend your build pipeline to also deploy to DTAP environments.

Justification

Compared to manual deployments, automated deployments are faster, more reliable, and leave a better record of the actions performed.

Implementation

Implementation

You SHOULD extend your build pipeline to also cover deployment to DTAP environments. Your build pipeline MUST call one of the approved deployment tools via API, to trigger deployment(s).

There are two deployment modes:

attended deployment - at least one person is monitoring the deployment

unattended deployment - nobody is monitoring the deployment

An unattended deployment has the potential to cause disruption if there is a problem. The following pre-conditions must be in place before a system is deployed unattended:

on-call support

auto-alerts to support when:

there are no healthy instances of a service

when deployment to Production fails

when the SLI drops below the SLO

zero downtime deployments

safe, automated rollback (e.g. pressing a button in CI)

deploy only small backwards compatible changes, facilitating simple rollback

Evidencing

Evidencing

For any app, you SHOULD show the automated deployment pipeline.

Trial

The trialling phase of the SDLC is the phase during which Abundo and iOSP users try a new feature before it is released to a client. Trialling is intended to get internal end-user feedback on a feature. Your product manager may make pre-release changes to the feature as a result of this feedback.
of this feedback. If a change is not client-facing the trial phase of the SDLC is not relevant, and should be skipped.

!> Trialling is not a QA phase. All changes must be fully tested by the development team before trialling commences.

TR1 - Use a Definition of Done checklist

TL;DR: You SHOULD use a feature level Definition of Done checklist to ensure a feature is ready for release.

Justification

Many activities need to be complete before a feature is ready for production release. These activities should be completed before the feature is trialled. Many of the activities are included in this SDLC, but some are not (e.g. getting text strings translated into other languages). Historically we

Historically we found that almost every feature was delivered for trialling with some activities not completed. This was inefficient because features were held up in Abundo waiting for the missing activities to be completed. The feature-level definition of done checklist ensures all features are
all features are ready for release before they are presented as ready for trialling.

Implementation

You SHOULD use the Product Management Definition of Done checklist. Augment the standard checklist with any additional activities specific to your team.

Evidencing

For any feature, you SHOULD show the completed definition of done checklist.

TR2 - Trial new features where appropriate

TL;DR: You SHOULD arrange for your feature to be trialled in Abundo or iOSP if you want internal end-user feedback and validation, before it is released to external OSP clients.

Justification

Trialling is a chance to validate that a feature provides the expected value, before it is released to external OSP retailers. Trialling takes place under normal operational conditions - something that is not possible earlier in the development lifecycle - so it can bring new issues to light. For
to light. For example, trialling may highlight usability issues when the system is used by people other than the development team.

Implementation

Not all changes can be, or need to be, trialled before release to clients. Only changes that will benefit from end user feedback SHOULD be trialled. Abundo and iOSP have well-defined processes for trialling a change. For Abundo, see the OSP trialling guide.

Your team MUST have a defined process in place to determine whether each particular feature should be trialled or not. This decision MUST be documented against the feature, and the process MUST ensure all changes flagged for trialling are trialled before release. It is the Product Manager who
Product Manager who ultimately determines whether a change should be trialled or not.

The operational team trialling the feature MAY be asked to use the system as normal, or (if applicable to the change) they MAY be asked to ensure that they try certain types of operation. They SHOULD NOT be asked to run through specific test scenarios, or to try to break the system - these are
system - these are testing activities that MUST happen before trialling.

Evidencing

For any feature, you SHOULD show where it's captured whether the feature should be trialled. You SHOULD show documented evidence of the trialling process for a feature that has been trialled, including any feedback. Where relevant, you SHOULD show actions taken following the feedback.

Operate

The Operation phase of the SDLC is the process of supporting the software when it's running in Production. Development teams are responsible for supporting their system in Production.

O1 - Implement monitoring and alerting

TL;DR: You MUST implement automated alerting for all applications. At a minimum you SHOULD alert when the system is down, is generating unexpected errors, or is exhibiting high latency. Add application monitoring using a tool like New Relic or Prometheus.

Justification

Teams cannot pro-actively watch dashboards 24x7, so automated alerting is required for all applications. Good alerting means the development team is alerted to a problem as soon as it happens. In the best case the team will be alerted before the user is aware there is a problem. The team can begin

The team can begin to investigate and mitigate the problem immediately, minimising end-user impact. The alternative is an end-user reporting the issue. This involves the end-users has contacted their service desk, who then have to contact our service desk (possibly via an intermediary translation

translation service for non-English speaking users), who then have to contact the right development team. This long call chain is time-consuming and error-prone, meaning increased mean time to resolution (MTTR).

We aspire to have development teams auto-notified of all problems before the end users are even aware there is an issue.Good monitoring allows a team to quickly identify the cause of a problem, reducing MTTR.

Implementation

An alert notifies the development team there is a problem. Monitoring allows the dev team to see metrics to investigate issues. Teams cannot pro-actively watch dashboards 24x7, so automated alerting MUST be configured for all applications. At the very minimum your alerting SHOULD cover these basic
cover these basic system metrics:

availability - is the service up and serving requests?

errors - is the service generating unexpected errors?

latency - is the service responding in the expected time frame?

Far more effective alerting is achieved by also alerting on business metrics, for example:

whether the number of searches on the Webshop is proportional to the number of logged in users

whether the proportion of payment cards failing pre-auth is much higher than average

See the Monitoring and Alerting section of the Tech Stacks sites for a list of tools available for monitoring and alerting. Recommended reading includes My Philosophy on Alerting by Google SRE Rob Ewaschuk.

Evidencing

Evidencing

For any system, you MUST show and describe the configured alerts, and any supporting monitoring.

O2 - Be notified of new open-source vulnerabilities

TL;DR: You MUST have a process in place to notify you when there is a vulnerability in an open source component used by your application. Log all vulnerabilities using the Security Issue Management (SIM) process.

Justification

A vulnerability in an open source component can leave the application that uses it vulnerable to attack. Vulnerabilities are often fixed in a later version of the component. Keeping components updating with security fixes has the additional benefit of iteratively keeping application dependencies up
dependencies up to date.
Implementation

Vulnerabilities can be found in open source software at any time. Your team MUST have a process in place to ensure they are notified when a new vulnerability is found in a library the team is using. All vulnerabilities MUST be logged in the relevant SIM tracker. The standard SIM process MUST then
process MUST then be followed for triage and remediation.

Use Snyk to scan your application for vulnerabilities. Follow our Application Security team's instructions when setting up Snyk, to ensure SIM issues are created automatically whenever a new vulnerability is detected.

Evidencing

Evidencing

For any system, you MUST show the ongoing scanning of the system for new vulnerabilities, and demonstrate how any detected vulnerability results in a SIM ticket.

O3 - Ensure regular penetration tests are scheduled

TL;DR: Public and retailer facing apps MUST be penetration tested (pen tested) every six months. The InfoSec team coordinate these tests. If you suspect InfoSec are not correctly scheduling pen tests for one of your systems you SHOULD notify them via the InfoSec portal.

Justification

Justification

Regular penetration testing gives confidence that an application continues to be secure. An application that was previously secure might now be insecure because:

the application code has changed

the application is using components that has had new vulnerabilities published

the environment the application is running in has changed

Implementation

Implementation

The InfoSec team are responsible for ensuring all public and retailer facing applications are pen tested every six months. However, if you suspect InfoSec are not correctly scheduling pen tests for one of your systems you SHOULD notify them via the InfoSec portal.

Evidencing

Evidencing

InfoSec will evidence that all applicable applications are regularly pen tested.

Deprecated activity O4 is intentionally omitted

O5 - Understand and follow the Incident Response process

TL;DR: You MUST ensure all team members who provide production support are aware of, and follow, the Ocado Technology Incident Response process.

Justification

The Incident Response process describes what to do before, during and immediately after a production incident. It is intended to be used by everyone involved in the incident response process, including software engineers, Service Desk and Incident Managers. The incident response process is designed
process is designed to ensure:

serious production incidents are mitigated as quickly as possible

the client is kept updated if an incident is affecting their service levels

These objectives can only be achieved if the engineers providing production support follow the process. There is little time for engineers to (re)familiarise themselves with the process during a production incident, so they must ensure they are familiar with the process before they enter a
before they enter a production support role.
Implementation

All team members MUST be aware of the Incident Response process. You SHOULD create a documented new-starter process for your team if you don't have one already, and include the Incident Response process. You SHOULD ensure support engineers can describe the Incident Response process in detail before

in detail before they join any support rota. They MAY use the Service Assurance team's Incident Response training to improve their understanding of the process. You SHOULD have them complete the Incident Response and Problem Management Quiz, then review their quiz answers together, and clarify any
and clarify any areas of confusion.

Evidencing

You MUST show that every team member who provides production support can describe, and follows, the incident response process.

O6 - Understand and follow the Problem Management process

TL;DR: The team MUST be familiar with the Ocado Technology Problem Management process. The team SHOULD follow this process, even for problems that do not result in a major production incident.

Justification

The Problem Management process is designed to ensure:

we learn from production incidents

we take steps to prevent their recurrence

teams can learn from incidents that other teams have experienced

These objectives can only be achieved if everyone involved in the development of our systems is utilising the problem management process.

Implementation

All team members MUST be familiar with the Problem Management process. You SHOULD create a documented new-starter process for your team if you don't have one already, and include familiarisation with the Problem Management processes in the new starter process. You SHOULD ensure your senior

ensure your senior engineers have completed the Problem Management process training. Review their answers together, and clarify any areas of confusion. You SHOULD use the Problem Management process even for issues that do not meet the Problem Management team gate-in criteria.

Evidencing

You MUST show that the team is familiar with the Problem Management process.

O7 - Keep dependencies and runtimes up to date

TL;DR: You MUST ensure direct application dependencies (libraries / frameworks) and runtimes are always on the latest major version, or the major version prior to that. You MUST apply minor and patch version dependency updates periodically. You SHOULD apply patch version dependency updates
dependency updates automatically using a tool such as Renovate. You MAY apply minor version dependency updates automatically too.
Justification

If dependencies and runtimes are not kept up to date then eventually the versions used in production will no longer be supported. This means they will no longer receive security patches or bug fixes. Using unsupported software versions in production increases the risk of security vulnerabilities,
vulnerabilities, and incompatibility with the wider production ecosystem which will be receiving regular updates.

A backlog of unapplied runtime and/or dependency updates is a form of technical debt. The more updates there are to apply, the more difficult and time-consuming it becomes to apply them, and the more transitive dependencies will also need to be updated to maintain version compatability. This larger

This larger piece of technical debt is then more difficult to prioritise because of the impact it will have on a team's roadmap. This problem can compound because updates are released continuously, so the longer the software is left without being updated, the more updates will need to be applied.
need to be applied. If left for long enough the size of the update task can become prohibitive. Software using very old runtime or dependency versions is a form of legacy software.

Applying a single patch or minor version update can be done relatively quickly. If there is a critical security vulnerability in a dependency then the fix will be released as a patch or minor version update. If your application has up-to-date dependencies, then patching a security vulnerability

vulnerability should be trivial. This means any critical vulnerability can be remediated rapidly. If applying the fix also requires a backlog of previous release versions to be applied, then it may not be possible to apply to fix immediately, meaning the system is insecure for longer.

By applying updates promptly, no tech debt is accrued, critical security patches can be applied immediately, and the application avoids drifting down the path of becoming a legacy application.

Implementation

Configure Renovate to create GitLab Merge Requests for application dependencies. Configure Renovate to auto-merge patch version updates. If an application has a comprehensive test suite then Renovate MAY be configured to auto-merge minor version updates too. Major version updates SHOULD NOT
updates SHOULD NOT generally be auto-merged because it's advisable to read the release notes before upgrading to a new major version.
If a direct dependency does not implement semver versioning you MUST apply an alternative approach to upgrades that aligns with the principles laid out above.

When you are notified that a new major runtime version is available to use you MUST then schedule work to upgrade to that new major runtime version. You MUST schedule the upgrade to occur before the next major version is made available, but you SHOULD schedule the upgrade to happen no later than

no later than half-way through the runtime release cycle (so if a new major version is released every 2 years, you SHOULD schedule an upgrade in the first year of the release). You MAY choose to schedule the upgrade soon after the new version is released.

Evidencing

You MUST be able to describe how version upgrades are managed. This SHOULD be documented. You MUST be able to show that you are following the process you describe.

Feedback

Feedback

The Feedback phase of the SDLC is the process of ensuring our systems continue to meet the needs and expectations of their users over time.

F1 - Product Managers receive end user feedback

TL;DR: If the team's products have users, then the Product Manager MUST receive feedback from those users. If a product contains a user interface, or an API, the product manager SHOULD regularly observe end users using this interface.

Justification

During product development, many assumptions are made about what end users want to achieve with the product, and how they will interact with the product. Many of these assumptions are made without even realising, and almost certainly some of them will be incorrect. Asking end users to provide

users to provide feedback on the product, and watching them using the product is the best way to correct any invalid assumptions. In addition, end users will commonly suggest product improvements that would otherwise not occur to the product manager.

Watching end users interact with an API (or inspecting the code used to call your APIs) should be just as instructive as watching them interact with a user interface. The exercise will reveal whether the API is easily meeting the user's needs, and whether they are interacting with it in the way
with it in the way that you expect.
Implementation

If the team owns a product that has end users, then the Product Manager MUST receive product and UX feedback from those end users. If the product contains a user interface, or an API, the product manager SHOULD regularly observe end users using this interface. See User feedback for more information
more information on the importance of gathering user feedback, and how the UX team will help you get started with this activity.

Evidencing

If your product has users you MUST demonstrate that you receive product feedback from those users.

F2 - Security issue management

TL;DR: You MUST use the Security Issue Management (SIM) process for all reported production security issues.

Justification

Justification

The SIM process ensures vulnerabilities are triaged, and appropriate action taken.

Implementation

Implementation

You MUST use the SIM process to log, triage, and remediate any security issues reported for your systems running in Production. These issues COULD be reported by end users, one of our internal cloud or platform tooling teams, InfoSec, AppSec, or another engineer.

Evidencing

Evidencing

You MUST show the SIM ticket for a security issue raised for a Production system, or justify why no security issues have been raised against your system.

Checklists

Building simple checklists into your team issue management tooling is the simplest way to ensure an SDLC activity:

is never forgotten - required for several Maturity Model level 4s

can be evidenced - required for SOC2 (see SOC2 evidence by change type for details)

Both the World Health Organisation (WHO), and the aviation industry recognise that checklists:

"help teams ... remember important details that may be missed"

"encourage teamwork and communication" [Source Use of the Surgical Safety Checklist to Improve Communication and Reduce Complications.]

As it states in this paper, "a growing body of literature points out that while the physical act of 'checking the box' may not necessarily prevent all adverse events, the checklist is a scaffold on which attitudes towards teamwork and communication can be encouraged and improved".

Secrets management

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #eng-prod-sig.

This section describes how to manage credential secrets you encounter in your day-to-day work, for example:

Passwords

Access tokens

Private keys

Private keys

The following examples are not secrets, and do not need the same level of protection as secrets:

Credential usernames

Public keys

Credentials exposed directly to end users (for instance, by inclusion in client-side Javascript)

Personal secrets

Personal secrets

Personal secrets belong to you as an individual and should not be shared with anyone, or deployed to any Development, Test, Acceptance or Production (DTAP) environment. Personal secret examples:

Your personal Ocado SSO log in.

Your personal ssh private key and associated password.

Your personal secret for third-party software that is not integrated with Ocado SSO.

Storing personal secrets

Follow the principles below when you handle personal secrets:

You MAY store them in Keeper Enterprise.

You SHOULD move them to Keeper Enterprise if currently stored elsewhere (e.g. Google Docs).

You MUST NOT commit them to GitLab repositories.

If necessary for software development in your local development environment, you MUST store them in environment variables (see example below).

Team service account secrets

Team service account secrets belong to a team. These secrets MUST NOT be deployed to a DTAP environment. Examples:

The password for an AD account that belongs to your team.

The Chancellor private token for an App Group.

The Sonatype Nexus read-write password for your App Group.

The Service Account API token for your Snyk organization.

AWS access credentials (regardless if it is an access key or other secret) need to follow these guidelines:

AWS access keys must be rotated at least every 90 days where viable.

Any unused keys must be removed after 90 days.

These secrets must be rotated more frequently to limit their lifecycle and associated risk. They must be changed whenever a user who is privy to the secrets leaves the team or Ocado, or no longer requires access.

Storing team service account secrets

Follow the principles below when you handle team service account secrets:

You MUST store and share them via Keeper Enterprise, if new.

You SHOULD move them to Keeper Enterprise if currently stored elsewhere.

You MUST NOT commit them to GitLab repositories.
If required during the build process, you MUST store them as masked and protected Gitlab CI/CD variables. Please be aware of the limitations of GitLab variable masking.

If necessary for software development in your local development environment, you MUST store them in environment variables (see example below).

Application secrets

Application secrets belong to an application and are deployed to a DTAP environment. Secrets for development and test environments MUST be protected in the same way as production secrets, because we do not want to facilitate unauthorised access to any of our systems, particularly as they
as they increasingly run on public cloud infrastructure, where we have the defense in depth approach to security. Examples:

An application service account.

An application’s secret to call a third-party API (e.g. Google, Slack, Jira).

An application’s database login secret.

Panda client secrets.

OAuth secrets that give an app delegated access to a user’s personal information.

Storing application secrets

Follow the principles below when you handle application secrets:

You MUST store them in an encrypted form in all DTAP environments, as per Hallmark D11.

You MUST NOT commit them to GitLab repositories in plaintext (i.e. unencrypted).

You MAY store them in GitLab in an encrypted form. In this scenario the decryption key secret MUST also be stored securely - follow the guidelines in this document. The decryption key MUST NOT be stored alongside the encrypted data!

You MAY store them as GitLab CI/CD variables which are injected into a config file at deployment time. Such variables MUST be appropriately masked and protected. Please be aware of the limitations of GitLab variable masking.

You SHOULD periodically rotate them if secret rotation is supported.
You MUST store them in Keeper to allow rapid recovery in a disaster recovery scenario. Note, this requirement does not apply if the key is being automatically rotated.

If necessary for software development in your local development environment, you MUST store them in environment variables (see example below).

Local development environment secrets

For information on how to store secrets in your local development environment, see Local secrets management.

Technical standards

Coding style guides

Standardizing code styles reduces the probability that a merge request will need stylistic changes to match the rest of the code. Having a unified standard also frees teams from spending time on local standards.

The teams that program in Java SHOULD follow the Google Java Style Guide{target=blank} when creating new applications. When time allows, you SHOULD modify the existing Java applications to follow the Google Java Style Guide.
IntelliJ users can use the google-java-format IDE plugin{target=blank} to reformat code to the Google Java Style Guide standard. Other workflows can use the google-java-format{target=blank} tool.

All projects SHOULD store their IDE style guide configuration in Git. You SHOULD commit non-trivial stylistic changes to code separately from any change that alters the code's behavior.

Style guides standards for other languages may follow in the future.

Build tools

Standardizing on a build tool lets our software engineers clone any codebase more quickly, get it running locally, and (when necessary) make changes to the codebase’s build process. Using a single build tool means internal build time plugins, documentation, and best practices only need to be
only need to be implemented once.

The teams that program in Java SHOULD use Gradle{target=blank} when creating new applications. When time allows, you SHOULD modify the existing applications to use Gradle.

Build tool standards for other languages may follow in the future.

Health check standardisation
Review this section to implement a /health-check URL for Load Balancers to determine whether the app is healthy.

Every app SHOULD respond on URL /health-check indicating its state of health. Having a fixed URL for the health check is a deliberate convention over configuration choice to make life easier for tools that need to health check a number of different systems.
The /health-check URL MUST be accessible without authentication, because AWS ELBs do not support authentication.

Applications SHOULD cache health check information to protect against denial of service attacks. Access to the health check URL cannot be protected by authentication because AWS Elastic Load Balancers do not support authenticating on health check calls. If your app might be susceptible to a denial
to a denial of service attack on the health check URL from an unauthenticated source, then have the app cache the health-check result for 30 seconds.

Health checks SHOULD NOT rely on the health of downstream applications, they should only report their own health. This makes it much easier to identify the root cause of production issues, i.e. which service is the broken one, and allows avoiding potential circular dependencies when
dependencies when health-checking. Upstream services MUST tolerate problems with downstream services through use of a circuit breaker library such as Resilience4j. (See C3)
The app SHOULD respond with JSON and an associated HTTP status code. The JSON format is:

{
  "appId" : "<app registry app id>",
  "healthStatus" : "<insert status here>",
  "lastCheckedTimestamp" : "<ISO 8601 UTC timestamp> of when cached health status was last generated",
  "additionalInformation" : "<extra info, e.g. why the app is unhealthy>",

"startupTimestamp" : "<ISO 8601 UTC timestamp> of when app instance started up>",
  "appVersion" : "<app version - optional for Atmosphere systems>",
  "details" : [
    {
      "name" : "<application-specific>",
      "description" : "<Some application specific health indicator or detail info>",
You MAY add additional application-specific fields to this JSON if you need to.

Before your application returns healthy it SHOULD perform some typical application logic (e.g. running a query against data stores and dependencies like S3 which may themselves be unavailable). This will give greater confidence that the app is actually functioning as expected, and will detect
and will detect routing failures with an AZ, and other cloud service issues. It is possible that the /health-check URL will not return anything if:

the port is not open

the request timed out

there is an internal server error

If the /health-check URL is externalised, authentication is not required, but an API key (e.g. AWS API Gateway x-api-key) must be used. If additional application-specific fields are added to the standard JSON response, ensure no sensitive information is exposed.

RESTful API conventions

We recommend everyone designing RESTful APIs reads the Apigee API design ebook, a fantastic concise guide to RESTful API design best practices. Below you can find the Ocado Technology conventions not covered in the Apigee API book.

Versioning

Versioning

Every API MUST include a version number at, or near, the root level. For example, if you want to version the whole API together:

https\://example-service.lastmile.org/v1/blog-posts

If you want to version parts of the API separately:
https\://example-service.lastmile.org/blog-posts/v1

The version number SHOULD be an integer, and SHOULD only be updated for breaking changes. Clients SHOULD be designed such that adding an additional field to the data returned from any call is not a breaking change. Teams SHOULD migrate to newer versions of APIs, so that deprecated APIs can be
APIs can be deleted.

HTTP methods

HTTP methods used in APIs MUST be limited to:

DELETE - for removing resource(s)

GET - for retrieving resource(s)

HEAD - for testing the existence of resource(s)

POST - for creating resource(s)

PUT - for creating or updating resource(s) at a specific URL
POST: When creating a resource with POST, do not supply an ID. An auto-generated ID will be returned in the result of the POST call. A POST call is not idempotent.
PUT: When creating a resource with PUT, you must provide an ID in the URL and the resource will be created or updated at this URL. A PUT request must be idempotent.

!> IMPORTANT: Do not use HTTP PATCH - it is not uniformly supported in all libraries, servers and infrastructure components. Even if it is supported by all components in our stack, client stacks (e.g. OSP retailer tech stacks) may not uniformly support PATCH. There is no single accepted standard
accepted standard for how PATCH should work. Tweaks to the API design can remove the need for PATCH.
HTTP headers

HTTP header names SHOULD follow the convention of using dashes to separate words (e.g. My-Custom-Header). RFC 2616 for HTTP/1.1 states that header names are case-insensitive, and case changes have been observed as headers are processed by intermediary servers or libraries, so DO NOT rely on case.

Note that the RequestID header does not use a dash to separate words because it predates this advice.

URL formats

Hostname and URI path MUST: - use all lowercase - separate words with hyphens - use the plural form of the word for resources in the URL (e.g. .../baskets)

Query string parameters MUST: - use camelCase, starting with a lowercase letter

Example:

https://example-service.lastmile.org/v1/blog-posts?postTitle=Blog%20Time

JSON body

JSON body

If an API returns a response, it MUST always be a JSON object, even if only a single value is being returned. Returning an object allows you to add additional fields later without breaking the existing clients.

Contrary to the Apigee API design guidelines, do not return the containing object in the JSON body. For example:

For a URL call of https://example-service.lastmile.org/v1/blog-posts/1:

Return this:

   {
  "title": "my blog post",
  "body": "some interesting content"
}

Do not return this:

Abbreviations in JSON properties SHOULD be camelcase: - "A Big Customer Fulfilment Centre" would be aBigCfc - "Some Identifier" would be someId - "Inter CFC Transfer ID" would be interCfcTransferId

Passing lists in APIs
A List API is an endpoint that accepts a list of identifiers and returns a list of objects, commonly in JSON format.

Using List APIs can cause the following issues: - Uneven load - E.g. A request comes into 1 instance asking for 20k items, a request comes into another instance asking for 7 - load balancer treats these equally. - Error handling - E.g. If someone requests 10 items, but you can only return 4, do you

return 4, do you need space in your response object for 6 errors mapped to which id they related to? That could end up with some terrible API response objects. - Problems with parallelisation - E.g. People get used to the ease of passing lists everywhere and don't realise a single 300ms call could

300ms call could actually be lots of 25ms calls concurrently executing, in some/many cases. - Inconsistent implementation - E.g. Some APIs return 400 Bad Request if any item cannot be found - others return partial result sets.

When List APIs are appropriate - sometimes it is beneficial from cost/performance point of view to have batch endpoints: - If a lot of entities which are asked for are in the same partition/index in a database. In this case, that database can scan it once and give you all needed data in one go. -
data in one go. - When there are a naturally grouped list of items that are related. For example, the contents of a basket.

Guidelines for implementing List APIs

Avoid using List APIs if you do not need to use them. However, there may be cases when a List API is appropriate and this is up to the team to decide. Below are the best practices when using a List API is necessary:

Use the correct HTTP request type.

Use a GET request when you can guaranteed the URL limit length will not be broken. Because GET requests do not allow data to be sent in the request body, it will often hit the URL length limit when being used to request a long list of objects. GET request can be used to request a list of objects

a list of objects from an API when the URL length limit is guaranteed not to be broken. - if using a GET request, favour the id=1&id=2&id=3 style of listed parameters (over id=1,2,3). Favour singular names over pluralised names, e.g. id over ids. - if breaking the URL limit cannot be guaranteed,
be guaranteed, use a PUT request with the list of request IDs in the request body. See below.

Use a PUT request with arguments in the request body when using GET is not possible. - use a PUT request with the list of arguments in JSON format in the request body. - when using the PUT HTTP method in this way use the prefix batch-query in the URL so that it explains that it is a request for
it is a request for data rather than the usual usage of PUT (e.g., /crm/batch-query/customersInfo).

PUT is the recommendation, but the choice is ultimately up to the team, for example if you already have endpoints that are using POST they do not need to be migrated immediately.

For every requested ID that succeeds, there should be a separate returned JSON object.

Failed entities need not have a separate returned JSON object, but may be represented in other ways (i.e.: a list of failed IDs). This allows one or more entities to fail but still return the rest of the request entities. For security reasons, the team may decide that a failed entity should fail
entity should fail the whole request (by returning an error code) if this is the appropriate action for that endpoint. Returned JSON should follow the JSON Body Hallmarks.
4.Set a maximum size for the unit of work

There should be a maximum size for the unit of work being requested from a service. This should be clearly stated in the API documentation. The max size for unit of work will ensure that each request takes a similar amount of time and resources to complete. This maximum size will often need to be
often need to be defined on a case by case basis, but it is important that there is a size limit so that clients will not be able to overload the API with a single Listed query.

Use of HTTP codes.

The response is not limited to these codes. Teams should determine the appropriate HTTP code to respond to specific cas

2xx - Success - No retry required

Example: - 200 - use when all IDs successfully return a JSON object; this can include the case of returning an empty body when there are no matching objects - 204 - use when there are no matching objects for any of the IDs - 207 - use when some IDs are successful and some have failed; include a

failed; include a list of failed IDs so that the client can identify between failures (example: get IDs 1 and 2; 1 is successful and 2 fails; return object 1 and list of failed IDs: [2]) or non-existent objects (example: get 1 and 2; 1 is successful and 2 does not exist; return object 1)

3xx - Redirect - Retry with considerations

4xx - Client Error - Retry with considerations

Example: - 400 - use when the number of requested objects exceeds the limit; Include a message to explain the issue and inform the client of the limit - 403 - use if there is a security reason to block the whole request (example: if client is trying to access data they do not have rights to); we do
rights to); we do not recommend people provide security implementations 'per object returned' and so therefore security is an 'all blocked or nothing blocked' concept.

5xx - Retry original request

Character encoding and language

Responses MAY include charset and language headers if applicable (note that the application/json media type does not support charset attributes, but assumes UTF-8). Example:

Content-Type: application/xml;charset=UTF-8
  Content-language: en

Spelling

APIs MUST use American spelling. Our APIs SHOULD be designed for potential third-party consumption in the future. Users and developers outside of the UK will be more familiar with US spelling, as evidenced by the Java JDK using American spelling in its APIs. Additional arguments for using US
for using US spelling can be found in the this StackOverflow post.

Naming ID fields

A call to a basket service that includes a basket ID as part of the response body SHOULD name the field basketId rather than just id.

Representing dates and times
Dates and times MUST be represented as UTC using ISO 8601. Use the appropriate level of resolution for your use-case (e.g. milliseconds, or seconds only).

Start dates (and date times) are inclusive. End dates (and date times) are exclusive. If a record is supposed to be "active" forever, this SHOULD be represented by having a null end date (or date time), rather than an arbitrarily high value.

Representing decimals

Exponents MUST NOT be used because some clients may not support them.

Representing money

Monetary values MUST be represented as strings in APIs. Monetary APIs must include the currency if there is any risk of ambiguity. If currency is included, the API MUST use the following form:

The currency field MUST contain the ISO 4217 currency code. The amount field minor units MUST correspond to the currency definition. Amount field precision examples: 1. GBP has 2 minor units so the amount should be: 10.00 2. JPY has 0 minor units so the amount should be: 10

Applications MUST NOT deserialise the amount field into a float, it may cause rounding errors.

For requests with multiple price occurrences, pass the currency in a request/response body:

Alternative units

If a field MAY represent a currency or MAY represent a non-monetary equivalent (for example, loyalty points), a domain-specific structure SHOULD be used. This structure MUST include the unit of measure if there is any risk of ambiguity.

Representing quantity units

The quantity MUST NOT be converted into non-SI units for storage, because of the potential for rounding errors. Conversion may happen for display.

Validation

Validation

The identifier values MUST be validated structurally (e.g. against a regex), and MAY also be validated in other ways (e.g.: checking that the entity exists in a downstream system, or performing ID conversion between internal <-> external ID and structurally validating the result).

The response code from a validation failure depends on the origin where the value encountered a validation failure: - Path parameter - For GET / DELETE methods 404 Not Found status MUST be returned - For PUT / POST 400 Bad Request status MUST be returned - GET with a query parameter: - If a single

- If a single entity is normally returned, then the response MUST be an error with 404 Not Found status (consider using path params for this case) - If an array is returned and no entities are found, then the response MUST be an empty array with 200 OK status - Other HTTP operations with query

with query parameter: 400 Bad Request status MUST be returned - Request body - 400 Bad Request MUST be returned - Response body (e.g.: a converted ID that was obtained from a downstream service) - 500 Internal Server Error status MUST be returned

?> NOTE: For external APIs we aim to avoid accidentally disclosing internal IDs or IDs of other tenants. In some cases this may require further design, in addition to the above recommendations.

HTTP status codes and error messages
Most API providers only use a small subset of the 70 HTTP status codes (see the Apigee API design ebook for more info). Ocado APIs should limit themselves to the following status codes:

Code Meaning 200 OK 201 Created 202 Accepted 204 No Content 304 Not Modified 307 Temporary Redirect (be aware that not all REST clients follow redirects) 400 Bad request, bad syntax, or validation errors, i.e. if retailerXId in body is not found 401 Unauthorised (really means unauthenticated -

unauthenticated - missing or invalid credentials) 403 Forbidden (credentials found, but user not authorised to access this resource) 404 Not Found, i.e. if retailerXId in path is not found 405 Method Not Allowed 409 Conflict 429 Too Many Requests 500 Internal server error 503 Service unavailable
Service unavailable (server level problem, not resource level problem)

If an error occurs the HTTP return code should reflect this. In addition, if the error originates from your code (as opposed to a load balancer for example), the body of the response SHOULD also include this info: - httpStatus - the HTTP status code (as a number), duplicated in the body to aid

in the body to aid readability during testing - message - a developer-friendly message for logging, in English. - code - an API specific code for the type of error that has occurred. The list of possible error codes that can be returned can be considered part of the API and should be documented as
be documented as part of the API.

and MAY contain this info: - moreInfo - link to a documentation URL for more info on the error

For example:

{
    "httpStatus" : 403,
    "message":"Access to shopping list forbidden for customer-id 43847",
    "code": "SHOP-123",
    "moreInfo": "http://www.twilio.com/docs/errors/SHOP-123"
}

Some API calls may return multiple errors, for example POSTs may fail multiple validation errors. For these calls return a list of errors. Each API call SHOULD consistently return either a single error, or a list of errors, so the client does not need to check the type of the return data (i.e.
return data (i.e. whether it's a list or not). This SHOULD be included in the documentation for the API call.

Error codes MUST be considered part of the API (and documented as such), so applications MUST NOT directly pass error codes on from downstream to upstream systems. Intermediate systems have no control over which error codes it may return. Error messages SHOULD also not be propagated directly, as

directly, as this risks exposing internal implementation details. Catch-all error codes for downstream error codes SHOULD NOT be used, so that information about the error is provided to upstreams. Error handling for each downstream error code SHOULD be considered individually.
Locale

When serialising locales, applications MUST use format of a two-letter ISO 639-1 language code followed by dash and a two-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 region code. This format is compatible with RFC 5646 ("language tag") format. This standard means using "en-GB", not "en_GB" which is the default
is the default serialization in Java.

Also use the above format when representing locale data in APIs.

Country code

Applications MUST use the ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 two-letter country code. It is the most widely used country code standard.

Asynchronous API calls - DRAFT

Some external API calls are asynchronous with processing of the call. By this we mean the API responds immediately, but processing of the request is performed asynchronously. In these cases, the API MUST allow the user to query the processing status of the asynchronous request. The mechanism will
The mechanism will be:

Asynchronous API calls

Where statusCode is one of:

PENDING - processing has not started

PROCESSING - processing is in progress

COMPLETED_OK - processing completed with no errors

ERRORED - an error was encountered during processing
In the case of ERRORED status, details of the error SHOULD be provided as described in HTTP status codes and error messages.

The status associated with an asyncProcessingId SHOULD be retained for a reasonable period of time. This will vary from API to API, and SHOULD be included in the API documentation.

Pagination

Pagination

Note: we MUST use streaming pagination for our external APIs. It's not yet clear whether it's worth the effort to standardise on pagination for internal APIs.

An API call result SHOULD be paginated if the result payload is large enough that sending it unpaginated might impact the performance or reliability of an API client, or the API server itself.

There are two approaches to splitting results for pagination:

A page based approach - use for data that will not change whilst being iterated over

A streaming based approach - use for DynamoDB backed APIs where you need pagination, but you don't know in advance how many results will be returned

A cursor based approach - use for time ordered real time data that may be appended to whilst the user is paging through the results

Further details and implementations for these types of pagination can be found below.

API documentation MUST mention if an API returns paginated results. APIs can be assumed to use page based pagination unless otherwise mentioned.

Streaming (e.g. dynamodb backed) pagination

Note: this pagination type is used in some of our external APIs.

These standards SHOULD only be applied if the API is paginated. The API documentation MUST indicate if the API is paginated. Do not return "metadata" in the body if the API is not paginated - just return the content at the top level of the JSON.

This type of pagination SHOULD be used for DynamoDB backed APIs where you need pagination but don't know in advance how many results will be returned.

Request query params are all optional and as follows:

maxPageSize - default 20 (or any other sensible value, which should be documented)

pageToken - meaning resume iteration from that point exclusively, regardless if it is some sort of id or not

sortOrder - asc, desc - case insensitive (no default value)

Response body params are as follows:

sortOrder - string value telling the order direction asc/desc (optional, default order should be documented as well as column name on which results are being sorted, if it is a scan operation that does not return results in any particular order then it should be documented as well).

nextPageToken - string value continuation token (optional).

next - string with fully constructed link to next portion of results to make it easier for clients as they would not have to build the link themselves (optional).

Page based pagination

Page based pagination SHOULD be used where the full result set will not change whilst a client is iterating over it. Page based pagination is not suitable for a resultset that might change whilst a client is paging through it. Use of page base pagination whilst the result set changes can result in:

The same record being returned on more than one page

A record not being returned on any page

Page pagination MUST implement page and maxPageSize URL parameters:

https://my.server/v1/my-api/my-resource?page=5&maxPageSize=20

where: - page - specifies which page number to start returning, inclusive. It's zero-indexed and MUST default to zero if not present. - maxPageSize - specifies the maximum number of records to return (for example, page=5 maxPageSize=10 will return records 50-59 inclusive). The API will return

The API will return exactly this number of records, unless there are fewer records than that remaining to return. The size MUST default to 20 if not present, unless a different default limit is specified in the API documentation.

Response body params are as follows:

  {
  "content": [...],
  "metadata": {
    "page": ...,
    "totalPages": ...,
    "totalCount": ...,
    "next": ...,
    "previous": ...,
    "first": ...,
    "last": ...,
  }
}

page - the current page number.

totalPages - the total number of pages.

totalCount - the total number of records across all the pages.

next - the URL to the next page (optional).

previous - the URL to the previous page (optional).

first - the URL to the first page (optional).

last - the URL to the last page (optional).

Cursor based pagination

Note: This pagination type is still in draft - it's not clear if anyone needs this pagination type.

If an API is returning time ordered real time data that might be appended to whilst a client is paging through it then cursor based pagination SHOULD be used instead of page based pagination.

Note - to bootstrap our Hallmarks for cursor based pagination I suggest we copy what Twitter are doing, so this doc references a copy of the Twitter API docs for now (if necessary we can then write our own version of this doc).

Cursor based pagination must implement max_id, count, and optionally since_id parameters:

https\://my.server/v1/my-api/my-resource?max_id=1030&count=20&since_id=1015

Our APIs MUST interpret these parameters consistent with how Twitter interpret them in their APIs, as detailed in Twitter Timeline API document, including the instruction in the "Optimizing max_id for environments with 64-bit integers" section to subtract 1 from max_id to avoid any result being
any result being returned more than once.

Sorting

Sorting:

MUST be controlled using a sort parameter

The value of sort MUST represent sort fields

Multiple sort fields MAY be specified by providing them as a comma-separated list

Sort fields MUST be applied in the order specified

The sort order MUST be ascending unless it is prefixed with a minus (U+002D HYPHEN-MINUS, "-"), in which case it MUST be descending

Here is an example:

https://my.server/v1/my-api/my-resource?sort=name,-title

Direct personal data in URIs

To avoid Information exposure through query strings in url, direct personal data MUST NOT appear in the URI, neither in the path nor in query parameters. For example, querying by customerFullName could (insecurely) be implemented as:

  GET /v1/customers?fullName=John%20Smith

but must instead be in the body to be secure:

  POST /v1/customersByFullName
  {
    "fullName": "John Smith"
  }

POST MUST be used instead of GET as many frameworks do not support GET requests with bodies.

Distributed tracing

Why do we need distributed tracing?

Assume we have systems A, B, C and D making REST API calls like so:

A calls B, B calls C and also calls D

There are three API calls there. If the call into system D fails then that failure may propagate back to system A. A, B, C and D may all be owned by different teams, so it will be important for team A to know where the original failure occurred without having to ask teams B and D to trace the call
D to trace the call through their logs.

OpenSearch will allow log searching across systems, but unless there is some way to find all the logs relating to the B -> D call that resulted from the original A call, it will not be quick to find the root cause.

Distributed tracing of HTTP calls via RequestId

When A calls B it SHOULD place a UUID in an HTTP header called "RequestId":

  A -> B   RequestId: b422d610-1290-11e4-9191-0800200c9a66

This UUID will change each time A makes a call to B. For all calls that B makes as a result of the call from A it should take the UUID in the A header append three characters > (that is "[space]>[space]") and then another UUID. Let's call this a composition of UUIDs. Here is what the HTTP header in
the HTTP header in B's calls will look like:

By also setting the UUID composition as a RequestId MDC variable in the log4j / logback logging context like so:

  MDC.put("requestId", "b422d610-1290-11e4-9191-0800200c9a66 > 22afb924-7703-4240-9b55-2e7fddf87e7e");
then app will also log the UUID composition as a field in each OpenSearch log record too.

Now when team A sees a problem with their request, they can search OpenSearch for all events containing b422d610-1290-11e4-9191-0800200c9a66. They will find all web requests and log entries relating to that request from systems A, B, C and D, all in one go. They should then be able to filter the
able to filter the logs for errors, and see an error occurred first in system D.

If the system call chain is longer - for example - system D then calls into system E when servicing the request from B:

A calls B, B calls C and also calls D. Finally, D calls E

then another UUID should be appended, for example:

The RequestId header is mandatory for all systems to ensure we can get end-to-end diagnostics. To ensure missing headers are picked up at the earliest opportunity during testing, any call without a RequestId header SHOULD be considered an invalid request, with the exception of:

/health-check URL - load balancers may call this URL but will not normally be able to send RequestId headers

non-Javascript requests originating from web browsers (i.e. end user HTTPS requests for web pages)

Capitalisation of requestId / RequestId

The MDC field is requestId (lowercase "r"). This is in line with all other fields being logged to OpenSearch.

The HTTP request header is RequestId (uppercase "R"). This is in line with HTTP header standards.

The New Relic transaction attribute is RequestId (uppercase "R").

Distributed tracing over SQS

Some call chains include hops over SNS/SQS as well as HTTP, e.g.

App W calls app X over HTTP. App X calls app Y over SQS. App Y call app Z over HTTP

It's useful to be able to trace this whole call chain in OpenSearch. It's also useful to be able to see which of the hops are made over SQS, because these hops will often be async, so may not be actioned immediately.

This can be achieved by adding an SQS message attribute called RequestId to all SQS messages, following the same pattern as for HTTP calls (detailed above).

For the example chain above (appW, appX, appY, appZ), the requestIds might look like this:

W -> X  RequestId:  c191b892-5786-41cc-acab-7b702c5a3d4e

   X -> Y  RequestId: c191b892-5786-41cc-acab-7b702c5a3d4e > 05eb5cda-c5ef-4e7e-8c4f-d8aaa2539418

   Y -> Z  RequestId:  c191b892-5786-41cc-acab-7b702c5a3d4e > 05eb5cda-c5ef-4e7e-8c4f-d8aaa2539418 > 1ee97356-6dab-4461-86cf-f325f51b188a

Distributed tracing in logging and monitoring tools

The RequestId HTTP header or SQS message attribute SHOULD be populated in all tools used for distributed tracing, including OpenSearch and New Relic.
