Code management

All teams SHOULD be using merge requests as the standard mechanism for making changes to the main branch of their codebase. There's no need to change what you're doing right now, but it's worth thinking about moving over to this approach for the benefits outlined.

Background

Following the deprecation of several old source control systems, we now have all of our application code stored in the centrally-run GitLab instance. With this in mind, it's worth having some shared guidelines on how best to make use of GitLab and, more generally, how we should be managing our code
managing our code so that we can; benefit from having shared practices, build upon those shared practices with tooling, and be better prepared for future compliance and audit requirements.
What are the recommendations?

All teams SHOULD be using GitLab merge requests as the primary mechanism for making changes to the main branch of their codebase. These merge requests will often originate from branches in the same project, but could also come from forks of the project that others have made. Branches and forks
Branches and forks SHOULD be short-lived - they should only last as long as is needed to get the change merged to the main branch and be deleted shortly thereafter.

Why?

The code management recommendation has two main purposes: to enforce code review, and to unify the review evidence standards (using merge requests). It is expected that this strategy will make audits easier, and help us become SOC2 compliant.

There are a number of benefits to using this specific approach: - We can embed team process/policies into the merge request process e.g. - Merge request templates with a checklist of what to look for in code review - Requiring a certain number of people to approve a merge request (e.g. for

request (e.g. for compliance purposes; PCI, SOC 2, etc) - Explicit list of people who can accept a merge, e.g. based on training needed - Checking code style/quality requirements before the code hits the main branch - ideally this is an automated process, but might also be part of code review - We

of code review - We can squash multiple commits down into a single commit to the main branch, reducing noise but still allowing for frequent commits and testing on the build server during active development - Aligning with common practice from the open-source community moves us in the right
us in the right direction for more easily contributing, or potentially open-sourcing our own work

There are a number of benefits from aligning on a common organisation-wide approach: - Easing cross-team commits - consistency means clear expectations - Each increment in alignment moves us towards better induction and team mobility - the ultimate aim is to have a shared induction process that
process that means a new starter (or mover) doesn't need to learn lots of things that aren't your team's domain
?> Does my team need to immediately change how we manage our code? What about in the long term?

No. Definitely don't jump to make changes without first understanding what you might be losing by switching from your current process. This is a set of recommendations rather than a mandate or a set of requirements. We recommend that teams adopt this approach over time for the benefits stated
the benefits stated above. Some teams may need to move to an approach like this more urgently if there are specific requirements for their context.
?> Pushing to trunk/main branch is a key part of the Continuous Delivery approach; are we against the ideas behind Continuous Delivery in general?

Using Merge Requests is compatible with regular pushes to the main branch. We absolutely want as many benefits of a CI/CD approach as possible! A lot of the arguments for trunk-based development are to resolve a set of problems that arise from complicated branching strategies. These can also be

These can also be resolved by not having long-lived branches and prioritising reviews/merges over writing more code. A short-lived branch approach also allows for your code to be reviewed and run through your full test pipeline before the change ends up on the main branch so that your code remains

your code remains in a working, deployable state. We are suggesting that you merge to main often - potentially as often as you would with a CI/CD approach - but via a merge request mechanism, to realise all the benefits mentioned above.
?> Are you saying we should be using feature branches?

We aren't advocating for the use of feature branches unless, perhaps, your feature is a very small change. Generally speaking, when people hear the term "feature branch" and think of the associated merging pain, it's because they are long-lived branches that aren't merged back in or integrated with
or integrated with other changes regularly enough.
?> Isn't this going to lead to merging hell?

It shouldn't; merging complications tend to occur when there are lots of changes happening to a single code base at the same time. Well modularised code can also help mitigate these kinds of problems. Also, we should be trending towards smaller commits, deployed more frequently rather than batching
than batching up changes to be deployed together.
?> Is this going to discourage refactoring or make reviews slow and painful?

It shouldn't discourage refactoring. If the merge requests are small and frequent, refactoring as you go shouldn't make code review any more complicated than with a trunk-based development approach. If there is large refactoring to be done, make it a merge request of its own; ideally, pair with

ideally, pair with someone as you make the changes to make the review process simpler (or potentially not even needed). Code review for a large refactoring is hard; separating it from a functional change would be a kindness towards your teammates.
?> What about feature toggles? Does this mean we should stop using this approach to keep our code deployable?

On the contrary - feature toggles are a key part of the approach we take to managing the rollout of changes or features, the use of Merge Requests has no bearing on this. Depending on the context, they might be absolutely mandatory; a given feature or piece of functionality might need to be active
need to be active for one retailer but not another, for example. Feature toggles also allow us to deploy a change independently of when we want that change to be active.
?> I have a system that only exists in DEV/SIT environments. Does this apply to me as well?

All of the benefits of aligning on a common organisation-wide approach still apply, though you may be working in a context where the benefits of this specific approach don't apply or aren't worth the trade-off of switching. If you are confident in your test pipeline validating that your system is

that your system is safe to deploy, you may still get some benefit from having this validation run before the code hits the main branch. It's also worth considering if others rely on your system to do their job; it might not be "prod", but it's production for somebody and we should make sure that
make sure that we are putting adequate care into keeping things working and keeping things deployable.

Fully managed vs. semi-managed cloud services

We have a preference for fully managed cloud services - see Hallmark A2. Below we describe what we mean by fully managed and semi-managed services, give examples of each, and explain why we prefer fully managed services.

?> NOTE: the terminology "fully managed" and "semi-managed" is Ocado terminology. The cloud providers choose not to make this distinction in their documentation.

Fully managed cloud services

Fully managed cloud services are those that are exposed only via API endpoints. It is not apparent to the service user how the service is provisioned within the cloud environment. Any upgrades to fully managed services are managed by cloud provider staff, and are completely transparent to the
transparent to the service user. These upgrades are not normally communicated in any way. Fully managed services tend to be proprietary services.

We prefer fully managed services to semi-managed services because; - fully managed services tend to scale to higher volumes than semi-managed services. - fully managed services are typically cheaper at low volumes. - fully managed services are upgraded without the service user even knowing. - fully
knowing. - fully managed services are normally better integrated with the rest of the cloud provider’s ecosystem (e.g. IAM permissions).

The following Tech Stacks Adopt services are fully managed (not an exhaustive list): - Amazon Route 53 - Amazon Kinesis - Amazon DynamoDB - AWS KMS - Amazon SNS - Amazon SQS - Amazon S3 - AWS Lambda - Google BigQuery - Google Cloud Storage - Google Cloud Functions

Semi-managed cloud services

Semi-managed services are provisioned by deploying them to server instances (EC2/GCE). The service user may need to specify a maintenance window for upgrades. The service user can sometimes upgrade the service themselves. Semi-managed services tend to be open source projects that have been adapted
have been adapted to run in a cloud provider ecosystem.

The following Tech Stacks Adopt services are semi-managed (not an exhaustive list): - Amazon ElastiCache Redis - Amazon RDS Postgres

Single sign out and session expiration

All applications with a frontend layer where user can log in MUST handle single sign out and session expiration as described below.

Session expiration

If the user's application session has expired, show the session expired pop-up. If they change the browser tab to another within the same application, then this pop-up should also be visible there. If the user clicks the "Continue" button, they MUST be redirected to Panda to login. If single sign
If single sign on (Panda) session is still valid, the user is automatically logged in, otherwise present Panda login to the user.

After the user has logged back in, they SHOULD see the page they were previously on. In case the original page was a result of form submission (POST), the application SHOULD NOT fail with "405 Method not allowed" error, instead it SHOULD handle the situation gracefully. The session expired pop-up
expired pop-up MUST be acknowledged separately on each browser tab.
Single sign out

When the user clicks the logout button and has logged out from one application, then they MUST be logged out from all applications open within the same browser. If they change the browser tab to another with any application, then present the Panda login form to them. If the user logs in correctly

logs in correctly then they SHOULD see the main page of that application. After that, if they switch to a browser tab with any other application then they are automatically logged in and see the main page of the given application.

Unauthenticated requests - when a user request is unauthenticated, the unauthorized (403) page MUST be presented to them. For example, if a user tries to access a protected page without sufficient permissions.
Logged user changes - when user gets logged out, and after that a different user has logged in, then the application MUST NOT keep any data of previously logged user in session.

Background requests - the application MUST NOT issue any background requests after session expiration, or when user has logged out.

Implementation guide

!> NOTE: To achieve the expected behaviour, it's not enough to implement only some points described below. All applications that have some form of UI MUST implement all aspects of the logout.

Logging out from the application

After logout is initiated by clicking the logout button, the user MUST be redirected to the Panda login page. After a successful login, they MUST be redirected back to the main page of the application from which they logged out. Proper logout behaviour can be enabled by:

if your application uses the Jar-Jar Panda Security Edge module you can control - among other things - the logout behaviour by using logout redirect properties - see the Jar-Jar reference: Security Panda Edge for details.

if your application is Java-based and uses client-filter library, enabling allowPostLogoutRedirect parameter.

directly using Panda's logout endpoint.

Consuming logout events

Panda emits a logout event when user logs out from an application. This event is then available via Parrot for other applications to consume. Handling these events is crucial to achieve single sign out experience for users. Single sign out functionality can be enabled by:
if your application uses Jar-Jar Panda security parrot module this behaviour is enabled by default on OSP accounts - see the Jar-Jar reference: Security Panda Edge for details.

if your application is Java-based you can use the sso-event-receiver-lib-parrot library - this applies both to applications protected by web filter and backend services handling token verification without web filter.

To test if your application handles logout properly you can use User Management application to logout from the system and see if the event is handled by your application.

Handling session expiration and logout in front-end

The previous two sections describe requirements for the application backend. However, to notify users about session expiration or logout, the application frontend has to be able to detect such events and act accordingly.

The Design System provides a session management component - it can poll application for session status, to ensure that the application reacts to session expiration and logout events. The library allows registering custom listeners for those events. This can be used to stop any background polling

background polling after the user logs out, for example. It also provides logout method which should be used for proper logout handling. See the library documentation for instructions on how to enable it in your application.

In order for the library to work correctly, the application backend MUST expose a session status endpoint.

If your application uses Jar-Jar Panda Security Edge - this feature is supported (thanks to client-filter). See the Jar-Jar reference: Security Panda Edge for details.

If your application is Java-based and uses client-filter library (version 5.0.0+), then the status endpoint is by default available at <SSO filter context>/sessionStatus.pandasso URL. This location can be customized by setting sessionStatusEndpoint property.

If your application is not java-based you need to provide your own implementation of the endpoint.

Session status endpoint

This endpoint is periodically polled by the session management component to determine user session status.

Query parameters: pandaSessionId (optional) - the id of Panda session which status should be returned. This will be null on first invocation of the endpoint by the session management component. For subsequent calls the value of pandaSessionId returned by the endpoint will be used (if any).

Response: Content-Type: application/json

Body:

{
  "sessionStatus": "<status>",
  "pandaSessionId": "<Panda session id>"
}

<status> is one of:

VALID - if the session (application and Panda) is still valid.

SESSION_EXPIRED - if the session (application or Panda) has expired; this means that either application state is lost and user cannot continue (application session expired) or user has to re-authenticate (Panda session expired).
LOGGED_OUT - if the user was logged out; this means that user either clicked logout button in this application, or the application received a logout event from Parrot for the Panda session.

<Panda session id> is the id of Panda session returned by the token endpoint when authorization code is exchanged for the access token. It can be omitted if the Panda session id is not known which can happen if pandaSessionId query parameter is not provided and the endpoint is not able to determine
able to determine Panda session id for the request.

Remarks

The status code of the response SHOULD be 200 OK.

The endpoint MUST NOT require authorization.

When the endpoint is invoked without pandaSessionId query parameter, it MUST return the id of current Panda session (associated with the application session), otherwise it MUST always return the id and status of session identified by the query parameter. This means that if different Panda session
Panda session is associated with current application session, it MUST return SESSION_EXPIRED status, as this means, that previous session is no longer valid.

Ensure that calling the endpoint will not interfere with application session expiration timeout. Because the endpoint is called periodically, the application server will consider the application session as active and never expire it.

To ensure that the session status is always up-to-date, the response from the endpoint SHOULD NOT be cached by the browser. You can add appropriate HTTP headers to prevent caching. For example:

Cache-control: no-cache, no-store\
Pragma: no-cache\
Expires: 0

Encryption of personal data

Our Software Development Life Cycle (C2) indicates that:

“Direct Personal Data must only ever be persisted in encrypted form, using one of the Ocado Technology approved encryption standards.”

The InfoSec Ocado Encryption Standards document these standards, and focuses on “minimum standards for cryptographic primitives”. However it is not an implementation guide. This section of the Development Handbook provides guidance on how teams should implement encryption of personal data in OTP to
data in OTP to ensure correctness, and adhere to our encryption standards.

Data classification

The Data Classification Handbook can be used to identify and classify any piece of information in order to apply, control, and protect data adequately. The following classifications are defined:

Data Subject related Information

SENSITIVE PERSONAL DATA

DIRECT PERSONAL DATA

INDIRECT PERSONAL DATA

OTHERS (NON PERSONAL DATA)

Business related Information

COMMERCIALLY SENSITIVE DATA

PROPRIETARY DATA

OTHERS (BUSINESS OPERATIONS DATA)

Data states

Data can exist in different states:

In transit: Data actively being transmitted between two devices, including data sent over networks or other communication channels.

At rest: Data being stored on a device or in a storage system.

In use: Data that is being processed.

Types of data encryption
Encryption in transit encrypts data that is being transmitted between two devices.

Server-side encryption at rest encrypts data at the storage layer, leaving data protection in the hands of the service provider rather than the data owner since the encryption occurs after transmission. When server-side encrypted data is retrieved (e.g. through an API, via a SQL query, or by

a SQL query, or by reading a file on disk), it’s automatically decrypted before it’s returned. Server-side protection only protects the data against someone who only has direct physical access to the storage device.

Client-side encryption at rest is encryption that is applied to the data at the application layer before it is transmitted for storage. When client-side encrypted data is retrieved, the returned data will still be encrypted. You need to then subsequently decrypt the data as a separate step.
as a separate step. Client-side encryption protects against someone who has direct physical access to the storage device, and from someone who can access the data remotely.

Encrypting data in transit

All data must be encrypted in transit using TLS/SSL.

See Data Encryption in Transit (Guru) for information on how to implement encryption in transit.

Encrypting data at rest

For information on how to implement the different types of encryption, see the following Guru cards: - Encryption Types - Client-side and Server-side Encryption - Data Encryption at Rest in AWS - Data Encryption at Rest for Retailer Data Feeds in GCP

User experience

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #ux-support.

User feedback

Gathering user feedback is an essential practice of being customer-centered. It includes a well-established set of methods to provide insights on how satisfied users are with tools and services. Gathering user feedback is meant to be a continuous process and can take place at any stage of the
at any stage of the software development life cycle.

User feedback enables the team to iteratively improve the product/feature being developed with a clear understanding of how it's being used. This gives the product the best chance of success as it will best fit with the users' lifestyle or workflows. The outcome of user feedback is an important

is an important input for prioritising planned features. The insight built enables teams to remove subjectivity out of the design, and development decisions, by creating a common understanding between team members on where they need to improve or add development efforts. The creation of innovative
of innovative solutions becomes easier as the team has a good understanding of users’ pain points and needs.

If you do not gather user feedback:

users are more prone to make errors.

you increase your product support burden.

you may prioritise work that matters less to your users.
you may incur costly development mistakes - the cheapest way to get feedback is by drawing your ideas out for the users on a piece of paper!

How can I start gathering user feedback? To gather user feedback you need access to the right users, and a research plan, including the appropriate method to use. Contact your local UX researcher for assistance. If you don’t have a UX researcher in your team, ask for assistance on
for assistance on #ux-user-feedback-sig.

Contacting OSP retail users

Involve the UX team in all OSP retail user feedback processes. Contact the relevant Solutions product manager (not the Solutions manager) to ask for time with the client to gather user feedback. Provide the Solutions product manager with:

the context for the request.

who they would like to speak to (roles/departments).

format of the session(s) they would like to run.

number of sessions.

session length.

The Solutions product manager will request a feedback session with retailer users. The retailer may not always agree to such sessions.

Common feedback methods and tools

Choose the right methods - they vary according to the type of users, the stage of the project and the type of assumptions you are trying to validate. The UX research (UXR) team can help you with this step. See common methods for details.

Most of the qualitative user feedback can be built by simply giving users relevant tasks, then observing them accomplishing the tasks with your product or current tools. You can also conduct a contextual study where you shadow and observe users in their environment, which you won’t need
you won’t need sophisticated tools for. However, there are specialised tools to enable you further - see a list of tools you can access with the UXR team.
Analyse success stories from various teams to discover how user feedback has been instrumental for the team's success.

Local machine configuration

If you have questions related to this section, ask them on #security-sig Slack channel.

Zero trust networking / VPN

You SHOULD have Zero Trust tunneling enabled on your local machine at all times, both when you work remotely and when you use the office network. Zero Trust tunneling mitigates the impact of any local machine compromise from a virus, malware, or phishing. It also ensures all traffic is encrypted
is encrypted over the local network, which reduces the chance of man-in-the-middle network attacks.

Local secrets management

See Secrets management for general advice on the management of secrets. When storing secrets locally, you SHOULD follow these guidelines:

Keep the main copy of the secrets in Keeper.

Use pass to manage the passwords locally.

Use direnv to load and unload project-specific environment variables, depending on the current directory.

Store secrets in a .envrc file at the top of each project directory and use pass to retrieve the passwords: export API_TOKEN=$(pass path/to/api/token).

Make sure .envrc is listed in .gitignore. You can add it to the user-level global git ignore file, so it doesn't need to be added per project.

Use Gitleaks, especially its pre-commit hook, to prevent secrets from being added to GitLab.

Localhost port binding

When you develop locally, some tools need to bind to a port on your local machine. This is common when running a local server, such as a web server or a database. Normally, you only want to connect to these ports from a browser or another app running on the same machine. In such cases, you MUST

cases, you MUST make sure the port is only bound to the local loopback interface (localhost or 127.0.0.1) and NOT to 0.0.0.0. This ensures that the port is not accessible to others through the local network and reduces the risk of unauthorized access to your machine. To learn how to bind to the
how to bind to the local loopback interface, read the documentation for the software you use.

Local developer tools installation

When you install software on your local machine, you MUST:

Use a package manager to install software when possible. For example, use brew on macOS, or apt on Ubuntu.

Only install software from trusted sources. For example, use the official package repositories or download from the official software website.

Keep software up to date. Check for updates regularly. Install them as soon as possible.
You SHOULD periodically review and remove the software you no longer need, to reduce the attack surface of your machine.

Autoscaling

Principles:

Aim for elastic services, scaling up as needed and scaling down as they can.

Aim for zero ongoing developer interaction required. Scale up and down automatically.
Consider using Target tracking autoscaling that automatically increases or decreases the current capacity of the group based on a target value for a specific metric.

Optimise for meeting the availability SLO while minimising cost. If it costs too much, consider alternatives and reevaluate the availability SLO.

Alongside setting hard limits (maximum instances), implement different alert thresholds for 24/7 and office-hours alerting.

Implement INFO level alerting on any metrics used for autoscaling, and on failed autoscaling.

Focus on your application’s bottleneck, but don’t neglect other dimensions.

Test against realistic load profiles, informed by production, using component performance tests:

total volume

total volume

rate of increase (spikiness)

rate of decrease (elasticity)

Be tolerant of availability zone outages, bearing in mind your recovery time objective and maximum tolerable outage. This may require some overprovisioning.

The same autoscaling configuration should be deployed to all accounts, while resulting in fewer instances for accounts with less load. In RTEs this should be overridden to be exactly one instance for cost savings.

If configuring autoscaling effectively for your applicaton presents a challenge, consider alternatives and whether a serverless alternative is more suitable. Especially for databases and spiky workloads where high utilisation of provisioned throughput is hard to achieve. They are often more
They are often more performant, cheaper, and don’t require autoscaling to be configured, though they do still have scaling speed limits.

Further reading: - When AWS Autoscale Doesn’t

Monitoring and alerting for autoscaling

Number of instances

The number of instances for applications has three limits:
Hard limit - maximum number of instances. This should be set very high (controlled with monitoring and alerting). Setting a hard limit low risks making a service unavailable.

24/7 alert limit - threshold that indicates it is very likely that autoscaling rules have been configured incorrectly and a lot of money is being wasted. This should be set at a value where the cloud costs truly justify immediate action. With maximum instances being configured to be very high, this

be very high, this threshold should be configured significantly below that limit. This also ensures we are not at risk of being limited by the maximum number of instances. Hitting this threshold results in an immediate out-of-hours alert for the issue to be investigated.

Office-hours alert limit - threshold that indicates it is quite likely that autoscaling rules have been configured incorrectly, but not too much money is being wasted, and investigation can wait until in-office hours. Hitting this threshold results in an office hours notification for the issue to

for the issue to be investigated. This limit should be set significantly below the 24/7 threshold to minimise the risk of an out-of-office-hours alert. This alert should not be expected to trigger based on the testing of the autoscaling rules. If this alert triggers, we should reevaluate the
reevaluate the autoscaling rules and the alert thresholds.

Where autoscaling is implemented, do not set alert thresholds too low. As we scale up our partners, we will need more instances running. The response to an in office-hours alert can also easily be to increase the alert thresholds.

Scaling metrics

Any metrics (for example DynamoDBReadCapacityUtilization) that have autoscaling rules against them should also have alert thresholds set against them. Following the principle of alerting on symptoms not causes, this should only be an INFO level alert, if there is sufficient application metric

application metric (latency or availability) or business metric level alerting instrumented. When an application or business metric alert has been triggered, an INFO level alert of scaling metrics being not within expected bounds is useful information for investigating the cause and resolving the
and resolving the incident.

?> NOTE: Following the principle of alerting on symptoms not causes, use failed autoscaling as an INFO level alert to aid investigation.

Application autoscaling

Application autoscaling supports three types of scaling. These are detailed below.

Target tracking scaling policies

Target tracking scaling policy lets you specify a scaling metric and a target value that should be maintained at all times. Application autoscaling creates and manages the CloudWatch alarms that trigger the scaling policy, and calculates the scaling adjustment based on the metric and the target

and the target value. The scaling policy adds or removes capacity as required to keep the metric at, or close to, the specified target value. In addition to keeping the metric close to the target value, a target tracking scaling policy also adjusts to changes in the metric due to a changing load
to a changing load pattern.

Unlike Step scaling, Target tracking doesn't require you to specify the amount by which to scale as autoscaling determines the number of containers to add/remove. This also helps applications to avoid specifying a very high number of min instances thereby reducing the AWS cost significantly.

When you create a target tracking policy, specify the target value of the metric and is usually the value in a 1 minute period.

The following are the 3 metrics currently supported for Target tracking * ALBRequestCountPerTarget * ECSServiceAverageCPUUtilization * ECSServiceAverageMemoryUtilization

Scale-in and Scale-out cool down period must be specified.

More details on how to deploy an application in Chancellor using Target tracking can be found here

Step scaling policies

Step scaling allows choosing scaling metrics and threshold values for the CloudWatch alarms that trigger the scaling process. You can define how your scalable target should be scaled when a threshold is in breach for a specified number of evaluation periods.

When you create a step scaling policy, add one or more step adjustments that enable you to scale based on the size of the alarm breach. Each step adjustment specifies the following:

a lower bound for the metric value (percentage or absolute value)

an upper bound for the metric value (percentage or absolute value)

the amount by which to scale, based on the scaling adjustment type; this can be ChangeInCapacity, PercentChangeInCapacity (most common), or ExactCapacity (not supported in OTP)

Scale-out and scale-in must be configured, and cooldown periods must be specified.

Chancellor supports a number of metrics for ECS scaling, with a single step scaling policy against a single metric, using ChangeInCapacity or PercentChangeInCapacity.

Scheduled scaling

Scaling based on a schedule allows you to set your own scaling schedule according to predictable load changes. For example, let's say that every week the traffic to your web application starts to increase on Wednesday, remains high on Thursday, and starts to decrease on Friday. You can configure a
You can configure a schedule for application autoscaling to increase capacity on Wednesday and decrease capacity on Friday.

Scheduled scaling should be avoided if possible (see Alternatives to step scaling and scheduled scaling), but may be needed to handle very spiky traffic profiles that target tracking scaling policies and step scaling policies cannot handle alone. Chancellor does not currently support scheduled
support scheduled scaling directly, though it can be achieved by scheduling updates to the scaling policy in GitLab pipelines.
CloudWatch detailed monitoring

CloudWatch detailed monitoring provides a one-minute resolution rather than five-minute for faster reaction at an extra cost. Contrast the resolution with the spikiness of traffic, costs of additional over-provisioning, how long it takes to add new instances, how many instances you launch per
you launch per scale-up, and cooldown period.

Relational Database Service (RDS)

Storage autoscaling

RDS supports autoscaling for storage but not for compute and memory as dedicated instances are used.

For general purpose storage, I/O capacity is provided and scaled as a function of storage size. Autoscaling for storage should be enabled, and monitoring and alerting should also be instrumented. Consider both burst balance and baseline throughput. For new databases, the initial capacity should be

capacity should be set high enough to provide enough burst and baseline throughput, informed by load testing. The burst balance can hide eventual resource issues if load testing is not run for long enough to exhaust the burst balance.

When general purpose storage does not provide sufficient I/O capacity without over-provisioning storage, consider using provisioned IOPS.

See Managing capacity automatically with Amazon RDS storage autoscaling for further details.

IOPS and instance manual scaling

Since RDS instances and IOPS cannot be configured to automatically scale up (except alongside general purpose storage), nor read replicas to automatically scale out, manual scaling must be performed, triggered by monitoring and alerting.

In-office hours alert thresholds must be set where normally an autoscaling threshold would be set. It must provide enough advance warning for manual scaling to be performed, including time for liaising with Cloud Support. For example, providing up to 3 days of headroom before the utilisation would
utilisation would trigger a 24/7 alert.

A 24/7 alert level threshold must also be set as a last resort, in case load picks up faster than anticipated in the in-office hours alert. This should provide more than sufficient headroom to allow manual action to take place in time.

Recommendations for choosing storage configuration and instance type for RDS

For recommendations on the storage and node type, see Choosing storage type, storage size, iops and Choosing instance type in the Chancellor documentation.

Kinesis

Kinesis

Kinesis supports on-demand capacity and manually provisioned capacity without autoscaling.

Kinesis shards cost approximately 150 USD per year. On-demand capacity is more simple, and in most cases more cost-effective (unless your application uses a lot of Kinesis capacity and the traffic profile is smooth enough to achieve high utilisation).

Take this into account when deciding between provisioning a fixed number of shards manually, creating cron jobs to update provisioned shards, and implementing autoscaling. When manually scaling, similar monitoring and alerting should be implemented as for RDS manual scaling. If you foresee
If you foresee significant cost savings by implementing autoscaling, contact your stream architect.

The main OTP use case for Kinesis is analytics events. When producing data events to Kinesis, at-least-once delivery must be guaranteed. Applications using the Event Producer Library can optimistically rely on the internal Event Producer Library buffer and rate-limiting without increasing the
increasing the number of potentially dropped events. You can hence trade-off latency, throughput, and provisioned capacity.

Monitoring and alerting should be instrumented. See the EPL monitoring and alerting documentation for more information on EPL provided metrics. Monitoring and alerting should also be instrumented on utilisation and capacity.

Load balancers

Load balancers

OTP uses AWS Application Load Balancers for applications, which automatically and without configuration scales up at a rate of up to 50% per 3 minutes from a baseline of 1000 requests per second.

Availability zone and instance redundancy

Hallmarks C1 and C6 indicate that:

C1. Application design MUST tolerate server instances failing regularly (i.e. daily).

C6. Cloud apps and associated services MUST be tolerant of an availability zone outage. Apps must run in at least 2 availability zones.

Prefer distributing instances over all three availability zones. Chancellor does this automatically.

Consider your recovery time objective and maximum tolerable outage when configuring headroom and minimum instances. Availability zone outages are uncommon, but instance shutdowns are more common.

When there is an availability zone outage, consider whether you need zero impact or just recovery within a certain amount of time. Consider how long it would take for your applications to start up in a different availability zone, and the potential impact of being unable to start new instances in
new instances in another availability zone because all of AWS’s other customers are trying to do the same.

Having minimal impact with one availability zone being unavailable requires provisioning 50% extra instances at all times if they are distributed evenly across availability zones. This costs 50% extra, and is typically not required to achieve the availability SLO.

Consider slightly over-provisioning application instances to cater for routine instance shutdowns. In this scenario, being able to support losing x% of application instances requires over-provisioning by 1 / (1-x).

Tuning and testing for step scaling

Look at production for a realistic traffic profile. In particular the most challenging period for scaling. This is not necessarily the highest load period, but is more likely the period where the traffic increases percentage-wise the fastest. Replicate the production scale-up profile in component
in component performance tests with a bit of headroom (for example traffic ramping up 20% faster than seen in production).

Look at resource utilisation in production to identify what the bottleneck is on the current instance size you are using. This is commonly CPU, memory, or network. The focus should be on the bottleneck as this is what will drive autoscaling, but other metrics should not be neglected. Scaling on
Scaling on request count or latency are alternatives, but are trickier to tune.

Determine what the effective maximum utilisation of the bottleneck is. For CPU this won’t be 100%. To enable efficient scheduling and predictable latency, the maximum effective utilisation rate may only be e.g. 70%, and going above might reduce performance.

Set initial scaling policy parameters and test using component performance tests. For example if your traffic can increase by 50% in 10 minutes, and your cooldown period is set to 10 minutes, then a scale up must provide the 50% extra capacity (plus a bit of headroom). If the maximum efficient
maximum efficient utilisation rate is 70%, then a target of 70% / 1.5 = 46% minus headroom could be 40%, which would allow a 50% increase over 10 minutes to not exceed 70% utilisation.

During component performance tests look at request success rate, latency percentiles, and resource utilisation during the traffic ramp up to ensure they remain satisfactory and that Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are met. Iterate on the autoscaling parameters until a good configuration is found.
is found. Optimise for availability first, followed by utilisation to minimise costs for a given availability target.

Sometimes it is sensible to make a tradeoff informed by SLOs, especially for rare and unpredictable spikes (e.g. unexpected spikes like voucher campaigns). These situations are a tradeoff between a delay in scaling that causes some unavailability or increased latency vs cost. For example spending
example spending $3000 extra per month in compute to protect against a spike that on average happens once per year, that would cause a 5 minute delay before scaling up, may not be worthwhile.

Be aware that autoscaling may need retuning when changing instance size or when production traffic profiles change. Keep component performance test and platform performance test scenarios up to date with production and include headroom.

If you are struggling with creating autoscaling that works well, consider Alternatives to step scaling and scheduled scaling

Alternatives to scaling

If you encounter difficulty configuring scaling policies to meet SLOs and remain cost effective, it is worth considering other options.
Serverless services

Serverless options are often a more elastic, lower management, more cost-efficient solution for your workload, without requiring autoscaling to be configured. Especially for DynamoDB where high utilisation of provisioned throughput is difficult to achieve, and on-demand capacity is trivial to
is trivial to enable. Serverless options can also be a good choice for spiky load profiles. For stable load profiles they are often more expensive.

Original Service Serverless Alternative Webservices on ECS Lambda DynamoDB Provisioned Capacity DynamoDB On-Demand Capacity RDS DynamoDB or S3

Asynchronous messages

If large spikes of requests come in at once, but can be handled asynchronously or with longer latency, it is worth considering SNS/SQS instead of REST/HTTP, as the queue will act as a buffer to smooth the load. SQS temporary queues can be used to implement request-response over SQS. Asynchronous
SQS. Asynchronous architectures bring additional challenges (for example testability and observability) so consider these alongside the scalability and performance considerations.

Rate limiting, load shedding, and backpressure

Another pattern is to require clients to not exceed maximum supported rate limits. This can be more of a challenge when there are multiple clients, but is a good option for external APIs.

If it is possible to keep within availability SLOs while rejecting some requests, then automated load shedding using Netflix concurrency limits is a useful pattern. This ensures that the system remains responsive and able to successfully process most requests without relying on client circuit

on client circuit breaking that might be delayed and not coordinated across clients or instances of individual applications. Load shedding also provide the service with a greater ability to recover from issues.

For both rate limiting and load shedding, backpressure can be applied using 429 Too Many Requests (for rate limiting) or 503 Service Unavailable (load shedding) and the Retry-After header. Client rate limiting and circuit breaking should respect these error codes.

Deadline propagation

By propagating a timeout deadline to downstream services, downstream services can reject or abort requests that have either already timed out in the originator, or that are unlikely to be responded to before the deadline is reached.

Graceful degradation

Fallbacks

Sometimes less resource intensive results can be returned. These can be implemented both on the server side and the client side. For example, drive time predictions normally use the computationally expensive algorithms to provide drive times, but straight line pathing is used as a fallback.

used as a fallback. Combined with load shedding, this can free up resources for other requests to succeed. Ensure fallback rates have appropriate monitoring and alerting, and that clients recover by getting more accurate results later once the service has recovered. If results are cached, then a
are cached, then a recovery mechanism to update to real values should be implemented.

Exercise caution when you implement fallbacks - they can introduce more problems than they solve. See Avoiding fallback in distributed systems from the Amazon Builders' Library for more information.

Traffic prioritisation

Some flows served by an application may be more critical than others. By selectively processing more critical flows with a higher priority, and/or load shedding less critical flows, resources are freed to process as many of the critical requests as possible.

Cloud cost optimisation

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #cloud-cost-optimisation-sig.

How to prioritise cloud cost reduction work

This page provides guidance to teams on when to invest in managing their application’s cloud costs. The intended audience are the teams in product development and EngProd.

Business Context

Ocado Technology is constantly improving its products and systems to satisfy existing customer commitments, improve our customer offering, and attract new customers. In most teams, there are things that could be done to reduce cloud costs through engineering improvements, but this may not be the
this may not be the most valuable work that team could do today. Product and Engineering management work together to ensure that we work on the highest value items at any given time.

Who has responsibility for monitoring cost?

Engineering and Product have joint responsibility for monitoring cost. At an app level, the entire team has responsibility for the cost of the app.

Techniques and tools for measuring cost change and improve over time. See the Cost optimisation resources section of this page for a list of resources. Resource updates will be announced on appropriate Slack channels.

Initiatives

Initiatives

There are occasionally large, prioritised initiatives to reduce cost, targeted at a certain technology or product. These should follow the standard Product process.

Cost hygiene

Cost hygiene

The cost hygiene guidelines described below should be followed at all times. They should be considered 'business as usual', regardless of whether the team are specifically prioritising cost reduction.

Design time

Design time

When designing a new application or feature, take a responsible approach to cost. Do not be wasteful. Consider how costs might increase as production volumes grow, but do not over-optimise prematurely.

Runtime

Runtime

You should monitor the runtime costs of your applications across all environments, including logging costs.

If an unexpected step change in cost is observed, it should be investigated. If it’s trivial to revert, it should be reverted. Revert such changes quickly, before the increased cost becomes considered a new baseline ‘normal’ cost.

If, in the course of monitoring costs, you spot a scenario where a trivial change will immediately result in a significant cost saving, make the change.

Ongoing technical improvements

Many teams have an agreed percentage of their capacity to be spent on technical improvement work. Engineering leaders and development teams can use this capacity for smaller pieces of cost-optimisation work, provided that this is the most valuable use of this time.

Unless agreeed with your product org, you should not invest in large initiatives to reduce cloud costs. Large changes should be added to the backlog for prioritisation, ideally with a cost saving analysis.

Unused or over-provisioned resources

It's not unusual to see costs incurred by resources that are over-provisioned, or by resources which are no longer required but have not been cleaned up. Periodically check to ensure expensive resources are 'right-sized', and that you do not have expensive resources provisioned that are no longer
that are no longer required.

Cost optimisation resources

Finops Dashboard - Looker studio report that shows all cloud costs broken down by GCP, AWS & third party costs that contribute to the delivery of OTP. Users can find costs that are associated with their Stream, Department, Team, and down to the individual level for Big Query.

Development Services invoice (beta) - shows per-stream costs across all accounts for a certain billing period. It allocates shared costs such as ECS/OpenSearch to streams.

WCS Costs - example CFC app costs analysis - point in time report that shows the costs incurred by the WCS app. Ideally this would be a Dashboard instead of a point-in-time snapshot but it does show the methodology, and demonstrates how to use the above reports to get a per-app cost.
Autoscaling guidance - how to configure your app so that it’s cheap to run at low loads, and scales up as load increases.

OTP Externalisation

If you have any questions related to this section, ask on the #application-security channel.

The OTP Externalisation process defines the steps that need to be taken to expose an API or UI to the public internet.

This process achieves two things:

Ensuring a new OSP application (UI- or API-based) CAN'T be externalized until:

it has undergone a pentest performed by an independent provider, and

all the Critical/High/Medium Security Issue Management issues featured in the pentest report have been addressed.

Establishing an audit trail that evidences the above steps have been followed. You can then use the evidence for audit purposes.

You can request a pentest through the #pentesting channel.

You MUSTN'T use the process in the following cases:

An already externalized application adds new endpoints.

The application is behind another system that's already externalized and behaves like a proxy, which means the application isn't externalized on its own.

The OTP Externalisation process

The OTP Externalisation process is tracked through the OTP Externalisation Jira project. Externalisation requests follow this workflow:

Externalisation JIRA Workflow

The Jira workflow has the following statuses:

OPEN: A new request to be populated with details about the resource to be externalised.

PENTEST REQUESTED: The pentest request form has been submitted for external pentest OR a pentest has been requested with the internal pentest team.

PENTEST COMPLETED: The pentest has been completed and the SIM tickets have been created. Jira will request the date of completion of the pentest (the date on the pentest report) when transitioning to this status.

EXTERNALISATION APPROVED: This is an automated transition, performed when our automation detects that SIM policy compliance is achieved, i.e all pentest related SIM tickets with Medium/High/Critical severity have been addressed (Closed or Risk Accepted).

APP EXTERNALISED: The application has been externalised. Jira will request the date on which the API/UI was externalised (i.e. the date of Chancellor deployment, or the date of friendly URL creation) when transitioning to this status.

OTP API Externalisation

To externalise an OTP API:

Create an OTP Externalisation Jira ticket.

Plan and request a pentest for the API. Refer to penetration testing for details. Change the status of the Jira ticket to 'Pentest Requested'.

While you are waiting for the pentest to start, make the API available in a production-like environment, such as Abundo PRD or Vector. This will require the externalisation switch to be flipped in the OTP Registry. This can be requested via #aws-support slack channel.

When the pentest is complete, change the status of the Jira ticket to 'Pentest complete'.

You MUST address all Medium/High/Critical severity SIM tickets raised in the pentest. When SIM tickets are in a compliant state, the Jira ticket is automatically moved to 'Externalisation Approved' status.

Externalise the API by deploying AWS API Gateway configuration to production account(s) using Chancellor.

OTP UI Externalisation

The following steps need to be completed by engineers in order to externalise a UI with a friendly URL. Note that an audit trail for this process is to be maintained using the OTP Externalisation Jira.

Create an OTP Externalisation Jira ticket.

Plan and request a pentest for the UI. Refer to penetration testing for details. Change the status of the Jira ticket to 'Pentest Requested'. Use the #pentesting Slack channel for any pentest-related coordination with SecOps (InfoSec).

While you are waiting for the pentest to start, make the UI available in a suitable AWS account.

If the UI pentesting is performed by the internal pentesting team, the following steps aren't required, as the team should already have access to the test environments.

Obtain the bounce credentials. Inform the pentest vendors that they need to use the bounce credentials with each request to OSP.

No additional steps are required if your application is being accessed through either of the following domains:

* `cymes-prd`: `cymesfood.osp.tech`, `abundo.osp.tech`, `api.cymesfood.osp.tech`, or `api.abundo.osp.tech`
* `cymes-sit`: `sit.cymesfood.osp.tech`, `sit.abundo.osp.tech`, `api.sit.cymesfood.osp.tech`, `api.sit.abundo.osp.tech`

NOTE: If the pentested UI is deployed outside of these accounts and domains, contact the pentest coordinators in the #pentesting Slack channel so that the required IP allowlisting could be requested on your behalf.

When the pentest is complete, change the status of the Jira ticket to 'Pentest complete'.

You MUST address all Medium/High/Critical severity SIM tickets raised in the pentest. When SIM tickets are in a compliant state, the Jira ticket is automatically moved to 'Externalisation Approved' status.

Externalize the UI by requesting a friendly URL in the relevant production AWS account(s).

You need to follow the same process for friendly URL creation requests in devtools environments.

Note: If the pentest report doesn't contain any vulnerabilities, that is, no SIM tickets are created, the automatic transition mentioned above doesn't work. In this scenario, the engineering teams should contact the AppSec team on the #application-security channel.

Technology choice strategy

We are trying to reduce the number of technologies we use in Ocado Technology. Having too many technologies in use means: - we are unable to properly support them all - we are too often using unsuitable or immature technologies in production environments - engineers find it harder to move between
to move between teams or streams

We aim to use a single technology of each type across Ocado Tech - one type of SQL database, for example. The chosen technologies are collectively called the Ocado Technology Platform (OTP). Each OTP technology is fully maintained, supported, secured, regularly updated and fully licensed.

buy-in will be sought before moving a technology to deprecated status.

The statuses reflect the current status of technologies within OTP. The statuses have been reviewed by Engineering Productivity and the Engineering Directors. Do not assume that a missing technology is automatically in the Adopt status. The site deliberately only includes technologies in use within
in use within OTP. If the missing technology is in use, please flag this on the #tech-stacks Slack channel.

Use the OTP Tech Stacks site Browse technologies menu to see all technologies included in OTP. To have new technology considered for inclusion on the Tech Stacks site see New Technology in OTP. Technologies actively under consideration can be tracked on the OTP New Tech GitLab board. This board
board. This board also shows technologies that have been considered and rejected in the past. If circumstances have a changed a technology that was previously rejected can be re-evaluated.

NOTE: The OTP Tech Stacks site does not generally go down to the level of individual libraries because we believe there is less benefit in standardizing down to this level. However, there might be exceptions for libraries that we actively want to stop using - you can find these on the OTP Tech
on the OTP Tech Stacks site.
Tech Stacks for OTP Internals

Technologies will sometimes be used to build OTP, without offering those technologies to users of OTP. The OTP Tech Stacks site refers to these technologies as OTP internals. There is a menu option in the Tech Stacks Browse technologies menu to show all technologies, including OTP internals. When

OTP internals. When building new OTP features, Engineering Productivity teams must only use technologies that are listed on this page as Adopt. If an EngProd team believes an additional technology is required to build OTP, they must follow the OTP New Tech process, even if the technology will not
technology will not be exposed to OTP users.
Programming languages

Ocado Technology has a strategy of using a limited number of programming languages. Our primary programming language is Java, which can be used for any applicable use case, in any domain. Other languages should be used only in specific domains. See the OTP Tech Stacks site for a list of domains,
a list of domains, and languages that should be used in those domains.
Review the following principles when deciding whether to approve use of a new language, or expand the use of an existing language into a new domain. Justification is also provided for each principle.

The language must be appropriate for the domain. This almost goes without saying, but a language must have the characteristics, libraries, tooling support and runtime required to make it appropriate for use in the domain.

Use mainstream and popular languages that are easy to recruit for today. Choosing a mainstream popular language makes it easy for Ocado to recruit engineers that have many years of commercial experience working with that language. This means new engineers can be fully productive more quickly, and
more quickly, and avoids the need to train engineers at Ocado’s expense.

Use languages that we are confident will remain mainstream and popular in the long term. If a language becomes less popular and difficult to recruit for in the future, we would inevitably choose to rewrite any code written in that language. For any non-trivial body of code this would incur a high
would incur a high cost. This risk can be managed by only working with languages we are confident will remain popular and mainstream in the long term.

Prefer to have one language in each domain. Having one language in each domain means engineers can move between teams without having to learn a new language, or refresh their knowledge of a language. Similarly, ownership of a system can be transferred to another team more easily. Preferring one

Preferring one language makes it less likely a system will be rewritten when transferring ownership from one team to another. A single language also means domain-specific shared libraries need only be written for one language.

When considering time taken to learn a language, we should not conflate time taken to learn the syntax of a language, with the time taken to learn how to program idiomatically in a language, learn its standard libraries, learn the surrounding tooling ecosystem , and understand its runtime

its runtime idiosyncrasies in a production environment. Some languages have a syntax that is relatively straightforward to learn. It may take only a few weeks to internalise the full syntax. However, it inevitably takes much longer to internalise how to use a language idiomatically in various

in various situations, since this only comes from writing sufficient and varied code in the language. Standard libraries take time to learn since they are typically quite large. Some languages have a comprehensive tooling ecosystem to learn too (e.g. tools for linting, managing dependencies,

dependencies, running tests, building, packaging, etc). Finally, understanding the idiosyncrasies of a language runtime in a production environment only comes with experience of running and maintaining production systems in that runtime.

Different languages have different attributes, but - ultimately - the evaluation which is best is subjective. There are thousands of Turing-complete programming languages, and more continue to be created. By definition any of these could be used to solve a general purpose computing problem. Each

problem. Each language has pros and cons, but which is better will always be subjective. For example, some languages pride themselves on having a very succinct syntax. This means more complex logic can be expressed in fewer characters. However, sometimes a more succinct representation is more

is more difficult to understand, particularly for someone who is less expert in the language. To counter this, some languages (e.g. Golang) intentionally have a syntax that is simple but verbose. One approach is not definitively better than the other.

Language choice is a personal preference. We should recognise that people have individual preferences relating to programming languages. Each person will have their own favourite languages. However, being a favourite language does not necessarily mean it’s the best language to use in the Ocado
to use in the Ocado environment.

Prefer the incumbent language(s). Changing the language(s) used in a domain is an expensive and time consuming activity that will involve retraining engineers and rewriting code. Language standardisation should therefore be weighted towards the dominant incumbent language(s) in use in that domain
use in that domain within Ocado Technology today.

Library and framework selection

Development teams do not need to ask permission to use libraries or frameworks. Refer to using open source software when selecting open source libraries or frameworks.
