Technology deprecation

The following process is used for deprecating technologies in Engineering Productivity - we also encourage other streams to use the same process. Reasons to deprecate technology:

save infrastructure costs

reduce support overhead

consolidate our in-house expertise on a smaller set of technologies

improve our security position

improve morale amongst support engineers

allow us to move to new technologies with greater speed

The OTP Tech Stacks site defines status for our OTP technologies:

DEPRECATE status means we are actively removing this technology.

HOLD status means do not use this technology for new applications.

Deprecation principles

All Deprecate and Hold technologies need to be formally supported until nobody is using them. A technology can only be moved to the deprecate status with agreement from Engineering Directors that they will schedule work to move off the technology.

Deprecation process

The deprecation process consists of three phases - planning, migration and shutdown. The process is a cooperation between Technology Owners (TO) maintaining the technology and Engineering Directors (ED).

Planning phase

(TO) Justify deprecation, including risks, costs and value.

(TO) Document migration, including the estimated cost of migration.

(EDs + TO) Agree on a realistic timeline with Engineering Directors, following EDs consultation with their respective product teams.

Migration phase

(EDs + TO) Publicise the decision, shutdown date and migration documentation.

(TO) Monitor the migration progress, sending reminders to the migrating teams, as well as their engineering management and product management.

(EDs) Ensure migration is driven within the streams and finished within the agreed time frame.

Shutdown phase

(TO) Confirm no users are left.

(TO) Shutdown.

(TO) Decommission.

Reassessment

As the migrations are usually spanning across multiple months, there is a chance that changes in the business may create a need to re-evaluate the plan. If a team cannot complete the migration within the agreed time frame, considering their existing commitments, see the Deprecation Policy for
Policy for guidance.

Tracking deprecation progress

The Deprecated technologies - making it happen spreadsheet tracks the status of deprecation candidates. Slack channel #deprecation-sig contains discussion and status updates.

SOC 2

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #soc2-sig.

What is SOC 2?

System and Organization Controls (SOC) 2 is an international standard for assurance over IT Controls. A SOC 2 certificate or SOC 2 report is issued by external auditors. An unqualified SOC 2 report allows service providers to demonstrate that their organisation has internal controls in place to
in place to ensure operational objectives, such as software security, are met.
What are SOC 2 Controls?

The SOC 2 standard can be applied to many industries, and many ways of working. It defines a number of Trust Services Criteria (TSC). Organisations choose which of the TSCs they want to be audited against, but all audits must include the Security TSC. Against each TSC the SOC 2 standard contains a

standard contains a list of Points of Focus (PoFs). Each organisation must decide what controls they will put in place to ensure their services meet these PoFs. The organisation is then audited against these controls. The audit checks that each PoF has a control in place, and that the controls are
the controls are operating as expected.

At Ocado all controls relating to software development are encapsulated in our Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), so development teams do not need to be familiar with the controls, the PoFs, or the TSCs - they only need to follow the SDLC.

Which services are in scope for SOC 2?

Any system that can (directly or indirectly) affect any of the following aspects of Production OSP environments is in scope for a SOC 2 audit:

Availability

Security

Service integrity

Data confidentiality

Privacy

How often is the SOC 2 audit performed?

We are audited for SOC 2 every 12 months.

What do software development teams need to do to ensure we pass the SOC 2 audit?

Teams developing software that is in-scope for SOC 2 must follow the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). Following the SDLC is both necessary and sufficient for SOC 2 compliance. The SDLC documentation provides guidance on how to implement each SDLC activity, and how to evidence to auditors
to auditors that each activity takes place. It's critical that teams are able to evidence that they perform all SDLC activities that are applicable to their systems.

!> IMPORTANT: SOC 2 auditors DO NOT look at the Maturity Model, and DO NOT look at team's Maturity Model scores. The auditors will only validate that the team follows the SDLC.

How is the SOC 2 audit performed?

When the auditors perform a SOC 2 audit they will randomly select changes deployed to production in the previous 12 months. For each selected change, we must be able to evidence that all relevant SDLC activities were performed. The auditors will ask us to follow the chain from deployment to GitLab

to GitLab commit(s). From the GitLab commit the auditors will ask us to demonstrate that all SDLC activities relating to the planning, discovery, code, build, test, documentation, and deployment of a change were followed. They will also ask for evidence that 'business as usual' activities are
activities are occurring, such as the periodic review of all threat models.

Testing

If you have a question or feedback related to this section, ask on #testing-working-group.

We need to build a software platform to provide an end to end solution for multiple international retailers. This platform needs to be reliable, scalable, maintainable and secure. Testing is a key pillar in achieving this goal - we need to have a consistent, streamlined and sensible approach.

Below you can find the test principles and the test types which should be the basis of the teams' test strategy. There may be other types of test not covered here, which is not an indication that they are not important. Specifically, these guidelines focus on tests embraced in the software

in the software development process, tests that are a relevant ingredient in obtaining the required quality level and consistently applied for each change (or each change of a certain type). Besides, there may be ad-hoc tests, which can be something a developer does before committing his or her
his or her changes. This document is not addressing such ad-hoc tests nor tries to formalise them.
Test strategy

As a general rule of thumb, all tests are created, maintained and executed by the same teams who write the code (with the participation of product people in their design). Some streams or areas may have a test strategy document - refer to this document for further details on test types, ownership
types, ownership and processes in your stream. Known test strategy documents:

Fulfilment stream

Test pyramid

The test pyramid is a metaphor for how frequently test types are used. Test types at higher levels of the pyramid should be fewer and less frequent. It's better to run more short unit tests than time-consuming manual tests. The following picture describes the Ocado Technology test pyramid.

Test pyramid

Test types

This section is a dictionary of commonly used test types ordered in a hierarchy provided to make discussions about testing less ambiguous and ease testing guidelines adoption. While there are standards defining types of tests like ISO/IEC 29119, we prefer to provide a reduced list of test types
list of test types with examples of the ones we commonly use at Ocado Technology, clarifying the intent of each of them.

For each type of test we provide:

Definition: A short definition of the test type.

Motivation: Why should you use it.

Example: An example of its usage.

Environment: The environment where we expect to run tests of this type.

Automation: Considerations to be made towards test automation.

1. Static analysis

Definition: Tool driven process that checks for code errors without executing the code. They can look for compilation, styling error or vulnerable dependencies.

Motivation: Help ensure consistency and standards, and catching code smells in a quick feedback loop.

Example: Finding unused private methods or fields in Java.

Environment: Local developer environment or build server pipelines.

Automation: Can be run on every build.

2. Manual tests

Definition: Tests carried out by humans.

Motivation: To cover very difficult to test features that may have an unusual and hard to set up use case. They are more expensive and should be used when there is a justified case e.g. not worthy to automate.

Example: Test that RF devices correctly display the image, and the name of a product when its barcode is scanned.

Environment: Run them in an environment as close to production as possible and safe to test.

Automation: Not automated. Consider automating parts of the manual process. Manual testing is, in general, discouraged in favour of automated tests, though there will be some situations where automated testing is not possible or not cost-effective to implement (for example, where human judgement is
human judgement is needed or sensitive data is exposed). The results of manual tests must be kept as described in the retention policy.

3. Unit tests

Definition: A test that allows quick validation of logic encoded into a logical unit of code. They only cover specific functionality inside a single responsibility code unit.

Motivation: They provide confidence in the code behaving as expected. They add a way to communicate the intention of the code.

Example: Given a class implementing a calculator, validate that with input cosine(0) returns 1. You would validate other cases, even ones generating errors.

Environment: Local developer environment or build server pipelines.

Automation: Run on every build.

4. Component tests

4. Component tests

Definition: A test that validates the communication between multiple logical units. This is usually performed within a single application and ensures that the application comes together and performs as expected. You can use the same tools as with unit tests.
Motivation: They provide confidence the application delivers the expected functionality, with its different logical units interacting properly.

Example: Test that a persistence layer stores a logical entity into a database. We can use an in-memory database to replace the real database, thus we can focus on testing that all the entity's values are saved properly according to the logic of the persistence layer.

Environment: Local developer environment or build server pipelines.

Automation: Run on every build.

5. System tests

5. System tests

Definition: Tests focused on verifying that a system as a whole delivers the expected functionality. The system is a set of services that constitutes a subdomain of any size, e.g. services owned by one team or implementing a business process.

Motivation: Ensure the application works end to end without using all external dependencies in order to have quick feedback loops.

Example: Verifying how requests are handled when the mocked dependencies are configured to return errors.

Environment: Test environments or the local developer environment, including Docker containers.

Automation: Run on every deploy to DEV or before merging to the main branch.

6. Contract tests

Definition: A kind of integration tests that verifies interactions between the consumer and the provider of an API without the need for launching them. Instead, the compatibility against a contract is verified separately for each of the participants. The contract is a sequence of actions that are

of actions that are expected during the interaction between the consumer and the provider, e.g. a request and a corresponding response. It is beneficial if providers and consumers use the same tooling.

Motivation: To validate compatibility between the consumers and the provider of an API without the need to run them together, so that they provide a quick feedback loop and create low infrastructure requirements. Contract tests add a safety net by answering if a consumer or provider version can be
version can be deployed to a certain environment by analysing compatibility against services versions already running in this environment.

Example: Team C develops an application consuming an API implemented by team P. Team C implements contract tests that ensure the expectations they have from the API, so they can detect any breaking changes.

Environment: Local developer environment or build server pipelines.

Automation: Run on every build.

7. Inter-system tests

Definition: Inter-system testing is conducted across system boundaries to verify complex business processes at the highest level. These tests may encompass a full business flow, such as creating an order, picking it, and then delivering, or focus on specific segments or partial flows within a

flows within a stream (e.g., the entire Fulfilment process or a subset like the Payments flow in E-commerce). Unlike other forms of testing, inter-system testing ideally operates with actual system integrations rather than mocked applications to ensure a realistic assessment of system interactions

system interactions and dependencies. However, this approach leads to longer feedback cycles and presents significant maintenance challenges due to the involvement of multiple dependencies. Despite being more costly and labor-intensive, the depth and breadth of coverage offered by inter-system
by inter-system testing make it invaluable for validating the end-to-end functionality and reliability of complex systems.

Motivation: Inter-system testing aims to confirm that key business processes function correctly, primarily using real system dependencies, including external systems like Payment Service Providers. The exception is hardware components, which may not always be integrated due to feasibility. These

feasibility. These tests provide early assurance that business-critical features work as intended, essential for preventing regressions and ensuring readiness for further validation phases, such as Abundo and iOSP trialling.

Example: We might test that we can pick 20 orders in a single shipment, and successfully dispatch the vehicle. We can create Webshop orders with specific features enabled (like Cash on Delivery). We can verify that Logistics can handle External API traffic coming from 3rd party Ecommerce.

Environment: Run on SIT.

Automation: Run periodically as a regression test, and before major releases.

8. Load tests

Definition: A load test stresses the system by exercising it with the estimated load. With the system under stress, the test can check for performance regression or notice edge case issues. This kind of tests provides assurance that the system will hold up when working in production. However, they
However, they imply longer feedback cycles.

Motivation: Give confidence that systems are able to handle a given load.

Example: Request 10k orders from a given API to ensure it performs as expected by the SLO.

Environment: Individual component load tests run in Dev (required). End to end load tests run in SIT (recommended).

Automation: Schedule load tests to run regularly, and run them before major releases.

9. Security tests

Definition: Tests that cover application-level controls for threats discovered during the threat modelling sessions.

Motivation: Ensure that the application is secure against identified threats.

Example: Send an API request with an SQL injection malicious payload in the URL parameters.

Environment: Local developer environment or build server pipelines.

Automation: Run before each release.

10. Penetration tests

Definition: Tests requested for an external entity that perform an authorized, exploratory, simulated attack on a computer system, to evaluate the security of the system.

Motivation: Provide additional and expert assurance that a system is secure.

Example: Checking whether there are any security vulnerabilities in a retailer facing user interface, or API.

Owner: InfoSec team.

Environment: SIT or Production.

Automation: Run on an ad hoc basis. Automated tooling may be used as part of the test.

11. Abundo and iOSP trialling

Definition: Abundo and iOSP trialling is a post-release activity, where new features are used in a production operating environment (Abundo retailer operations), validating the feature in the real world conditions. The feature must be fully tested before it is released for trialling - the trialling
- the trialling phase is validation phase, not a test phase.

Motivation: Get initial feedback from real users.

Example: We might trial a new bot routing algorithm with real physical hardware, or trial a webshop voucher creation system with live customers.

Environment: Abundo production and iOSP test grid.

Automation: No automation - users interact with the system as normal users.

12. Exploratory tests

Definition: Exploratory testing is an experience-based testing technique that emphasizes a rapid cycle of learning, test design, and test execution. Rather than trying to verify that the software conforms to a pre-written test script, exploratory testing explores the characteristics of the

of the software, raising discoveries that will then be classified as reasonable behaviour or failure. This type of tests helps identify new failures that can be added to the scripts. Sometimes it exposes behaviours that are benign, even welcome, but not thought of before. Should be executed within
be executed within a limited amount of time and with focus on one test charter.
Motivation: Discovering and learning.

Example: We are rewriting the application frontend to a new technology, doing our best to compare the current and newly created versions during development. However, we may miss some aspects, or break something that is not covered in our test scenarios or the manual verification during the rewrite
during the rewrite stage. Hence, an experienced tester spends time exploring various paths in the new frontend to identify unexpected behaviours.

Environment: Run on SIT.

Automation: Run at the frequency the team defines. Consider automating the scenarios that helped find bugs.

Test principles

Each principle has a description and a rationale in italics.
Test as early as possible To find defects early, both static and dynamic test activities should be started as early as possible in the software development lifecycle.

The earlier you find a defect in your code, the faster and cheaper to correct it, and the sooner to have a new version of the software.

Simplicity Push testing coverage down the test pyramid to reduce complexity, cost and fragility.

Generally tests further down the pyramid run faster and are much easier to be corrected. A fast feedback cycle provides more testing value to the teams. Alternatively, you may find that you can replace some system tests with component tests, which are easier to maintain. Always try to replace tests
to replace tests with simpler ones that run faster and/or provide better value.

Pragmatism Only add tests where needed. It's OK to not have all the types of tests. You should only add tests where it adds value. Testing everything (all combinations of inputs and preconditions) is not feasible except for trivial cases.

There's no point in adding tests to make sure you have a test at every level. If a unit test covers the functionality well, don't add system tests or component tests. A test adds a maintenance burden to the team - if the test isn't delivering value (as the code is covered sufficiently by a simpler
by a simpler test), then it should be deleted.

Automation Automate tests and test pipelines to enable fast feedback loops. Favour test automation over manual testing.

Having tests automatically run on commit means you'll get feedback on the code. All changes are tested.
Fast feedback Run tests as soon as it brings value - potentially even on every commit.

A fast feedback cycle allows you to quickly iterate through code, improving it and refining continually. Simpler tests are easier to write and they bring value quickly, offering fast feedback, while more complex end-to-end tests are slower, and often mean waiting around for results.
Stability Tests should be reliable - not failing randomly or producing false positives. Flaky tests should be fully analysed to make them stable.

Tests that produce false positives or fail randomly break trust in the testing suite, and get ignored. These tests should be disabled and fixed whenever it is possible; otherwise, they should be deleted.

Threat modelling

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #threat-modelling.

Threat modelling (TM) is an activity which aims to identify risks affecting a system based on its architectural design and functionality. By creating a risk profile of an application to determine why a malicious actor would compromise a system, we can then reason how this could be achieved and
be achieved and decide whether mitigating the risk is worth the effort required to implement the necessary controls.

Threat Modelling is not optional - it is activity C1 in the Ocado Technology SDLC.

All applications deployed to production environments must be threat modelled.

Threat models should be continuously reviewed and updated throughout the lifetime of the system being modelled.

Threat modelling is an iterative and structured engineering technique that can be used to identify and understand threats, attacks, and vulnerabilities which can affect an application. Threat modelling can be used to shape an application's design by balancing risks, controls, and usability. It is

usability. It is also used to protect assets - anything that offers value, be that tangible, such as delivery vans, bots or picking devices, or intangible, such as brand reputation, quality of service or personal data.
Threat modelling and the maturity model

Threat modelling is listed in the Ocado Technology Maturity Model as activity C1, with both an internal and SOC2 target level of 4. To achieve the desired level of maturity for this security activity, development teams would need to ensure the following criteria are met:

For every application owned by the team, a threat model must be created and reviewed at least once every 12 months.

The output of the threat modelling session is captured in a consistent manner.

Threat Modelling is built into the team’s SDLC so that for each feature change and architectural redesign, the team considers whether a threat model update is required, and an incremental threat modelling session takes place if necessary.

A system-wide threat modelling session is normally conducted the very first time an application is assessed, and then reviewed every 12 months. As the name implies, the scope of the session is quite broad as all the data flows, data stores and external entities an application interacts with are
interacts with are being mapped out and analysed for possible threats.
Incremental threat model

To achieve the highest level of maturity in this security activity, it is necessary to adapt the team’s development process such that for every change, the team determines whether it is required to conduct a threat modelling session that focuses solely on the components that are to be added or

are to be added or modified. By holding an incremental threat modelling session before moving on to the implementation stage, the team may be able to adapt the architectural design to avoid certain threats altogether, or alternatively, ensure that appropriate security controls are included in the
are included in the acceptance criteria of the story.
To assist with this, AppSec have defined a list of questions which the team should answer during technical discovery in order to decide whether a threat modelling session should take place:

Are there any applications owned by the team that are in scope of the feature for which threat modelling has not been done?

Does the project require creating a new application?

Does the project require adding, deleting or modifying any of the assets?

Does the project involve changing or implementing new authentication or security controls?

Does the project require storing or accessing sensitive data?

Does the project introduce new calls to systems not owned by the team or across trusted zones?

Does the project involve creating a new endpoint or UI application that will be accessible from outside of a trusted zone?

Is direct application security involvement required throughout the project?

?> NOTE: The recommendation regarding trusted zones is to trust data that is exchanged between services belonging to the same application group provided they all reside in the same VPC.

Implementing threat modelling

During a threat modelling exercise teams are expected to develop a common understanding of what assets need to be protected and how this can be achieved. This is done by discussing what it is they are building, what can go wrong with the system, and what they can do to protect against these
against these scenarios.

Threat modelling structure

The following questions help understand the objectives and direction of threat modelling:

What are we building? Answering this question is the goal of the security profile definition session. In order to understand what assets should be protected, the team should document the application's architectural diagram, its intended users, the main use cases, and the nature of the data stored

of the data stored or processed by the application. It is very important to ensure that all team members have the same understanding of the system and its architecture, and agree on the impact that a loss in confidentiality, integrity, or availability would have on both their system as well as the

as well as the wider platform. The outcome of this session should be recorded in a document based on the provided template as well as in OTP Registry under the application's Security Context section. To find out more about how to conduct this session and how to record the outcome, see the Security
see the Security profile definition section.

What can go wrong? Armed with the architectural diagram created during the security profile definition session, teams should attempt to identify viable threats that might affect the application. The outcome of this session should be recorded in a threat model document. To learn out more about the
out more about the recommended methodologies that can be employed to identify threats and how to record this information, see the Threat identification section.

What are we going to do about it? All identified threats should be assessed to determine the risk they pose. Taking the risk into account, the team, together with their product manager, should then decide on what actions to take. The decision made during this session should be recorded in the
be recorded in the threat model document. To discover more about risk analysis and mitigation actions, see the Analysis & mitigation section.

Did we do a good job? At the end of the threat modelling session, it is recommended to hold a short retrospective and discuss what went well during the meeting, whether the scope was clear and what can be improved going forward. Please share any feedback on the process on the #threat-modelling
#threat-modelling Slack channel.
Application security profile

The main objective of this session is to understand the value and impact of an application to the reputation, finances and productivity of the project or business. It is based on the data processed, stored or simply accessible by the application. The recommended methodology is called Rapid Risk
called Rapid Risk Assessment (RRA). This is typically a 60 to 90 minute session during which the team will:

Analyse the system, describe how it works and what it does.

Determine what data is processed, stored and who has access to it.

Determine if there are any additional compliance requirements (i.e. PCI DSS).

Determine if the application is exposed to the internet.
Determine the application's security requirements of confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

It is important to note that the focus of this session is not on enumerating and analysing security controls or determining the likelihood of various attack scenarios. The goal is to build a security profile for the application by identifying its security requirements. This information will be
information will be particularly valuable during the threat identification stage as it will help the team focus on the threats that are most relevant for their application.

The outcome of the session must be recorded in a Google Document based on the Threat Model template.

Threat identification
There are many ways to analyse a system in regards to the threats it's susceptible to. Some leverage diagrams, some rely on textual descriptions and other focus on the code itself.

At Ocado, the recommended aproach is called STRIDE-per-element. It is based on system diagrams which you can learn how to build using this dedicated resource. As the name suggests, it's based on the STRIDE taxonomy which defines threat categories, that can be used to study the system with a more
system with a more defined threat scope. As a methodology, it's very generic, simple enough for anyone to use, and with proven results across Ocado.

Spoofing

Tampering

Repudiation

Information disclosure

Denial of service

Expansion (elevation/escalation) of Privilege

STRIDE-per-element builds upon the STRIDE categories and realizes that not all elements are the same. It defines 3 groups (Processes, External entities and Data stores) of elements that will have a specific subsets of STRIDE categories associated, depending on the group characteristic. This allows

This allows us to simplify the analysis activity, save time and avoid identification of threats which are out of scope or that we can't do anything about. The STRIDE-per-element methodology also examines connections between elements, again using the subset of STRIDE that better suits these
better suits these connections.

Starting a threat discovery exercise can be intimidating as you start from scratch, and people often struggle because they believe they require advanced security knowledge, to be able to complete the task successfully. Having the knowledge helps and makes the process go smoother, however it is not
however it is not a requirement. Anybody can perform threat discovery, even if at a basic level, which yields valuable benefits.

The application security team has put together a series of cheatsheets which can be used by anyone, to: - get hints and guidance on which questions to ask - know what system and environment characteristics to consider

The cheatsheets are organized by supporting technology, like systems that rely on OTP Jar-Jar, but also other types of system like mobile applications or standalone applications. A web application sheet is also provided, which would fit most applications. There's also a generic cheat sheet which

cheat sheet which aims to provide help for scenarios that don't fit any of the more specialized sheets - if your scenario does not fit, if you believe it should have a dedicated sheet, or even if you believe there's opportunity to improve, please get in touch with the application security team
security team using the #threat-modelling channel.
It is crucial to capture any countermeasures that may be already in place and that mitigate a particular threat, since this factor will influence correctly assessing the risk it poses to Ocado.

Teams that have achieved a good level of proficiency in the recommended methodology are free to experiment with other approaches that they believe can bring more value to them - threats identification will benefit from diversity in analysis. Methodologies used across the organisation range from the
range from the recommended STRIDE-per-element, to Microsoft's Elevation of Privilege card game, OWASP’s Cornucopia card game as well as risk storming.

A good approach with regards to threat identification is to experiment with various methodologies and settle on the one(s) that the team feels most comfortable with and works well in conjunction with the team’s other SDLC activities.

Regardless of methodology, the outcome of the threat modelling session should be recorded in a document based on AppSec’s Threat Model template.

Risk analysis & mitigation

Once the threats have been identified, decide with your Team leaders (ETL, PO/PM) what actions you should take. Responsibility for the risk is shared across the team leadership. To make an informed decision, perform Binary Risk Analysis for each identified threat to determine the associated risk.

associated risk. Risk is a function of threats exploiting vulnerabilities to obtain, damage or destroy assets. Thus, threats may exist, but if there are no vulnerabilities then there is little or no risk. Similarly, you can have a vulnerability, but if you have no threat, then you have little or no
have little or no risk.

?> NOTE: The BRA and RRA is now part of the Threat Model template. So if the team is using the newer template, there will only be two documents to be shared with AppSec, these are the application's architectural diagram and the Threat Model sheet. The older template is not deprecated and teams can
and teams can still continue using the older RRA document and Threat Model sheet making certain the contents are still relevant and up-to-date.

When the risk is determined, the team should make an appropriate risk decision:

Mitigate: Implement appropriate controls to reduce the risk.

Accept: Take no further action. The team is comfortable with the risk level.

Transfer: Pass the risk to another team, provided such a team agrees to take the ownership.

Avoid: The affected component can be decommissioned. Decommissioning it is more practical than mitigating the risk.

Once the threats have been analysed, discuss with your Product Manager the mitigation approaches for relevant threats. Depending on the nature of the identified threat, typical mitigation approaches include:

Adding additional acceptance criteria.

Adding additional tests.

Changing the team’s definition of done.

Embedding additional security activities such as security code reviews into the team's SDLC.

Creating an issue in the SIM project if an exploitable vulnerability has been identified so that it can be addressed within the SLO timeframes.
Conducting timeboxed spikes to determine whether the application is indeed vulnerable which result in SIM tickets if this proves to be the case.

Raising a Jira issue (previously done via an elevator pitch) to implement significant security safeguards and ensuring this is prioritised alongside feature work.

The threat model document should be updated to reflect both the risk and the actions the team has agreed to take.

Threat Modelling - Definition of Done

To complete the Threat Modelling process, the following must be ensured:

The final Threat Model artifacts (the application's architectural diagram and the Threat Model spreadsheet) are accessible to the relevant stakeholders based on the specific criteria described in the Threat Modelling - Artefacts sharing settings Guru card.

The Threat Modelling section under the Security tab of your application in the OTP Developer Portal is updated with the link to the Threat Model artefact and other required information, as described in the Threat Modelling Tracker Guru card.

The Security Context section under the Security tab of your application in the OTP Developer Portal is a source of additional background data. It lets you assess the risks related to the application more precisely.

SOC 1 Compliance Framework

Communication note: If you have a question related to this section, ask it on the #soc2-sig.

1. What is SOC 1?

1. What is SOC 1?

System and Organization Controls (SOC) 1 is an assurance report produced by an external auditor. It evaluates the internal controls in place to ensure the integrity of key financial reporting data.

While SOC 2 focuses on information security, SOC 1 is specifically valuable for partners where the Ocado Smart Platform (OSP) processes a material proportion of their revenue or stockholding, or where it's a Contractual requirement. An unqualified SOC 1 report lets service providers demonstrate
demonstrate that their organization has internal controls in place to ensure it meets the objectives related to internal control over financial reporting (ICFR).

Relationship to SOC 2

These two reports are complementary. The SOC 1 report relies on the security controls already established in the SOC 2 report for general system safety.

2. What are SOC 1 controls?

The SOC 1 standard focuses on financial processes supported by OSP modules. At Ocado, the framework consists of financial integrity controls and IT General Controls (ITGC).

Core control objectives

The core financial scope is defined by eight primary control objectives:

Control area Objective Sales orders Integrity between orders, picking, and delivery. Products and pricing Accuracy between the applied prices and the client product catalogue. Promotions Correct application of valid promotion types and criteria. Payments Accurate and timely processing of payment

of payment requests. Refunds Correct processing of refund requests. Tax Accurate calculation and reporting of sales taxes and charges. Stock (supply chain) Reconciliation between purchase orders and goods received. Stock (inventory) Accuracy and completeness of stock levels, movements, and waste.

3. Scope of services

The report covers the software components of OSP and the supporting operations relevant to financial reporting. Any system affecting sales transaction processing or inventory maintenance is in scope.

Specific modules: eCommerce and Fulfilment.

Approach: Controls for these areas are built natively into OSP to provide a generic report suitable for all partners.

4. Audit schedule and methodology

Frequency

The SOC 1 audit is performed annually:

Audit period: March to February.

Report availability: Typically mid-April.

Methodology

Methodology

External auditors perform the audit by assessing the controls built into OSP and reviewing the lifecycle of financial data. The audit process includes:

Evidence review: Examining evidence of daily and monthly reconciliations.

Sampling: Sampling the remediation of exceptions to make sure data integrity is maintained.

Cross-referencing: Validating IT general controls from the SOC 2 framework (for example, logical access, system changes, and disaster recovery).

5. Native reconciliation
Native reconciliation is the process of identifying and documenting any reconciliation issues found in retailer data.

Ocado software teams have built and are responsible for maintaining this reconciliation. Issues are detected daily for the data generated on the previous day. Each rule identifies the reconciled transactions and provides a count of both successful and failed records. This data is classified by
is classified by retailer-business rule and reported.

DGov SOC1 audit results (Looker): Shows the developers the details of each investigated rule. This is for Ocado internal use only and isn't shared with external auditors, as they don't need line-by-line information.

The team creates a summary of issues, grouped per retailer and rule, and consolidates them in Jira. These are raised every Monday for the issues identified the previous week. Data Governance identifies newly raised issues in Jira and notifies the responsible teams.
Native reconciliation—SOC1 dashboard (Looker): Gives a summary of the issues raised. This dashboard is available for internal Ocado OSP employees, Ocado Solutions, and external auditors.

Note: External auditors will be provided with an Ocado laptop, granting them access to the dashboard and the underlying Jira project, to review how the development teams investigated the reported issues.

Required actions

Required actions

Make sure that the SOC 1 Assurance Tool (or a native reconciliation observability tool) is performing daily and monthly reconciliations.

Make sure all exceptions are investigated and tracked to resolution using documented remediation processes (through Jira tickets or dedicated Slack channels).

Make sure remediation activities are recorded and maintained for inspection by auditors.

IMPORTANT!

IMPORTANT!

The SOC 1 audit isn't just about the code. Instead, it's about the process. Auditors will examine the data processing approach, the exceptions they find, and the evidence that these exceptions are corrected, in line with the documented framework.

developed by Ocado Technology are reliable and secure and therefore vulnerability assessment has to be performed.

purely for the use by Ocado employees are not covered.

Whenever possible, tests should be carried out in a trialling environment such as Abundo or Remus Major. If this is not possible, tests can be carried out in a test environment fulfilling the following criteria: - the same version of the code as production - deployed on a similar infrastructure to

Runtime platforms

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #eng-prod-sig.

This section describes our strategy for runtime platforms at Ocado, and is specifically forward-looking rather than describing the current situation only. Over the long term our direction is focused on:

greater use of public cloud services

a move towards high abstraction, high value managed services

more use of containers

more use of serverless compute

teams spending more time writing business logic and less time managing infrastructure and middleware.

Platform consolidation

We will consolidate our runtime platforms to a total of three, with a clear preference for where our software runs:

Public cloud platform, the default and preferred choice.

Edge Compute.

Oracle-powered CFC Platform, exclusively for the long-term support of our oldest UK CFCs.

A standard, consistent and well supported public cloud platform is the preferred environment to run our software. There are, however, areas which require software to run very close to the hardware it is controlling, such as PLCs or wireless controllers - these will run on the Edge platform.

Consolidation of our platforms requires organisational level work over a significant period of time, with assumptions that:

The software already running on the Oracle-powered CFC Platform will continue to run there.

All other Ocado-developed software will consolidate to run on the public cloud platform, Edge Compute, or will be decommissioned following the switchover of Ocado Retail to use OSP.

Public cloud platform

A general purpose cloud-based solution for running both applications on Amazon Web Services, and analytic workloads on Google Cloud Platform. This platform is known as the Ocado Technology Platform. This is the default choice of runtime. We will continue to review cloud providers on a regular

on a regular basis. If we change providers for either data or applications, then significant central support will be given to teams to assist in the migration. By having greater alignment and consistency, any future changes will be made more straightforward.

This platform is an 'on-demand' service, provided neutrally to all streams and products. Instances of the platform are linked to the to product use-case rather than being owned by an organisational stream. For example an environment intended for OSP retailer scope can contain Webshop from

Webshop from e-commerce, supply chain from Logistics & Supply Chain and Dispatch software from Fulfilment. The intent is to empower engineers to deploy software to the most appropriate place regardless of their location within the organisational structure.

Part of the platform goals are to maximise development productivity and minimise cost of operation, by favouring high abstraction, managed services, and a PaaS model of service deployment. The platform is kept aligned with technical standards and tech-stack, and designed to make it easy to do the

it easy to do the right thing, and hard to do the wrong thing. It is tightly integrated with development infrastructure to make the overall experience of developers as automated and seamless as possible.

Moving existing services to the public cloud platform will require engineering work to become sufficiently compliant with the technical standards. This is by design - we want to invest in improving technical quality as we move runtimes, rather than adopting a lift and shift pattern.

Edge Compute

Our OSP robotic grid technology combines hardware and software into a tightly integrated product. A complex set of technologies support the grid, bots, wireless and wired networking within an OSP CFC. Some elements of the solution cannot be run on public cloud and require compute power at the
power at the 'edge' - physically inside the CFC. These are typically real-time, low latency and embedded systems.

Consider a range of compute locations, starting at one extreme with public cloud then moving through hybrid cloud, on-premise data centre, through to devices at the network edge. Our strategy is to move towards the extremes of this range and away from the middle group, pushing compute use outwards
use outwards towards both edge compute and public cloud.
Oracle-powered CFC platform

To maximize the return on investment of the oldest CFCs, we will continue to operate these facilities using the existing MHE and WMS solutions. This means we will need a stable and supportable platform, close to the CFCs, to support the largely Oracle-centred stack. The strategy is to make their

is to make their WMS solutions as self-contained as possible, running on local data centers with their own firewall security perimeter and API layer, and in a stable, steady state such that they can operate reliably for a 10- to 20-year period.

This platform does not necessarily need to be aligned with, or share components with, the public and private cloud platforms - the primary goal is finding the best way to run the CFC software for the longer term.

OSP runtime environment scopes

Because OSP is a very large and complicated solution, and in order to increase the isolation level between different clients, there are multiple types of OSP production environment. Each of these environments is network isolated from other environments, and contains its own copy of the various

copy of the various support services required. For public cloud environments this approach allows us to determine which global region in which to run different parts of the platform. Edge compute environments are naturally located at the site in which they operate.
Global

This scope is provided for software which supports Ocado's global efforts in providing the OSP services, and is not instanced per client - specifically where one copy of the software is used across all clients. Current examples include software measuring and reporting on our SLA performance across
performance across clients, and software related to the manufacturing and management of Bots.

Service restoration

If you have any questions related to this section, ask in the #incident-response-sig channel, or speak with one of your line managers.

Backout rather than fix forward
Deploying a fix to a defective deployment is known as a fix forward. This is in contrast to a backout or rollback.

If there is a problem with a deployment, you SHOULD backout the change rather than try to fix forward. A backout is normally the safest and quickest way to restore service. Even though it’s often tempting to try to fix forward, a backout should be preferred because:

Forward fixes do not always work first time (or even second / third time)

Forward fixes rely on build pipelines running and tests passing - these can be flaky or slow

Forward fixes can take longer to code and test than first expected

The previous version of code is normally known to work, so is a safe option

The previous version will normally have some metrics history still accessible within our observability tools. These can be compared to metrics after the rollback, to confirm that service levels have been restored

Rollback is an operational procedure that can be documented and rehearsed, sometimes even automated, but every forward fix is a unique new development task

Rollback from each new version to one (or more) previous versions can be tested ahead of deployment in a test environment
Sometimes it is necessary to fix forward, but if it’s not necessary then it should be avoided.

Temporary Access Approval Policy

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #eng-prod-sig.

It is our policy that nobody has permanent access to Production systems or client data, except any exposed technical metrics or aggregated business metrics required to monitor system health. This policy exists because:

The data in many of our systems belongs to our clients - it does not belong to Ocado.

Some of our systems contain personal data we are legally obliged to safeguard.

Many of our systems contain commercially sensitive data or software.
Allowing permanent write access to Production increases the risk of an accidental or malicious update.

When additional access to Production systems or data is required, it must be granted on a temporary basis only, applying the principle of least privilege. To ensure we apply due diligence in protecting client data and operational systems, production requests must require approval. Such requests
Such requests must only be approved if the approval prerequisites below are met.

Approval Prerequisites

A requester must not approve their own request. A request must only be approved by:

somebody on the team that owns the system or data being accessed, or one of their line managers.
an Incident Manager - incident managers can approve requests out of hours (e.g. for on-call support).

Before approving a temporary access request, ensure the following conditions are met. If you cannot ensure all of these conditions are met, ask the requester to find somebody else to approve the request.

For all requests:

For all requests:

[x] The request links to a ticket or discussion thread that shows there is a legitimate need to access Production. For example:

A production incident ticket or Slack channel

A support request ticket or Slack message. If non-public, the approver must be able to read it.

A backlog item that has just been deployed and needs checking

A deployment job that need investigation

[x] The request details what action will be performed.

[x] The level of access (environment / datasets / roles) requested looks reasonable.

[x] The length of time requested looks reasonable (e.g. not 4h to update a single value in a database).

Additional checks for write/update access:

[x] It is appropriate to use temporary access rather than deploy a config or code change.

[x] Likelihood and risk of causing a production incident have been considered.

[x] Unless it is an emergency change, the change has been tested in DEV and/or SIT.

[x] The change has been reviewed or ‘live updates’ will be pair programmed (except out of hours).

[x] It is clear how the change can be reverted.

[x] This is a good time of day to make the change.

Security Issue Management

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #sim.

Security Issue Management (SIM) is a central process for reporting and managing security issues. Whenever we discover a security related issue we want to have it registered, tracked and fixed within the Service Level Objectives defined by the Information Security Committee.

Where we are aware of a vulnerability in our systems, our default position is to correct it in a timely fashion. We should fix it unless there is a compelling reason not to. Where there is a compelling case not to fix an issue, this requires escalation and acceptance of the risk at a senior level
at a senior level by both Product and Engineering functions. The decision will be subject to review by the Information Security Committee.

Security issues include, but are not limited to, vulnerabilities found internally by a team in their own project, vulnerabilities found in another team's projects, vulnerabilities found during penetration tests, vulnerabilities found by third parties and reported to us by outsiders, as well as any
as well as any potential vulnerabilities detected through static or dynamic application analysis.

Penetration testing issues

SIM tickets are automatically created for every single issue described in a penetration test report. These tickets contain a link to the report and have the CVSS score and severity populated accordingly.

Snyk issues

Snyk issues

Snyk is a security tool which scans project dependencies and checks against its large database to detect publicly disclosed open source vulnerabilities. Snyk vulnerabilities can be automatically imported into SIM. See the Ocado's internal Snyk documentation.

By populating the application's Security Context section found in OTP Registry under the Settings tab, the CVSS Score and CVSS Severity of the imported issues will be automatically refined, and therefore an appropriate severity level will be assigned to the SIM ticket. Define the security context -

security context - otherwise, during triaging, the team would need to manually assess the impact by following the same process described for regular SIM tickets. If the application is not defined in the OTP Registry, or the App Security Context Settings is not populated, the SIM ticket's CVSS Score
ticket's CVSS Score and severity are copied directly from the Snyk vulnerability.

As part of the Snyk setup, teams are expected to populate the OTP Registry with the link of the Snyk project(s), as described in the Snyk prerequisites. This only applies to Chancellor applications in the production state.

Handling vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies

It is often the case that an application does not use vulnerable code in third-party libraries in an exploitable way. If a fix is available and the team can upgrade the library without significant effort, we recommend changing the severity to 'No Severity' and fixing the SIM ticket rather than
ticket rather than closing it as a false positive.

If an upgrade is not available (there is no fixed version for the vulnerable packages) or if the upgrade would require significant effort and, during triaging, the team determines that the issue isn't applicable (for example, the vulnerability is in a Postgres connector but your app doesn't and

app doesn't and won't use this DB) then the ticket can be closed as a false positive with an appropriate comment. An automated process, which runs daily, will permanently ignore the vulnerability in Snyk, preventing similar SIM tickets from being raised against the application in the future.

In some cases, the vulnerability may be introduced by an indirect dependency. For example, the dependency path may look like x > y > z, and z is the dependency that causes the vulnerability. In such cases, if the direct third-party dependency x doesn't have a fix available, the teams may consider

teams may consider introducing an updated version of the indirect dependency z in the classpath to fix the issue. You should use this option with caution to avoid breaking the application functionality.

On the other hand, if the issue is relevant and the team cannot mitigate a certain risk level at that particular point in time, the issue should follow the risk acceptance process. When a risk is accepted, the risk acceptor needs to provide a risk acceptance period, and the duration should reflect

should reflect the perceived level of risk. Upon expiry of the risk acceptance period, the automated processes will prompt the team to re-triage the issue. The team may resolve the issue if a fix has become available in the meantime.

An exception to this is waiting for the fix to become available when the team knows that a release is imminent. For example, a fix is not available today, but the issue has been reported on Github, and a contributor has committed to addressing it within a sensible timeframe. In this case, the team
this case, the team can mark the SIM ticket as Will Fix rather than going through the Risk Acceptance process.

For all cases, teams should not manually manage the .snyk ignore file or apply ignore rules directly in Snyk (this feature can be disabled by Snyk org admins). Teams should interact with SIM, as the integrations apply appropriate ignore rules in Snyk depending on the state of the SIM tickets.

Triaging issues

Triaging means reviewing an issue to determine whether the reported vulnerability is exploitable and sanity-checking its severity. Engineering teams should triage SIM issues assigned to the application groups they manage within seven calendar days of the creation of the ticket.

The severity of an issue is usually assessed by the engineering team and product manager. If necessary, assistance from AppSec Engineers can be requested on the #sim Slack channel. CVSS vectors and scores are used to determine the severity of an issue. It may be that our assessment of the issue may

of the issue may differ from the one provided by the ticket creator. The person raising the ticket may be applying general conditions, wheareas the team triaging the ticket have more knowledge of the application and its security requirements that they can take into account.

The CVSS provides a way to capture the principal characteristics of a vulnerability and produce a numerical score reflecting its severity. The numerical score can then be translated into a qualitative representation (such as low, medium, high, and critical) to help us properly assess and prioritise
and prioritise the vulnerabilities. The Common Vulnerability Scoring System Calculator can be used to perform this task.

We map CVSS scores to the severity of the issues in line with the CVSS v3.0 NVD Security Vulnerability Ratings:

During triaging, an assessment should be made to determine the applicability and severity of the vulnerability in the context of the application. If the issue is not a false positive and there is no fix for the vulnerability, then the following options should be considered: - Check the library's

Check the library's Github project or equivalent to determine if the issue has been acknowledged by the maintainers and when a fix might become available. If this is expected to take place before the SIM ticket's due date then mark the issue as Will Fix and re-assess closer to the date. - Consider

For vulnerabilities in indirect open-source dependencies introduced through an internal component (e.g. an OTP library such as JarJar or Totoro), liaise with the relevant internal development team for guidance.

Informational tickets generally highlight deviations from good practice. These tickets do not have a due date and we recommend to address these only if it is practical to do so. Otherwise, these tickets can be closed down with a comment justifying the decision.
?> How do I ensure Snyk issues are raised with the correct severity?

By default Snyk issues will be assigned the severity of the vulnerability in the underlying open-source dependency. However, this doesn't take account of the context of the application i.e. whether it's accessible from the internet, whether it's only used by development teams, etc. To ensure

Common security issues

Some of the identified issues cannot be addressed as they are not applicable, while others will not be fixed and always be marked as risk accepted (for example issues resulting from AWS configuration or OTP design). In such cases, it is safe to close the ticket and link it to the corresponding
the corresponding Common Security Issue. For the full list of known issues, see the common security issues list in Jira.

False positives

There are different reasons a vulnerability could be a false positive, for example:

Human error - the person raising the vulnerability might have made a mistake

Duplicate - another SIM ticket already exists for the reported vulnerability

Inapplicability - a vulnerability may exist but there is no way that vulnerability could ever be exploited, not even by a privileged user. For example the vulnerability could be a Spring vulnerability related to HTTPS processing, but the system in question is a batch system that doesn't (and never
doesn't (and never will) communicate over HTTP
?> When is it safe to close a vulnerability as a false positive?

If you suspect the vulnerability is a false positive due to human error, ask the person that raised the issue to try to reproduce it. Only close they issue if they agree they can't reproduce it. If a duplicate ticket exists, link the two tickets and close the most recent one as a false positive
as a false positive with a suitable comment.

If you suspect the vulnerability is a false positive due to inapplicability, before closing the ticket do ensure that: - you have considered defence in depth. A single component failing should not compromise the security of your system, so if (for example) a vulnerability could only be exploited by

be exploited by an internal system, this does not mean the vulnerability is inapplicable. You should fix the issue to provide defence in depth for your service. - you have considered whether the vulnerability might be applicable for your application in future. If you close the vulnerability as a
vulnerability as a false positive you will never be notified about again.

Risk acceptance

Requesting risk acceptance

Only request risk acceptance when the vulnerability is present, the risk is real and the team cannot implement a fix or introduce mitigations by the due date.

A DREAD assessment must be provided for High and Critical severity vulnerabilities before risk acceptance is requested. This assessment is already integrated in the SIM JIRA workflow. The following details are captured as part of the DREAD risk analysis:

Potential Damage: This is used to assess the scope of damage in case of the successful exploitation

Reliability of exploitation/reproducibility: This indicates our confidence in the exploit, if available.

Ease of exploitation: This indicates the effort and skills required to successfully exploit the vulnerability. This also captures the level of system access required before the vulnerability can be exploited.

Affected users: This indicates the pool of users that may be at risk.

Note: The last 'D' in DREAD is for Discoverability (how easy it is to discover the threat). For the purpose of risk assessment, we do not rely on the risk being a well kept secret. For this reason, there are only four fields that need to be populated.

The workflow presents the user with the following screen while requesting for the risk acceptance for SIM issues with a Critical or High severity value:

DREAD analysis for SIM issues

The options available for each of these fields need to selected to summarize the risk. In case of any questions or concerns reach out to the APPSEC team on the #sim Slack channel. Regardless of the severity of the vulnerability, also include the following items in the comment section:

options for remediating the risk, mitigating the risk, or adding compensating controls, and why the decision is being taken not to implement any of these options

why there is a compelling rationale for accepting the risk - the default position is to take a corrective action
a suggested time period for which the risk should be accepted

Once the information above is provided, the ticket can be transitioned to the Request Risk Acceptance state. Notify the Head of Engineering & Head of Product about the ticket so they can review the review and approve the risk acceptance request.

Accepting risk

Accepting risk

Risk acceptance is a value judgement. A risk should only be accepted when the work required to remediate, mitigate, or control the risk is not warranted given the nature of the risk.

Only Heads of Engineering and Heads of Product can accept SIM risks.
Risk Acceptance means an explicit decision has been made that no further action will be taken to: - remediate a risk - mitigate a risk - add compensating controls

The "Accept Risk" screen in the Jira workflow displays the DREAD-based risk analysis (DREAD Narrative), containing contextual details on the damage potential, affected users and exploitability of the vulnerability. Use the DREAD narrative along with any other information provided by the development
by the development team to assess the risk, and weigh this against work required to remediate the risk, mitigate the risk, or add compensating controls.
The decision to accept the risk should then be reflected in SIM by transitioning the ticket to the 'Risk Accepted' state, and providing a 'Risk Accepted Until' (RAU) date.

Populate the Comment for ISC field (mandatory) with a justification for risk acceptance when accepting the risk for a high or critical vulnerability. The Information Security Committee reviews all high and critical risk accepted tickets on a quarterly basis.

Whatever the reason for not addressing a vulnerability within the SLO period, the goal is to have it remediated in the shortest time possible. The maximum risk acceptance duration is 12 months. The majority of the issues should only be accepted for much shorter periods of time so they can be
time so they can be addressed as soon as there is capacity to address them.

The risk acceptance period provided upon risk acceptance is used to calculate the new due date of the ticket. The due date is extended by the duration defined by the ticket's severity, as described in the Security issue resolution times section, ensuring the team has the same amount of time to
amount of time to resolve the issue upon risk acceptance expiry as when the ticket was created.
Review of accepted risks

Upon risk acceptance expiry the ticket will automatically transition back into the Triaged state and the development team will be asked to re-evaluate it. The team can commit to fixing the vulnerability, or, if necessary, they can request risk acceptance once more by following the standard process.
Security issue policies

Security work usually does not create value - it protects value. Therefore there is a natural tension between product innovation and security work. For this reason, the security issue management policy sets objectives for fixing identified security issues. Each severity has a relevant timeframe for

timeframe for the fix, so there is a clear understanding of the priority of this work. Ideally security fixes should be included in the regular work stream of the team so that targets are met - sometimes even as expedite work.

Policy principles:

Do not include sensitive information - specifically the description how to exploit a vulnerability - in publicly accessible documents. Such instructions should be readable only to those responsible for triaging and fixing the security issue.

Resolve all critical, high and medium tickets before deploying a new application to a production environment for the first time (either by fixing or accepting the risk).

Resolve a security issue with a critical or high severity first (it has higher priority than ANY feature development work).
Schedule security fixes as a fixed date class of work. Resolved security issues within the required timescales defined below.

Even in the case of "internal" (i.e. non-internet or retailer facing) applications, avoid accepting the risk of security issues with critical or high severities unless the application will be switched off within three months.

Teams and the management hierarchy are responsible for resolving security issues through either fixing vulnerabilities, or formally accepting the risk, within the mandated target times.

Security issue resolution times

New security issues must be triaged within 7 calendar days. Depending on the severity of the issue the timescale and urgency of the fix differs. The resolution timescale includes the 7 calendar days triage period.
Severity Resolution Time (Calendar Days) Critical 14 days High 30 days Medium 90 days Low 180 days No severity Address if practical

Due dates of SIM tickets are automatically calculated to reflect the resolution times listed above. Changes in a ticket's severity, for instance during triaging, will also cause the due date to be recalculated.

The due dates are normally calculated in relation to the creation date of a ticket. For tickets which have been risk accepted, the due dates are recalculated and are based on the date risk acceptance expires.

!> For any high and critical severity ticket which has not been resolved within the relevant resolution time, the Head of Engineering must populate the Comment for ISC field with a justification for exceeding the due date. The Information Security Committee reviews all high and critical severity
critical severity tickets which have breached SLOs on a quarterly basis.
Alerting for SIM tickets

To receive Slack notifications about SIM tickets assigned to a particular application group, the team that manages the group must update their Raven configuration to provide a Slack channel for security alerts. If a security Slack channel is not defined for the team, the system's Slack channel

Slack channel configured in the OTP Registry will be used instead (select your system from the catalog, navigate to the Overview tab, and then check the Contact details). If this information is also not provided, alerts are sent to the owner's Slack channel configured in the Registry in the
the Registry in the Notification details.

Security issues prior to launch
SIM gives us a standardised, documented and transparent process for managing vulnerabilities which is also perfectly suited for services that are going to be externalised.

If you are preparing for opening your service to the open internet, or to a third party where we don't have any control over the security perimeter, penetration testing must be performed prior to launch. Every penetration test is concluded with a report which lists all the vulnerabilities
the vulnerabilities discovered by pentesters. SIM tickets will be automatically created for every vulnerability found during pentesting.

The service cannot be launched externally until all such tickets are:

triaged (i.e. have the severity confirmed by the team)

fixed or risk-accepted (following the risk acceptance process) all tickets with a medium, high and critical severity

Ideally, it is good practice to fix low severity issues and informational items. However, it is not a gating criteria. You can decide to launch your system externally with unresolved low severity or informational issues. But as they will be recorded in SIM you also need to process them according to
them according to SIM policies.
For more details on how to schedule a penetration test for an application that you would like to externalise, please refer to the penetration testing section.

Open source

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #open-source.

Using open source software

You MUST assess the following before using any open source software:

License - check if the code includes any license that is not valid for the intended use case, or is not compatible with any other licenses used alongside. See open source licenses for information on which licenses can be used for different use cases.

Security - check if it has any outstanding critical or high vulnerabilities and exposures (CVEs), or seems like CVEs do not get fixed in a timely manner, or at all.

You SHOULD assess the following before using any open source software:
Community - if the project is not very active, has a single or low number of maintainers, unanswered issues and PRs, or generally seems "stale", it might not be the best choice.

Popularity - is the library a popular choice in the community, does it have many stars and download metrics. How widely used is it?

Sustainability - check the project dependencies health to assess its long term viability. Does it rely on other stale libraries/frameworks?

To help you assess some of these things, there are some free resources on the internet. For instance, Snyk has:

Snyk Advisor

Snyk Vulnerability DB

The value and approach

Open source software underpins almost all the software that Ocado Technology creates, and the platforms on which that software runs. By contributing we will:

not having an us/them mentality for paid/unpaid contributors.
