Ocado Technology maturity model

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #maturity-model-and-sdlc-sig.

The Maturity Model (MM) is a self-assessment tool used by software development teams to help them measure their progress in adopting the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). Teams periodically reassess against the MM to better understand:

the activities they should be performing as part of the SDLC

which activities they are already performing well, and which can be improved

the level of maturity we are targeting for each activity

which other teams can provide assistance to a team looking to up-skill in an area of the SDLC

Having teams across the organization health-check themselves helps us understand our level of maturity as a whole. Having identified areas where we are less mature, the leadership team undertake work to understand why we are less mature, and what support we need to move us forward. When new
forward. When new activities are added to the SDLC, the MM is used as a mechanism to track progress of adoption of the activity across Ocado Technology.

!> IMPORTANT: SOC 2 auditors DO NOT look at the Maturity Model, and DO NOT look at team's Maturity Model scores. The auditors will only validate that the team follows the SDLC.

How is the output from the Maturity Model health-checks used?

At the team level: Each team should compare their health-check scores against other teams their department, to understand on which activities they are more mature than their peers, and on which they are less mature. Teams that are more mature should coach teams that are less mature, sharing
mature, sharing processes, code and expertise as appropriate.

At the department level: Each department should look for opportunities to equalise maturity levels across teams, such that each team in the department is performing to a similar level on each activity.

At the stream level and division level: The health check scores are assessed in aggregate in each stream and across the division, to determine activities where many teams need support to reach the desired level of maturity. The senior leadership team iteratively identifies the areas that need
the areas that need improvement and proposes an improvement strategy. Future health checks will validate if the strategy is successful.

How often must teams run Maturity Model health checks?

Teams MUST perform a full Maturity Model health check every 12 months. This must include: * reviewing activity scores, and updating them as necessary * scoring against any new Maturity Model activities introduced since the last review

The full review SHOULD be performed by the whole team, so that everyone is aware of all the activities, and the team can collectively re-score each activity. Teams may choose to perform a full review more frequently than every 12 months.

One team member SHOULD perform a lightweight review of the Maturity Model on a quarterly basis. During this review, any changes to team practices (or the maturity model itself that would affect the team's scoring) should be flagged up to the team, and any associated score updated.

The Maturity Model spreadsheet contains a 'Model changelog' sheet that contains a history of changes made to the model. Each activity also has an 'activity last updated' column.

Performing a Maturity Model health check

It's recommended to perform the health-check as a 90-minute team activity. Check your progress against the clock to ensure you have completed the health-check when the time is up.

In advance of the team health-check session:

Have someone from your team familiarize themselves with the Ocado Technology Maturity Model activities.

Clear up any questions that arise from reading through the list of activities and maturity levels

During the team health-check session:

Open the Maturity Model activities.

In a new window, open the Team scores sheet and copy the template column (C) to create a column for your scores. There is no need to ask the sheet owner to add the column.

For each activity on the Activities sheet:

determine which level best describes your team/system

record your scores in the team scores sheet

start with all your scores set to ?; Use n/a if an activity is not relevant to your system

round down if you're between levels

be honest about what level you're at - it's not a competition!

Update the Team's last full MM review date row on the Team scores sheet.

?> We have several teams working on the same system, what do we do?

Score each team against the maturity model separately.

?> My team looks after several systems, how should we score activities where the score will vary by system?

Record the lowest score in the range.

?> What do I do if I have questions about the Maturity Model or associated processes?

Ask on Slack #maturity-model-and-sdlc-sig.

?> What do I do if I have suggestions on how to improve the Maturity Model?

Start a discussion on Slack #maturity-model-and-sdlc-sig.

?> Can anyone help me run our team's first Maturity Model health-check?

Ask on Slack #maturity-model-and-sdlc-sig and somebody will try to help.

?> Where do I find the red/amber/green visualisation of the Maturity Model scores?

This can be found on the visualisation sheet of the Maturity Model Activities doc.

?> How are the red/amber/green classifications calculated?

The score coloring represents the difference between your team's score and the Target Level for the activity in question.

Red: the score is more than 2 below the target level.

Amber: the score is 1 or 2 below the target level.

Green: the score meets or exceeds the target level.
Entries with n/a entered are classified grey. Entries with no data entered are classified red.

Observability

If you have a question or feedback related to this section, ask on #observability-announce.

Section overview

This section captures how observability is currently helping the developers to improve their software systems. It also discusses how observability can be defined and classified, and how engineering teams could improve or adopt new observability tools or techniques as part of their day-to-day job.

It starts with some small theoretical definitions and concepts, then it attempts to breakdown into disciplines and what practices and concepts exists for each of the discipline of Observability. Finally, it is exposing some cross-cutting concerns that teams could apply based on their needs.
What is observability

Observability is "the measure of how well internal states of a system can be inferred from knowledge of its external output" (Wikipedia), or simpler, it's one way of mastering the complexity of ever-growing microservices' architecture, or "knowing the unknowable while questioning the known".

Observability is also sometimes called "multi-dimensional monitoring", because one might argue that is monitoring, BUT:

It is a property of the systems, not a tool

It includes elements of metrics and time

It crosses the boundaries from apps to services to discipline

It consists of logs, metrics, events and traces

Why Ocado Technology love observability

Observability is already a fundamental practice in companies with a customer-focused engineering culture like Ocado Technology. In the culture of "You build it, you own it" that is prevalent in the Ocado Technology teams, the need of observability embedded as part of the Software Development

Development Lifecycle (SDLC) started to emerge and become as important as other auxiliary disciplines such as testing, security management, continuous integration or deployment. The practice is also becoming every day more and more part of the infrastructure-as-a-code stack that each team is
that each team is maintaining as their standard development practices.

Observability pillars and disciplines - MELT

The concept of observability pillars is well-established. The pillars that Ocado Technology believes are relevant to the system are metrics, events, logs and traces (MELT):

Metrics - time-series based, aggregated measurements grouped or collected at regular intervals

Events - a discrete action happening at a moment in time

Logs - lines of text a system produces when certain code blocks get executed

Traces (distributed traces) - causal chains of calls between components

For a more comprehensive guide and explanation, see the MELT 101.

The main disciplines that are followed in Ocado Technology are based on the pillars mentioned above:

Metrics --> Monitoring and Alerting

Events --> Event Management

Logs --> Logging

Traces --> Distributed Tracing

?> NOTE: Observability teams constantly aim at improving the observability discipline by adopting new tools and techniques, or revisiting the existing ones. Please use the OTP Toolbox and Ocado Technology Tech Stacks to get the latest information about what is the current offer and recommendations.

This classification provides better insight into how observability can be used as part of the Ocado Technology Platform. However, the most important principle is to use all the disciplines together.

Monitoring

Monitoring

Monitoring is a common industry practice used to observe and track the operations and activities of users, applications or other services on a software system. Monitoring may be decomposed into at least 4 relevant parts: instrumentation, telemetry, backend system and visualisation.

Monitoring Practices

The following monitoring practices were identified and defined in the monitoring industry and are frequently used by various teams at Ocado Technology.
Healthcheck Monitoring - a simple probe to check that the application is alive (usually performed on a /health-check HTTP endpoint, or similar). This is also a hallmark in Ocado Technology.

Application Performance Monitoring (APM Monitoring) - a monitoring practice designed to help application developers to ensure that the applications meet performance standards and can take improvement actions on the performance and efficiency of the application.
Infrastructure Monitoring - practice used to monitor cloud, on-prem or hybrid infrastructure that becomes specialised on the cloud provider, or virtualization setup or orchestrators like Kubernetes.

Business Monitoring - a custom-tailored monitoring practice based mostly on custom metrics agreed and sent by the applications, very commonly events arriving from a distributed architecture like microservices.

Error Monitoring - a specific style of monitoring looking to identify, group, and create patterns from the abnormal behaviours the system might encounter; very helpful in identifying changes (degradations or improvements) in the application state.

Synthetic Monitoring - a style of monitoring specific to web applications that repeatedly checks specific web endpoints to identify problems and determine if a website or application is slow or experiencing downtime before that problem affects actual end users or customers.

SLO Monitoring - practice aimed at monitoring agreed service level objectives (SLOs) for a system or a group of systems, in order to understand the effect of changes in the system and ensure a certain level of reliability is met by the technical or business systems.

Frontend Monitoring - practice aimed at monitoring rich frontend applications, like applications based on Javascript, in order to provide useful frontend insights, like crashes, analytics or many others.
Mobile Monitoring - practice aimed at monitoring mobile native applications, that are usually small running programs and require specific semantics for adding monitoring on the running process.

Network Monitoring - practice aimed at monitoring network, in order to make visible the network topology, the network availability and other insights that might help keeping the network quality at acceptable level.

Instrumentation

Fundamentally, instrumentation is taking measurements from an application. It allows monitoring, troubleshooting, debugging, profiling, and understanding how applications function and why they function a certain way. There are many specific ways to instrument software programs historically, but for

but for Java Virtual Machines there are specific instrumentation techniques embedded into the virtual machine itself that make it possible to collect relevant telemetry data directly from the runtime of the application.

In Ocado Technology the instrumentation techniques used for the purpose of monitoring are usually achieved via the following standard approaches that are provided either by enterprise products or open-source libraries:

New Relic Agent

New Relic Agent

Observability Micrometer Library - for JVM-based applications, with Prometheus, New Relic and Cloudwatch support

OpenTelemetry Library - OpenTelemetry Metrics are now assessed by the observability teams. Use them with care, not centrally provided yet as practice
Kamon Agent - this is not centrally offered as part of the OTP platform, but its usage is accepted for Scala based applications

?> NOTE: Grafana Cloud Agent is also a telemetry collector that can be used together with instrumentation libraries to send metrics to Grafana Cloud. Use it mostly for non-JVM based applications intended to use Grafana Cloud.

Telemetry

Telemetry

Telemetry is one of the core concepts of the monitoring discipline, representing the data collected from the system in a given format. Most of the time it's called telemetry data, as usually the data is already well-structured and represented for the system that processes it.

The current industry efforts are focused on standardizing telemetry data for monitoring towards time-series data, in the form of dimensional metrics. In the monitoring industry, "dimensional" metrics refer to the metric data that has a variety of attributes or tags (dimensions) attached to it, such
to it, such as duration-related attributes (start time, end time), instance-id, region, host for example. This amount of detail allows for in-depth analysis and querying.

?> NOTE:: Taking the leap towards the adoption and usage of dimensional metrics is crucial for transitioning from traditional monitoring to modern observability.

Backend System

Monitoring systems are usually off-the-shelf products that can process and make intelligence out of the telemetry data, making a meaning out of it, storing it and processing it in a meaningful way for the consumers. The monitoring tools provided centrally by Engineering Productivity at Ocado
at Ocado Technology are:
New Relic - health check monitoring, APM monitoring, synthetic monitoring, error monitoring, infrastructure monitoring, business monitoring, frontend monitoring, mobile monitoring

Grafana Cloud (Prometheus, Tempo, and Loki) - APM monitoring, synthetic monitoring, business monitoring, SLO monitoring

AWS Cloudwatch - APM monitoring, synthetic monitoring, infrastructure monitoring, business monitoring

Sentry - error monitoring, business monitoring

Visualisation

Any monitoring activity becomes very meaningful when the telemetry data gets visualised in case of a debugging session, a reported issue or an automatic event. Visualisation is provided out-of-the box by the monitoring tools - for most of them, custom visualisation semantics is available in the
is available in the form of dashboards with graphs that can present the data in different desired formats.

Standard visualisation

The standard out-of-the-box visualisation is provided by the following tools:

New Relic - APM monitoring, synthetic monitoring, infrastructure monitoring, business monitoring

Grafana Cloud - APM monitoring, SLO monitoring

AWS Cloudwatch - infrastructure monitoring, business monitoring

Custom visualisation and insights (dashboards and querying)

Observability tools usually offer the option to visualise and query the data. By using an SQL-like syntax, you can create temporary or permanent dashboards.

New Relic - custom dashboards, rich visualisation, data explorer, NRQL query language

Grafana Cloud - custom dashboards, rich visualisation, data explorer, PromQL query language

AWS Cloudwatch - custom dashboards, rich visualisation, data explorer, SQL query language

?> NOTE:: Even in a custom visualisation setup, create a home dashboard that contains the golden signals. The team should consider it a crucial source for monitoring their application. The recommendation from EngProd is to use the RED Method (Rate, Error, Duration) for at least one of their
least one of their dashboards.

Alerting

Monitoring systems generate discrete events of what occurs in the system being monitored, usually with the intention of generating alarms, alerts or incidents. These terms in the monitoring industry are sometimes interchangeable, depending on the monitoring product's definition.
Alerting Systems

OTP monitoring tools provide various alerting capabilities. Despite the efforts to unify the monitoring configuration with initiatives like Micrometer and OpenTelemetry mentioned above, this is still not valid for alerts, so the way the alerts are created, evaluated, triggered or delivered is

or delivered is defined separately for each monitoring system. Plan ahead when you decide what types of alerts you might need and from where you want to deliver them. * New Relic Alerts * Grafana Cloud AlertManager * Cloudwatch Alarms * Sentry Alerts
Alerting Delivery

While some monitoring systems include alert delivery capabilities to several notification channels (Email, Phone, SMS, Slack), it is highly recommended to use PagerDuty as a central piece to send the alerts that any of the monitoring systems might generate for the applications. PagerDuty is not
PagerDuty is not only an incident response platform, but a fully fledged event management tool, allowing efficient handling of low/high priority and low/high urgency application events.

Alerting Principles

There is no definitive guide to alerting - this practice is highly dependent on the ways of working and nature of each team.

See Effective Alerting in Practice before you begin developing alerting practices in your new system. This guide is also handy if you and your team experience alerting fatigue (significant number of alerts, most of them ignored or closed), or the other way around, too much panic when faced with a
when faced with a very simple issue. Another recommended reading is My Philosophy on Alerting by Rob Ewaschuk.
Monitoring and Alerting Architecture

Monitoring and alerting tools and concepts cooperate together to form a coherent platform that you can use for your application. This platform is no different from the OTP general platform, but it constitutes one of the functional core areas. It is referred to as OTP Monitoring and Alerting
and Alerting Platform (OTPMAP).

Logging

Logging is the act of keeping a journal of messages produced by the application independently of the transport. It is most of the time locally stored in the form of a file (or several files), and lists the activities that a software program executed during the runtime. Given the decentralised

the decentralised nature of the modern distributed systems, logging is used as an interchangeable term for centralised logging, which is a practice of keeping log files for multiple runtimes of a software system in a central place (such as a database or filesystem). It's mostly tied to the L from
tied to the L from the MELT explanation before.

Logging (centralised logging) practice can be divided into at least 3 relevant parts: instrumentation, backend system and visualisation.

The centralised logging solution offered centrally at Ocado Technology is OpenSearch (OS).

Collection (log instrumentation)

Collection referes to collecting logs from the process and storing them in a local filesystem of the computer where the program runs. In modern logging solutions the log file is monitored, and the logs get collected, transformed and sent to the centralised system. The log format then becomes
format then becomes crucial for the transformation part to happen in a meaningful format.

Modern logging instrumentation tools used at Ocado Technology are filebeat, logstash or fluentd. Logs get collected by filebeat, but in order to be relevant, the log format has to respect the format required by logstash or fluentd. This is typically achieved by configuring a logging appender in
logging appender in your application to serve the desired format.

Backend System

Collected and transformed logs are stored and aggregated for a longer duration in a centralised backend using OpenSearch, which is part of the Logging (i.e OpenSearch) stack provided in Ocado Technology.

Visualisation

Visualisation

Visualisation is a very important part of the solution, as it helps significantly in building an understanding of the log data and, in effect, how to navigate it. In Ocado Technology, OpenSearch Dashboard is used for this purpose.

OpenSearch Dashboard is a very powerful visualisation tool that allows creating and managing dashboards that provide insights from the OpenSearch logging data. By using it, developers can understand the behaviour of the system from a technical standpoint - for example, how many orders were
many orders were processed without an error or how many requests received a 500 status code with a specific server problem.
Distributed Tracing

Distributed tracing is a discipline of observing requests (usually web requests in a web environment) as they propagate through distributed cloud environments, like microservice architectures. Distributed tracing follows an interaction by tagging it with a unique identifier, frequently called the

called the trace ID. This identifier stays with the transaction as it interacts with microservices, containers and infrastructure. It provides visibility and reveals service dependencies – showing how the services respond to each other. Visualising transactions in their entirety lets you compare

lets you compare anomalous traces against performant ones, and thus see the differences in behaviour, structure, and timing. This information allows you to better understand the culprit in the observed symptoms and jump to the performance bottlenecks of your systems.

As with other observability disciplines, there are 3 meaningful categories: instrumentation, backend system, and visualization.

The Distributed tracing solution at Ocado Technology is Grafana Cloud Tempo.

Instrumentation

Instrumentation

Distributed tracing instrumentation is mostly related to the collection of traces and spans from the ongoing web request, for the purpose of sending them to the central backend system.

The anatomy of a trace

In distributed tracing, a single trace contains a series of tagged time intervals called spans. A span can be thought of as a single unit of work. Spans have a start and end time, and optionally may include other metadata like logs or tags that can help classify what happened. Spans have

Spans have relationships between one another, including parent-child relationships, which are used to show the specific path a particular transaction takes through the numerous services or components that make up the application. * Trace - represents an end-to-end request; made up of single or

up of single or multiple spans * Span - represents the work done by a single-service with time intervals and associated metadata; the building blocks of a trace * Tags - represents the metadata to help contextualize a span

The libraries that are currently helping instrument the application for distributed tracing are:

OpenTelemetry Library - open telemetry tracing are still in beta mode,

Micrometer - both open telemetry and brave are supported,

AWS SDK XRay - in case you use AWS CloudWatch for tracing,

Spring Cloud Sleuth - a mature library provided and supported by Spring,

Jar-Jar - a reference implementation of Ocado Technology C4 Hallmark RequestId trace passing to other services (Handbook: Distributed Tracing).

Sampling

An important concept in distributed tracing is sampling, which is most of the time the responsibility of the client. It's almost impossible to have a 100% request tracing due to application performance impact, or because of the processing capacity of the distributed tracing tool itself, or because
itself, or because of the cost implications.

The most popular techniques for sampling are head-based sampling, tail-based sampling and random sampling.

Backend System

A wide range of open-source or enterprise distributed-tracing tools is available to process the tracing data instrumented from applications. Nowadays, most providers of observability tools and platforms offer distributed tracing solutions. Traces, along with metrics or logs, can significantly
can significantly increase the observability capabilities of applications. Currently, there are two officially supported central tools, but only one is recommended, as explained below:

Grafana Cloud Tempo is officially recommended for general-use cases.

AWS X-Ray is on hold and SHOULDN'T be used.

Visualization

Visualization helps create a deep understanding of what is happening in a complex microservice ecosystem. The backend system that ingests the data is reflected in the tools that provide powerful visualizations, allowing traces to be clearly seen. Additionally, it’s essential to visualize the
to visualize the service map of the ecosystem, which extends beyond just the immediate upstream and downstream services or interactions.

Event management

Event management is an important discipline of defining and managing the process of monitoring, investigating, escalating and responding to events that occur in a distributed system, or a platform.

Event management is usually a standard practice that happens in every organisation that has systems in production, but it usually deviates to accommodate specifics of their business or customers. In Ocado Technology's case, given the autonomous and "you-build-it-you-own-it" culture of teams, it
of teams, it defines how Ocado Technology expects the team to handle event.

Below you will find some important concepts that Observability teams believe are very important for the area, and then some considerations about the two distinctive categories in the area of event management - incident management and post-incident management.

Event management concepts

An event is a detectable change of state to an Ocado Technology managed product. An incident is a single or a group of events that has a negative customer or business impact. An alert is an event matching criteria predefined in a monitoring and alerting tool. A notification is a message that is
a message that is sent to a user, regardless of the channel.
Event Management Stages identified at Ocado Technology are:

Detection - An event notification is triggered via a team’s chosen tool, e.g. monitoring or alerting tools or bespoke systems triggered by data platform. Monitoring and alerting tools include: Cloudwatch, New Relic, Google Cloud Monitoring, etc.

Triage - The event notification is added as a log file or considered. If an alert is triggered, teams should decide whether they are to live with this state, apply a workaround or fix the issue. If it is determined to be customer impacting, the team will trigger the incident management process.

Response - An event is purely informational and no response is required. An alert is investigated by the appropriate teams and worked on as teams feel appropriate. An incident will trigger the incident management process and therefore be worked on until service recovery.

Resolution - An event is added as a log file. An alert is investigated appropriately and teams decide whether it is acceptable (they should consider if this is really any alert), apply a workaround and document this or fix the issue. Any actions should be captured in the teams backlog. An incident
An incident is resolved once service is restored.

Cross-cutting concerns

Observability-as-Code

Observability-as-Code is a practice of making observability embedded in the source code repository of an application or service, hence helping to work with an observability-focused approach in the early phases of the application development. This doesn't mean that only new application development
development should apply this principle, it should be an easy effort to reverse engineer the observability stack into an existing application source code.

As Infrastructure-as-Code revolutionized the managing and provisioning of infrastructure. Observability-as-Code is designed to supply engineers with policies and practices that enable them to better observe the state and behaviour of systems across environments. Additionally, by injecting and
by injecting and automating observability policies early on in the SDLC, the teams are better equipped to bring quality, learn and recover quicker from situations.

In order to go on the path of Observability-as-Code, the following practices are recommended:

Consider observability as early as possible as part of your design and implementation.

Make observability configuration and infrastructure part of your application source code.

Run jobs in your application deployment pipeline that will take care of observability.

Favour the use of declarative observability code, meaning using specific configuration and files isolated from your enterprise code.

Use programmatic observability only if necessary, keep your enterprise code clean and focused on the business.

Add small and incremental changes, if you plan to migrate, continuously improve the Observability stack to meet your application demands.

See Why Developers Need Observability-as-Code for more guidance.

Some of the recommended tools that can help achieve Observability-as-Code are: Terraform, Cloudformation, YML, Python, shell scripts.

?> NOTE: You may already apply the Observability-Driven-Development principles, making you a pioneer ready to run a workshop about your excellent team way of working.

Code observability

Code observability is not Observability-as-Code. Code Observability is the practice of getting observable insights from your code, and in case of an error, ideally showing the offending code statement that produced the error, or that changed. This means that source code and commit history of your

history of your application have to be instrumented and exposed to the observability tool. It also means that is important for this practice to instrument the deployment pipeline, so the analyzing tool can understand what changed.

Error monitoring is a monitoring practice that - if used correctly - opens the path to code observability. Sentry and New Relic error inbox can help you achieve that.

Observability Mix'n'Match

Observability Mix'n'match is about seizing the opportunity of having many sophisticated observability tools that Ocado Technology provides. It is the Observability team's job to provide you with clarity on how to use the available options.

As per CNCF "organisations will often mix and match different observability tools to meet very specific needs of their applications and infrastructure. The Cloud Native Computing Foundation‘s End User Technology Radar on Observability released in September 2020 reported that half of the companies
of the companies surveyed used at least five observability tools and a third of the respondents used more than 10 observability tools.

Observability is an ever-growing pioneering domain that recently made the leap from monitoring or logging practices to a more elaborated practice suited for a rather complex microservice architecture. Many of the tools that are in place use techniques to keep the market steady, like the use of

like the use of specific agents that provide out of the box functionality, that might be completely enough for many use cases, but not all of them. And the problem is that you usually don't know when you don't observe.
End-to-end observability

End-to-end observability offers interconnected and well-balanced value stream of observability for your system - from code to the unfortunate incident that needs investigation and back. There is a lot in between, like testing environments, failed deployments, repetitive alerts that are snoozed, too
are snoozed, too many debug logs in development environments or too many traces in production.

To achieve end-to-end observability, use Micrometer Library and OpenTelemetry Library. Both provide metrics and traces capabilities, and are already available in beta mode.

Observability insights

As per this very good source, "observing microservices and serverless applications becomes very difficult at scale: the volume of raw telemetry data can increase exponentially with the number of deployed services. Traditional log aggregation becomes costly, time-series metrics can reveal a swarm of

reveal a swarm of symptoms but not the interactions that caused them (due to cardinality limitations), and tracing every transaction can introduce both application overhead and prohibitive cost in data centralization and storage. A strategic approach to observability data ingestion is required."

Treat observability data as data. If it's too much, it is not good. Is it's too less, it is not good. Make sure your data-centric approach in developing your app remains when working with observability. Beware of high cardinality, too many logs, too many samples, but make sure you have enough to
you have enough to know your systems.

NOTE See the Take Control of Your Observability Data Before It Controls You article for a more meaningful insight about how to keep observability data at scale.

Observability pipelines

As defined by Clint Sharp in What is an Observability Pipeline?, an observability pipeline is "a stream processing engine that can unify observability data processing across all types of observability (metrics, events, logs, and traces), collect all the data required, enrich it, eliminate noise and

eliminate noise and waste, and deliver that data to any tool in the organization designed to work with observability data." The author mentioned that the pipeline should have the following attributes:

Schema-agnostic processing: enrich, aggregate, sample, suppress, or drop fields from any shape and only keep agreed schema data.

Adapter: normalise, denormalize, and adapt data for any of the backends is supposed to go to the backend tools.

Protocols: work with existing delivery mechanisms provided by tools or libraries or agents.

Configurability: fast reconfiguration of the data that will be sent to collectors.

Transport: at least once delivery semantics to ensure data arrives in the right place.
Sampling: build everything with sampling in mind.

Agile and lean

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #catalyst-sig.

At Ocado Technology, our approach to software development is to break down work into manageable chunks and use collaboration, iterative processes, and incremental delivery to deliver value. We reflect upon our working practices and review our data, encouraging continuous improvement, and validating
and validating that what we build is valuable.

Our approach

While this page focuses on software development practices, we are also successfully adopting and adapting agile and lean practices in areas of hardware development.

At Ocado Technology, we value agile and lean approaches for delivering software, even though we do not adhere to a specific agile framework or methodology. We understand that faithfully following a framework in complex environments with emerging properties is an anti-pattern.

Why agile and lean?

The Agile Manifesto represented a paradigm shift in software development over 20 years ago, and now we recognise that the context has changed. The challenges of delivering meaningful and valuable change remain, but the pace of change has accelerated, technology has become ubiquitous, and the world
and the world has grown increasingly complex.

So, why do we engage with agile and lean concepts today? While some articles claim that the agile movement is dead, others argue it remains as relevant as it was two decades ago. While the principles and values of the Agile Manifesto reflect the challenges and technologies of the time, the
of the time, the associated frameworks and practices provide us with tools and techniques that remain useful today.
Fundamentals

At Ocado Technology, we adopt a lean and agile approach to deliver value early and often. This approach encourages us to validate value and discover quality through rapid feedback, ensuring we are building the right thing, and building it right. It also helps us to actively manage the flow of work
the flow of work to achieve that early and frequent delivery.

Agile methods emphasise collaboration, iterative processes, incremental delivery, reflection, and continuous improvement. Complex challenges are broken down into smaller parts, facilitating iterative processes alongside an incremental build approach. This enables fast feedback and the frequent

and the frequent delivery of valuable things. The team reflects on its practices and delivery capabilities and looks to validate the value of what was built. Insights gained from these reflections are used to adapt and enhance our working practices. We amplify what works, stop what doesn’t, and do
doesn’t, and do not subscribe to agile dogma. This aligns with our core values; Build Trust, Collective Potential, and Learn Fast.

Lean, as inspired by the Toyota Production System, also influences our processes and practices. Understanding how work flows through various teams, functions, and crafts within the organisation, along with identifying bottlenecks and inefficiencies, allows us to identify opportunities for

opportunities for improvement and optimise our delivery capabilities. The lean approach empowers individuals to manage their work and to take accountability for improvement, aligning with our core values of Aligned Autonomy, Collective Potential, Craft Smart, and Build Trust.
Beyond teams

A team’s delivery capabilities are influenced by the environment in which it operates. To fully leverage lean and agile practices, we consider how work flows across the organisation, and look to optimise interactions and processes beyond individual teams. Applying lean and agile principles and
principles and practices at the departmental, stream, and organisational levels creates further opportunities for improvement.

Emergency changes

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #maturity-model-and-sdlc-sig.

An emergency change is a change that needs to be made immediately to mitigate a production incident.

!> You can follow the emergency change process only if there is a production incident logged with Service Desk. Otherwise, follow the full SDLC change process. See the Incident Response site for instructions on how to raise an incident. Always prefer to roll back a change rather than try to fix
than try to fix forward. Only make an emergency change if rollback is not an option.
This list describes the subset of SDLC activities that should be performed when making an emergency change:

C4 - Decide how changes will be tested Take a risk-based approach to testing emergency changes. If a change feels high risk, spend longer checking and testing it before deployment. There is no need to create a test plan.

C6 - Write the code, along with automated tests Aim to reproduce the issue through failing unit tests before making the code change. Manual testing will be more appropriate for many emergency changes than additional higher level automated tests (such as component or system tests). These can be
These can be added when the production incident has been mitigated.
C8 - Reference a ticket ID in every Production commit message You must still reference a ticket ID in every commit message. You may reference the production incident ticket ID.
C9 - Ensure all changes are reviewed before deployment to Prod During office hours ask a colleague to peer review any change before it is deployed.

Out of hours you may opt to skip the pre-deployment peer review, and have a change reviewed retrospectively instead. Take a risk-based approach to skipping pre-deployment peer review. If you have any doubts about a change, invite a colleague to join the incident to perform a review. Retrospective

Retrospective reviews should take place on the next working day. * C10 - Perform a security code review Take a moment to consider whether an emergency change might affect the security of the system. Any decision to temporarily reduce system security to mitigate the incident should be taken at an

be taken at an appropriate level of management, dependent on the level of risk being introduced. Involve your line manager in any such discussion. * C11 - Update support documentation Ensure your team members are aware of any emergency change that is applied to the system. If applicable, update the

update the support documentation once the incident is mitigated (e.g. to capture a temporary workaround that has been implemented, or to describe how to mitigate the problem should it occur again before the root cause is fixed).

Like any other change, emergency changes should always be built on an approved build server B1, have artifacts stored in an approved artifact repository B4, and be deployed via automation DE4 using an approved deployment tool DE3.

To mitigate the incident in a reasonable timeframe, it may be necessary to skip some long-running automated tests, reduce the bake times in your Wave & Bake deployment pipeline, and/or not to deploy changes to all DTAP environments. A defective fix rushed into production often extends the duration
the duration of a production incident, so consider the risk/reward balance before skipping tests or DTAP environments.
The Incident Response and Problem Management processes O5 should be followed for all Production Incidents.

Being on call

If you have any questions related to this section, ask them in the #on-call-sig channel, or speak with one of your line managers.

What does "on call" mean?

Being on call means you can be contacted to investigate, mitigate, or fix the issues reported for the systems you're responsible for. On-call responsibilities extend beyond office hours. Some teams have a separate office-hours rota and out-of-hours rota. If you are on call out of hours you are

of hours you are expected to respond to issues over the weekends and at night. On-call support is critical to the availability and correctness of our systems. Ocado Technology prides itself on providing great customer service and there are SLAs established for many systems.

On-call support is a part of your employment contract with Ocado and you will be paid for being on call. It's your responsibility: * to know when you're on call, * to make sure you can be contacted when on call, * to make sure you can think and act clearly when called.

On-call principles

Never hesitate to escalate. Never feel you need to resolve an incident on your own, and never let pride or your ego stop you from asking for assistance. Both your line manager and the rest of your team should be available to assist you on a best-efforts basis. Your line manager should brief you on

should brief you on the escalation process your team uses before you join the on-call rota. If input is required from another team, bring them into the incident. Be aware that the Service Desk may also escalate an issue based on its impact and urgency. This is not to show a lack of confidence in
of confidence in you but to make sure you get the support you need in an emergency.

No blame culture. There will be incidents. Some will be caused by you, some will be caused by others. Some will just happen. Our incident response process is blameless. Focus on mitigating the incident instead.

On-call response times

The people on call offer the first line of support for multiple business-critical operations. Incidents can have a significant impact on Ocado, our partners, and their shoppers. Many need to be resolved quickly. There are two on-call response timeframes that you are expected to adhere to:

Initial response time: When you're first contacted you must respond (that is, acknowledge the PagerDuty alert or answer the phone) within this period.

Log-on response time: Within this period you must log into your machine and be able to provide practical assistance.

Target Worst Case Initial response time Immediate 15 mins Log-on response time 15 mins** 45 mins

**If you can log on immediately, you should.

You can't acknowledge and investigate alerts when commuting. Unless you have a very short commute, you may need to set up an escalation in PagerDuty to escalate any alerts that occur while you're travelling. See the PagerDuty configuration section for advice on how to configure escalations.

Incident priorities

All incidents reported to the Service Assurance team are triaged to determine their priority. For more information on incident priority, see the Incident Prioritisation Guru card.

Preparing to be on call

Make sure you're familiar with the Incident Response process.

Follow the steps below to ensure you are prepared for being on-call:

Make sure you know when you're on call. Consider setting reminders.

Always have your phone, your laptop, and a known reliable internet connection nearby when on call. Have access to a quiet location, too.

Make sure you can:

Make sure you can:

Stay connected for a long time if required.

Recharge your phone or laptop.

If you rely on mobile data, make sure you won't hit data limits.

Make sure your laptop and your local development environment are configured and functional.

If you can't meet the above criteria for a period, you should arrange for a substitute in advance.

It's your responsibility to arrange an on-call swap (or alternative rota substitution) for any on-call duty periods that occur while you're on an annual leave or otherwise unavailable.

During an incident

Roles

There are several roles involved in incident response, as seen below.

Incident roles

A brief summary of each role is:

Incident Manager - Coordinates major incidents and drives them to resolution.

Service Assurance Specialist - Responsible for interacting with partners, either by phone, or via Jira Service Desk tickets updates.

Subject Matter Expert - Knows the most about the system, responsible for diagnosing the problem and finding a solution.

Head of Engineering (Optional) - Can apply higher level subject matter expertise, provide additional information on business impact, and help make calls on risk.

General incident management protocols
Depending on the priority and complexity of the incident, the Incident Manager may choose to coordinate the incident on Slack or ask everyone involved to join a Google Meet.
If the issue is primarily in your area and senior area escalation has not been made, then you should expect to be the lead contact for the incident.

Every incident has a dedicated Slack channel that you must join. If there is no requirement for a Google Meet when the problem is escalated to you, you will be able to analyze the problem independently. You should update the Service Desk about your progress on Slack at least every 20 minutes

every 20 minutes (unless an alternative period is agreed with the Incident Manager) for non-trivial issues. Depending on the issue, the Service Desk may then start a Google Meet unless you believe you have fixed (or are about to fix) the issue.
When asked a question that requires investigation or analysis to answer, let the requester know that you will need time and try to approximate how long it will take to provide an answer.

If you are asked a question while you are already concentrating on another aspect of the problem, you should clarify which action you should prioritise.

Before taking any action, let the call participants know, so that others have a chance to give their input.

If you need help, ask the Incident Manager for additional escalation. It's better to get help rather than to try something you are not confident about and inadvertently make the issue worse.

Google Meet incident protocols
When on a Google Meet, join the appropriate Slack channel as well to ensure everyone is kept up-to-date and can share information easily.

If you cannot join both the Google Meet and the Slack channel then you should not actively participate in the incident response, to avoid disjointed communication. Liaise with someone who is both in Slack and on the Meet, and provide them with any input you may have, so that they can raise it.

Ask for more information from the client if you need it.

Provide a summary of what has been done so far if others join the Google Meet and need to be updated.

Be attentive during the Google Meet and on Slack channel. Respond to any questions directed to you.
Meets can be noisy and busy. If you're in a noisy environment or talking to someone off the call, please mute your phone. When you have something to add, speak clearly and concisely.
Once in a Google Meet, you should only leave after you let others on the call know you're leaving and they acknowledge that.

Be aware of what others in the Google Meet are discussing. If you have something to add, wait for an opportunity. Only interrupt if you have something urgent to say. However, if you believe it to be urgent or pertinent to the discussion, do interrupt.

Use a Slack channel or a private group to discuss investigations among peers. If your area has a senior escalation on the call, they can coordinate with the group so that you can continue investigating.

If you don’t think you're required any longer, ask others in the Google Meet if they still need your support.

After the call

After the call

If appropriate, share a summary of the incident with your team. Note the hours that you worked so that you can submit a timesheet. Your line manager will be able to provide information about on-call compensation rates.

When your on-call period ends

At the end of your on-call period you should provide a quick summary to the next on-call person about any issues that may come up during their shift.

On-call rota size

There should be least six people on rotation in each on-call rota whenever possible.

On-call etiquette

On-call etiquette

Always consider covering someone else's on-call time if they request it, and you are able. We all have lives that may get in the way of the on-call time and one day it may be you who needs to swap their on-call time.

If you're making a change that impacts the recurring on-call schedule (adding or removing yourself, for example), let others know since many people make arrangements around the on-call schedule well in advance.

If you're performing an action that will cause a page, consider whether it's appropriate to set a maintenance window in PagerDuty. Alternatively, you may want to arrange to "take the pager" for the time during this period.
Be considerate if someone has been on call all night or has been called multiple nights in a row. Consider whether you can help them by offering to cover some of their upcoming on-call time.

Hallmarks

If you have a question related to this section, ask on #hallmarks-sig.

A Hallmark is an indication that a product meets a certain standard. We want to ensure our applications meet a certain standard, so this document outlines our “Hallmark requirements” for them.

?> NOTE: This page uses rfc2119 MUST/SHOULD style terminology.

New applications SHOULD NOT implement all Hallmarks from scratch. They should use one of the Hallmark helper libraries. You MUST implement the Hallmarks before you deploy your application to Production.

Hallmark versions and change log

Expand the section below to see a summary of the Hallmarks changelog. Full details of any change can be found in the Handbook's git repository history.

Hallmark requirements

A: Tech stack

A1. Applications MUST use approved technology stacks.

A2. Where there is a choice between a semi-managed service and a managed service, apps SHOULD use the managed service unless there is a good reason to use the semi-managed service. See Fully-managed vs semi-managed cloud services for definitions of these terms.

A3. We MUST NOT deploy services into the cloud on EC2/GCE IaaS if the cloud provider provides a similar service. For example, don’t deploy PostgreSQL on Amazon EC2, use Amazon’s RDS PostgreSQL service instead.

B: Data sources and data exchange

B1. All data exchange MUST be via JSON unless there is a good reason to use another format (e.g. you need an XML Schema for validation), or you need better performance or smaller data volumes than JSON can offer.

B2. Applications SHALL NOT access other application’s data except through a web service or messaging interface.

B3. APIs MUST follow Ocado Technology’s RESTful API conventions.
B4. Applications MUST NOT have dependencies on the Ocado corporate network, either at startup or at run time (aside from for applications that must integrate with Ocado Retail Limited).

B5. Applications SHOULD source data from AWS, and process data in AWS, unless this would result in either: - storing or processing truly 'big data' in AWS - an AWS service storing long term historic data which it would otherwise not need to store

C: Resilience

C: Resilience

C1. Application design MUST tolerate server instances failing regularly (i.e. daily).

C2. All web service API calls MUST be coded to expect and handle failures (e.g. retry multiple times with idempotency), and should fail gracefully if a dependent service is unavailable, or if a sequence of calls is performed in an unexpected order.

C3. Microservices architectures are extremely prone to cascading failures. Every synchronous HTTPS API call MUST therefore go through a circuit breaker library that conforms to these circuit breaker requirements, such as Resilience4j.

C4. Microservices architectures can result in API calls through many different services. To facilitate searching across systems for all log entries relating to a particular call, each service MUST support request tracing as specified in the Distributed tracing section. This is mandatory for web
mandatory for web service calls, and optional when sending messages over SNS/ SQS.
C5. This Hallmark has intentionally been removed. Data store backups are now mangaged centrally as part of the Ocado Technology Platform.
C6. The cloud apps and the associated services that process data synchronously MUST tolerate availability zone outages. These apps must run in at least two availability zones.
C7. Developers MUST NOT use cloud provider features that have not been fully released (e.g. those in the “preview” or “experimental” phase) without prior agreement from your Head of Engineering.

C8. For systems with sufficiently variable load, you MUST consider whether to implement auto scaling to optimize cost.

D: Security

D1. All web services and web applications MUST serve requests using HTTPS only.

D2. Measures MUST be in place to prevent unauthorised updates to production data stores.

D3. Inter-system authentication MUST be via OAuth2, using Panda for OSP apps, and Azure AD for everything else.

D4. Apps SHOULD use a security framework that allows you to change the underlying authentication mechanism without changing your application code, e.g. Spring Security.

D5. End-user authentication and authorisation of Ocado Group employees MUST use Azure AD.

D6. Apps MUST be deployed using approved deployment tooling, which implements access and audit controls.

D7. Measures MUST be in place to prevent unauthorised access to sensitive data.

D8. External resources (storage buckets, queues etc) MUST be locked down so only the appropriate applications can access them.

D9. Be aware all retailer UIs will be public facing on the internet.
D10. All APIs MUST be written with a level of security such that they could be exposed on the public facing internet.

D11. All passwords or tokens used to authenticate to other services MUST be stored in encrypted form in DTAP environments. If running on AWS, use AWS KMS to store the encryption keys. Note that OTP encrypts application buckets using SSE-S3 by default. See Storing application secrets for additional
for additional information relating to application secrets).

D12. Access to all web APIs, all web UIs, and all provisioned resources (including, but not limited to, SQS queues) MUST be configured according to the principle of least privilege. Each calling application or user should have only the minimum set of read/write/update/delete privileges they need on

they need on each web endpoint or resource. To prevent unauthorised access, all endpoints and resources SHOULD default to ‘Access denied’ until an Access Control List (ACL) rule is applied to that resource in code.

D13. All user interfaces MUST implement single sign out and session expiration.

D14. Applications MUST protect session IDs as they can be used to log in as another user. Session IDs MUST be masked before being sent to logs or other monitoring tools.

D15. Panda access_tokens MUST NOT be passed down the call chain between systems. Each call in the chain MUST authenticate separately. If information about the original caller is required by a downstream system (for example, for audit purposes), it MUST be passed by other means, such as in the
such as in the request body or headers. See OSP ADR 87 for more details.

E: Personal Data

E1. Access to personal data MUST be restricted to a needs-must basis.

E2. Applications MUST NOT log direct personal data in log files (unless exempted, note that an exception has been granted for Ocado employee username logged for audit purposes).

E3. All persisted direct personal data MUST be encrypted using the processes and techniques described in the Encryption of personal data standards before ingesting into Data Intelligence Platform (unless exempted).

E4. Applications MUST NOT persist direct personal data unless they are the ultimate source of that data.

F: Internationalisation

F1. All datetimes MUST be sent, received and stored in UTC (non-UTC times should be rejected) in the ISO8601 format. Times should only be converted to non-UTC for display purposes or adding/subtracting a relative amount of time (like days, months). Remember to take daylight saving time into
saving time into account.

F2. Applications MUST support multiple languages and MUST provide tests to demonstrate this, including correctly accepting and displaying Unicode characters (also Unicode supplementary characters). [Handling supplementary characters correctly in Java]

correctly in Java] http://www.oracle.com/us/technologies/java/supplementary-142654.html) can be complex if your code manipulates individual characters. Your app MAY need to support right-to-left text direction languages. To learn if you need to implement this, consult the relevant product manager.

F3. UTF-8 character encoding MUST be used.

F4. Multiple currencies MUST be supported. Display currencies as they are most commonly displayed in the locale (e.g. “£1.23”, not “1.23 GBP”).

F5. This Hallmark has been intentionally removed.

F6. Retailer apps MUST display the user’s preferred language.

F7. Applications MUST NOT implement ‘default’ or ‘fallback’ config for missing languages or locales - see S-SC3
?> NOTE: Also see Hallmark S1 (Retailer and banner separation Hallmarks) for details of how to implement retailer-specific/banner-specific display strings.

F8. System internals MUST process quantities using metric units of measure. APIs and data feeds MUST use metric units only. Metres, kilograms, degree Celsius, and seconds SHOULD be the preferred units where suitable. Conversion for the end users for locale purposes (UI input or output) should

or output) should happen at the point of input/output. An exception can be made for display-only strings that contain quantities and units (e.g. "price per 100 fl. oz.") - these do not need to be converted to metric.

G: Monitoring and alerting

G1. G2. G3. These Hallmarks have intentionally been removed.

G4. All systems MUST be registered in the OTP Registry, including a link to support docs.
G5. Every app MUST respond on URL “/health-check” indicating its state. More details in the Health Check Standardisation doc.

G6. Every app SHOULD: * implement heartbeat monitoring that would periodically check the application is up and running. * implement standard alerts and dashboards such as golden signals alerting (RED method: request rate, error rate, duration), so that early degradation symptoms of an application
of an application are easily spotted. * configure and use monitoring as code, and alerting as code.
H: Data and analytics

H1. Apps SHOULD raise data lake events (through Kinesis) for the actions and the associated data relevant to business objectives, when the data is required now or will be in the future (for reporting, analysis, or improving service and application functionality). Events SHOULD provide a data-level

a data-level view of the product. Such a view SHOULD remain up-to-date by continuously aligning it with application logic. All the events MUST comply with the Data Governance Naming Standards & Principles.

H2. Business or end-users MUST NOT have direct access to operational data stores.

H3. Data MUST NOT be retained for longer than allowed by any applicable data retention policy. Data Producers MUST ensure their data is tagged / categorised to facilitate data retention processing.

H4. All the BigQuery objects (datasets, tables, and views) MUST have a Data Owner and a Data Steward assigned. To learn how to do that, read the Sherlock guide.

I: Logging

I1. It SHOULD NOT be necessary to log into individual servers to read logs. Application logs and web server access logs should be sent to the appropriate Logstash instance where they will be indexed by OpenSearch, and can be queried via OpenSearch Dashboard. (If you’re running on Google Cloud, use
Google Cloud, use Google logging instead of OpenSearch).

I2. The Opensearch best practices documentation details standard fields that all apps MUST log to Logstash. Apps SHOULD use FileBeats to send logs to Logstash.

I3. This Hallmark has intentionally been removed.

I4. Production apps MUST log only to centrally managed logstash servers so that access to logs is managed through centralised access control.

I5. All applications used by end-users MUST keep an audit log (via Kinesis events) of all changes made through the application including:

Enough details of the change to constitute an audit trail

Who made the change

When the change was made

J: Multi-tenancy

The Hallmarks in this section apply to multi-tenant systems. They ensure the separation of retailers, banners, operations, and sites within these systems.

!> IMPORTANT: This Hallmark section contains platform-wide multi-tenancy Hallmarks, and it is being added to incrementally as more platform-wide standards are agreed. OSP streams with multi-tenanted systems also have their own multi-tenancy standards (e.g. Ecom multi-tenancy standards).
standards). Multi-tenant systems MUST implement both local stream standards, and the Hallmarks in this section.

J1. Any app persisting to DynamoDB data belonging to a retailer, banner, operation, or site entity MUST ensure:

Each tenant's data is stored in separate items

Every primary key partition key, and every global secondary index (GSI) partition key, has a prefix of the following format:

<TenantType>Id#<TenantId>#...

The <attributeType>Id#<attributeId> pattern MAY be continued for the remainder of the partition key.

J2. Any app persisting to Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL data belonging to a retailer, banner, operation, or site MUST ensure:

Each tenant's data is stored in separate rows

Each table holding tenant-specific data has a column that identifies the tenant that each row belongs to, e.g. a retailerId, banner_id, OperationId, or siteId column
PostgreSQL Row Level Security is used to enforce isolation of all tenant-specific data.

All SQL queries MUST also be written as if there is no Row Level Security (RLS) enabled. This will ensure tenant isolation is maintained even if RLS is misconfigured. In practise this requirement will most often be achieved by including a clause equivalent to WHERE banner_id=aaaa-bbbb-cccc in all
in all queries that involve tenant-specific data.

J3. Any app storing data in a cache that belongs to a retailer, banner, operation, or site entity MUST ensure each tenant's data is stored in separate cache entries. This Hallmark applies to both external caches (e.g. Amazon ElastiCache) and in-memory caches. In most cases you SHOULD prefix the

SHOULD prefix the cache key with the TenantId, however you MAY instead use a separate cache for each tenant. You MUST NOT implement a getAll() or equivalent method that returns data belonging to multiple tenants – cache retrieval calls MUST only fetch data for a single specified tenant. [This
tenant. [This Hallmark was created as a result of OSP Architecture ADR-76.]

K: Access to servers and production data

K1. You MUST be able to support your app without SSH'ing into the server or container environment that is hosting the application.

K2. Dev teams MUST NOT have any access to Production data by default. If access is required (read or write) it MUST only be granted for a limited time (hours) via a Change Request. Apps MAY have diagnostics and administration screens / APIs built in to help diagnose potential issues.

K3. You MUST NOT copy retailer data out of retailer production environments into non-production environments.

L: Deployment

L1. There should be no interruption of service during a deployment. Apps running on ECS SHOULD be deployed using a rolling deployment strategy.

M: User interface design and branding

M1. ONE UI design system and one-ui-react JS lib SHOULD be used for all user interfaces (excluding systems exposed publicly to shoppers).

M2. Internal web user interfaces that may be used by end-users who are not at their desk SHOULD provide a mobile-friendly interface.

M3. Where a mobile interface is to be provided it SHOULD be coded first (i.e. a “mobile first” approach).

M4. Web apps that may be used by a third-party on their own hardware MUST be tested on the following browsers:

Chromium - latest version

Android Chrome, if app needs to work on Android mobile device

iOS Safari, if the app needs to work on an Apple mobile device

M5. Web apps that will only be used within Ocado Head Office MUST support:

Chrome - latest version

iOS Safari, if the app needs to work on a mobile device

M6. Web apps that will be used in a CFC MUST support Chromium.

N: Static analysis and code coverage

N1. When starting a new codebase teams SHOULD configure static analysers like PMD, SpotBugs, Checkstyle, ESLint. Also configure tools calculating test-related metrics or heuristics, e.g. JaCoCo (for test coverage) or PIT (for mutation testing). Have builds fail if the analysis does not meet a
does not meet a configured target.

O: Artificial intelligence

O1. All apps and services that incorporate AI (as defined in Ocado AI guidance) MUST be registered in the AI and robotics book of work before being deployed to any environment. This helps us meet our obligations under the EU AI Act.

P: Documentation

P: Documentation

P1. All APIs (including internal ones) SHOULD be documented, and the documentation should be published to rAPIer.

P2. Application documentation SHOULD meet our escrow requirements.

Q: New Retailer or Fulfilment operation setup

Q1. Setup procedures for new retailers SHOULD NOT involve manually entering data into Production data stores.

R: Identifiers

R: Identifiers

R1. Domain entities (e.g. retailers / recipes / delivery routes) MUST have a version 4 UUID as their primary identifier. If a human-readable identifier is required this should be added as an attribute, rather than being the primary ID.

R2. Retailers (or other third-parties) MUST NOT specify the primary ID for an entity that is persisted within our systems. If both OSP and the retailer have an ID for an entity, then the Atmosphere ID MUST be the primary ID (a UUID), and the retailer ID MUST be treated as just another attribute of
attribute of the entity, albeit one that has special meaning for the retailer.

R3. Applications MUST NOT encode systemic information in identifiers - it MUST NOT be possible to parse identifiers to extract meaningful information.

S: Retailer and Banner separation

!> IMPORTANT: This Hallmark section does not apply to multi-tenanted apps.

The Hallmarks below are controls to ensure the separation of banners and retailers within OSP.

Retailer runtime environments Hallmarks

S-RE1. Retailer apps running in Amazon Web Services (AWS) MUST run in one of the retailer's dedicated AWS accounts.

S-RE2. Retailer apps running in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) MUST have their own retailer-specific GCP projects.

Retailer and Banner Specific Configuration (RSC/BSC) Hallmarks

All app types except mobile native apps:

S-SC1. All apps MUST have: - An application code artifact that is the same for every retailer - All RSC config within a retailer-specific RSC artifact, i.e. one RSC artifact per retailer. (If the app has no RSC then the RSC artifact can be omitted.) The application artifact and RSC artifact can
RSC artifact can optionally be brought together to form a single retailer-specific deployable artifact. It is not mandated how the source code for these artifacts is managed in git.
S-SC2. Retailer A's config MUST NOT be present in Retailer B's runtime environment. This includes Abundo and Fraisey RSC/BSC - these MUST NOT be present in other retailer's runtime environments.

S-SC3. Applications MUST NOT implement 'default' or 'fallback' config for missing: - Branding - Theming - Display strings - Languages - Locales - Time zone - Currency

All of these values MUST be explicitly provided by RSC / BSC.

If required config is not found: - the app MUST either: 1. refuse to start up 2. return / display an error 3. display nothing, or display a placeholder for the missing config (e.g. [login_button_text]) - the app MUST NOT fallback to using a default retailer or banner - the app MUST NOT display the
NOT display the text using a default language or locale - the app SHOULD raise an alert

S-SC4. RSC and the runtime environment MUST be cross-checked during app start-up and (for apps that support dynamically updating config, while the app is running) whenever the app detects a change in config: - Each RSC deployable artifact MUST explicitly contain the retailer ID, i.e. have a

ID, i.e. have a retailerID property, or include the retailer ID in the filename - Each runtime environment MUST make available to applications the ID of the retailer it is serving - Each app MUST check that the retailer ID in the RSC matches the retailer ID exposed by the runtime environment 1. at

environment 1. at start-up and 2. if config is updated dynamically at runtime - If they do not match, or either ID cannot be ascertained, then the app: 1. MUST NOT start up, or 2. MUST NOT accept the dynamic config update and SHOULD raise an alert

?> Remember, all changes (including config changes) MUST be code-reviewed before deployment to Production.

Mobile native apps

S-MN1. If a native app is built for a retailer or banner(s), the RSC / BSC MAY be built into the native app. The native app MUST NOT contain RSC / BSC for other retailers or banners, i.e. if RSC / BSC is built into the app there MUST be a specific build of the app for the retailer or banner(s).

Config implementation

Use the following techniques to reduce the amount of RSC - Use relative URLs that will work in all environments - Use the environment to determine the retailer the application is serving

Testing

Testing

Add tests to ensure your apps and config meet the Hallmarks above. Add tests to ensure a retailer config contains no references to other retailers, e.g. the word 'Morrisons' doesn't appear in any Casino config files.

Multi-banner API and UI Hallmarks

S-AU1. All code that stores or retrieves data by banner MUST be accompanied by automated tests that ensure the data is correctly stored and retrieved by banner. These tests MUST test the code from API through to the data persistence layer (i.e. be Component Tests, not Unit Tests where the
Tests where the persistence layer is mocked out).

S-AU2. All code that displays content by banner MUST be accompanied by automated tests that ensure the correct banner's data, display strings, and graphical assets (e.g. logos) are displayed by the user interface.

S-AU3. Threat Modelling activities MUST consider the case of a malicious OSP retailer user or customer who tries to intentionally circumvent banner separation.

Justification for each Hallmark
S-RE1, S-RE2 - Running retailer apps in separate AWS account and GCP projects gives the highest level of separation, and thus 'single tenancy'. We have explained to retailers that this is what we do.
S-SC1, S-SC2 - Not having retailer A's config in retailer B's Production environment provides the strongest level of separation possible.

S-SC3 - It is not acceptable to retailers to substitute other branding in place of their own brand. Neither should it be considered acceptable to use the wrong language.

S-SC4 - Checking that the correct config if deployed to the correct environment avoid possible pipeline or deployment errors.

Hallmark helper libraries

New applications SHOULD NOT implement the Hallmarks from scratch. Doing so would be incredibly time-consuming. There are many production-hardened libraries that implement the Hallmarks. Use these instead.

Java Hallmarks helper libraries

Jar-Jar is a set of libraries that help you quickly build ready to deploy applications that implement Ocado Hallmarks, and integrate with the Ocado Technology Platform. It consists of modules that can be selectively plugged into your Spring Boot app. With Jar-Jar you can focus on writing your
on writing your business code, allowing Jar-Jar to handle the rest.

Java is our primary programming language. As a result it has the most mature Hallmarks library by far in Jar-Jar modules.

Python Hallmarks helper libraries

Totoro is a Python library designed to build Hallmark-compliant applications on the Ocado Technology Platform (OTP). It provides ready-to-use building blocks that meet basic Hallmark requirements (like authentication or loggging) to let you focus on the business logic. Python is allowed in multiple
allowed in multiple areas, and Totoro can be useful in all of them, but it is primarily focused on data science, data exploration and machine learning.

PagerDuty configuration

If you have any questions related to this section, ask them in the #pagerduty-sig channel.

If you or your team are just starting to use PagerDuty, this section contains some configuration recommendations.

!> Make sure your rota, contact details, notification, and escalation rules are correctly configured in PagerDuty. Make sure you test these after every change by sending a test alert from PagerDuty. Make sure PagerDuty texts and calls can bypass your "Do Not Disturb" settings.

Who should be on the rota?

New members of the team should shadow your on-call rotation during the first few weeks. They should get all the alerts, and should follow what you do.

Engineering Team Leads can be part of your normal rotation. It gives a better insight into the current situation.

Recommended notification rules

Each user has to set their own notification rules in PagerDuty. These configure how PagerDuty will contact you.

The main objective of PagerDuty is to contact you and allow you to acknowledge all the alerts within 15 minutes. Because of that, it's recommended to have PagerDuty phone you. You may want to start with a push notification or an SMS, but it's easy to miss these notifications. A phone call is more
phone call is more likely to attract your attention. Have PagerDuty try to call you again if you miss the first call.

A recommended set of notification rules might therefore look like this:

Immediately after it's assigned to me, html email me at john.smith@ocado.com (Default)

Immediately after it's assigned to me, push notify me on John's iPhone

1 minute after it's assigned to me, sms me on +44 123456789 (Mobile)

2 minutes after it's assigned to me, phone me on +44 123456789 (Mobile)

5 minutes after it's assigned to me, phone me on +44 123456789 (Mobile)

10 minutes after it's assigned to me, phone me on +44 123456789 (Mobile)

After 15 minutes the alert would then escalate to an escalation schedule.

You may want to add a step to email your team for general awareness too:

1a. Immediately after it's assigned to me, html email me at {$teams-email}@ocado.com (Work)

Recommended escalation policy

It's recommended that you set your escalation timeout to 5-10 minutes. This should be plenty of time for someone to acknowledge the incident if they're able to. If they're not able to within this time, then they're probably not in a good position to respond to the incident anyway.

To meet the requirement of acknowledging all incidents within 15 minutes, it is strongly recommended that your team set up multiple levels of escalation. Suggested escalation policies include:

Person on-call => [10 mins] => Engineering Team Lead => [10 mins] => Everyone in the team

Person on-call => [10 mins] => ETL+EM rota => [10 mins] => HoE

Person on-call => [5 mins] => Secondary schedule => [10 mins] => Engineering Team Lead

Escalation policies should be designed to ensure there is cover while the person on call is on their office commute.

Whatever process you adopt, it is recommended that the vast majority of issues be acknowledged by the primary person on call.

Install the PagerDuty app on your phone

It's recommended to install the PagerDuty mobile app on your phone. It brings a number of benefits:

You can be contacted in areas with poor mobile coverage but good Wi-Fi.

Messages in the app are more informative than a text-to-speech voice call.

The app gives you more context before you can reach your laptop
In "Settings" you can enable "High-Urgency Override Do Not Disturb mode" to make sure PagerDuty can always reach you.
